what I think a lot of people don't understand is that stomach paralysis risk isn't uniform across doses. there's a real difference between a carefully titrated low dose held for weeks before any increase versus someone who jumped to a higher level quickly because they weren't losing fast enough. I don't have a medical degree but the pattern in the case reports seems to skew toward people who were on higher doses for extended periods without the slower titration approach.

I've been on compounded sema through formblends for about six months and the titration protocol my provider uses is genuinely conservative, like slower than I initially wanted. at the time I was impatient. now reading about gastroparesis cases I'm a lot more comfortable with that pace.

the "sister drug" framing CNN uses is interesting too because Ozempic and Wegovy are identical molecules at different approved dose ceilings, so separating their risk profiles is a bit misleading. what actually varies is how high people go and how fast. that's the dosing question that matters here, not which label is on the box.

- How to Keep the Weight Off AFTER OZEMPIC (Avoid the Regain!): Kind: captions Language: en So, you've lost weight with Osmpeic, Mjaro, WGOI, or Zepbound. Maybe you're still taking it. Maybe you're even thinking about stopping. And now the question you're asking yourself is, how do I keep the weight off? Well, the truth is weight loss is only the first part of the journey, keeping it off. That's the part that really matters, and that's where most people struggle. Today, I'm going to walk you through a step-by-step strategy um I've developed over the years to maintain your results after GLP1s so you can avoid the frustrations of the dreaded regain. You can stay healthy and feel confident in your long-term plan. So, let's get into it. So, why does weight gain happen? Let's start with the truth. Weight regain after stopping a GLP1 agonist medication is very common, but it's not inevitable. Why does it happen? OMIC works by mimicking the hormone called glucose-like peptide 1 or GLP1 for short. And this hormone that comes from your small intestine when you eat helps suppress appetite. It regulates blood sugar and slows gastric emptying among many other things. And we're finding out things every single day. When you stop the medication, those effects go away. Suddenly, your hunger returns partly due to a rushing back of another hormone called ghrein. And I call him greedy grein. this guy right here. He causes your cravings to creep back in. And the key to success at this point will will rely heavily on how well you've done with your lifestyle changes during your lifestyle journey. And if you haven't built sustainable habits along the way, it will become very easy to slip back into your old patterns. For so many people, this isn't about willpower. It's about preparation. And that's why this video exists. The first key to maintaining weight loss is a mindset shift. Stop thinking of Ompic as the thing that that made you lose the weight. Start thinking of it as the thing that gave you time and space to build better habits. If you relied 100% on the medication itself and didn't use that window to change your nutrition, your movement, or your routines, it's going to be much harder when it's gone. But if you've used the medication as a bridge or a jump start or a tool, you're already ahead of the game. Now it's time to reinforce those foundations you built. Let's talk about the five things you absolutely need in place if you want to keep the weight off after stopping a GLP1. Habit number one, protein first nutrition. Even when you're off the medication, protein is your best friend. It helps control hunger, stabilizes your blood sugar, and preserve muscle mass, which supports your metabolism in the end. A good rule is to maintain or aim for 30 to 40 grams of protein per meal, especially breakfast and lunch. If you start your day with protein, you're less likely to overeat later on in the day. Protein isn't just about muscle, it's about appetite control. Habit number two, exercise and resistance training. This one's big, and not just big muscles. When you lose weight, you often lose a mix of fat and muscle. And if you don't build muscle back up, your metabolism begins to slow, and that makes regaining weight much easier. Strength training just two to three times a week can protect your results. Use weights, resistant bands, body weight, whatever works for you. And the goal is to maintain lean muscle mass so your body stays efficient at burning calories. Habit number three, daily movement you need to have built in. You don't need to do intense workouts every day, but daily movements is non-negotiable. Walking, biking, stretching, light cardio, these add up. Movement helps regulate insulin, improve mood, reduce cravings, and reinforce your healthy identity. Not to mention the stress component maint and helping you to uh break down and and keep stress under control. A good target is anywhere between 7,000 to 10,000 steps per day. Not because steps are magic, but because movement keeps your body in a fat burning, glucose stabilizing rhythm. Habit number four, balance meals with fiber and fat. Appetite control isn't just about protein. You also need fiber and healthy fats because together they slow digestion and promote fullness. Think about it like this. Vegetables with olive oil, lentils with avocado, berries, and almonds, just to name some of the healthy meals that you can take in. If your meals are mostly carbs, especially fat digesting ones, you're going to feel hungrier sooner. Every plate should be built with fullness in mind. Habit number five, consistency with your sleep and stress. These don't sound exciting, but they matter more than you think. Poor sleep and chronic stress raise cortisol, which increase hunger hormones and leads to emotional eating and disbalance of your metabolism. 7 to 8 hours of sleep, plus intentional stress management is not not nice to have. It's actually required for long-term success. You can try breath work or walks, journaling, faith practices, therapy, whatever works for you. Make it part of your plan. Now, a lot of people ask me, "Doc, when should I stop taking Ozic or any other GLP1 that they're on?" Here's what I tell them. Only stop when you've built at least two to three months of consistent sustainable habits without relying on the medication to control your appetite. If you're still skipping meals, not exercising, or emotionally eating, you're not ready yet. That's not judgment. That's just data. In my experience with you know over a couple hundred few hundred of these consults with these medications. Remember the medication helps you build momentum but your habits are what keep you moving forward. So ultimately it's when you are near or reach your goals and feel you are ready to take on the tasks of lifestyle modifications on your own without the help of the GLP1. So what happens if I regain the weight? Let's be honest. Weight fluctuations in life are normal. Life happens. You might regain a few pounds. That doesn't mean you failed. What matters is your ability to respond effectively. Don't panic. Many times, life happens and a big change causes a change in your routine, which then causes weight to start creeping back up. So, go back to the five pillars we just talked about. Check your sleep, recommmit to strength training, track your food, drink your water, get back to structure in your lifestyle. you don't need to go back on the medications necessarily unless your hunger becomes um completely unmanageable or you're trending upward consistently over several months, let's say. This is a long game. If you've put in the work, trust yourself and give yourself room and grace to course correct. Well, if you've made it this far, you truly care about your long-term health and are looking for solid information from a doctor who cares. That's why I created my free 14-day GLP1 boot camp for you. It's a step-by-step guide that covers everything we just talked about here and more. Details for both are in the links below. Stay healthy, stay informed, and I will see you soon.

- Ozempic/Wegovy and Suicidal Thoughts: Is There a Link?: Kind: captions Language: en-CA In July 2023, the European&nbsp; Medicines Agency initiated&nbsp;&nbsp; an investigation to explore a potential&nbsp; link between GLP-1 receptor agonists, such as Ozempic and Wegovy, and the occurrence&nbsp; of suicidal thoughts and thoughts of self-harm. The probe is expected to reach its&nbsp; conclusion in November of the same year,&nbsp;&nbsp; however this possibility has many&nbsp; people worried, myself included, especially for those who are already taking these&nbsp;&nbsp; drugs and really don’t want to&nbsp; have to wait so long to find out. So I conducted my own small literature&nbsp; review to see what the current evidence&nbsp;&nbsp; says about these types of thoughts&nbsp; and the use of GLP-1 agonists. Firstly, how serious is this probe? The specific&nbsp;&nbsp; drugs under investigation&nbsp; are dulaglutide (Trulicity), exenatide (Byetta and Bydureon),&nbsp; liraglutide (Saxenda and Victoza), lixisenatide (Adlyxin) and semaglutide&nbsp; (Wegovy, Ozempic, or Rybelsus). However, it's not just about these specific&nbsp; medications; it could have broader implications. See, there are many other drugs being developed&nbsp; that also fall into the GLP-1 agonist category. So, the results of this investigation could&nbsp; potentially impact those future medications too. Now, the European Medicines Agency, or&nbsp; EMA for short, is the scientific agency&nbsp;&nbsp; responsible for evaluating and overseeing&nbsp; medicines for both humans and animals. But here's the thing, the EMA doesn't have direct&nbsp; regulatory powers like some other organizations. Instead, it acts more like&nbsp; an advisory committee. Its&nbsp;&nbsp; main focus is on scientific evaluation and&nbsp; providing expert opinions about medicines. So even if the probe reveals some problems and&nbsp;&nbsp; the EMA recommends removing a medication&nbsp; like Ozempic or Wegovy from the market, it doesn't mean they'll be yanked&nbsp; off the shelves right away. The EMA's recommendations are valuable&nbsp; and play a vital role in decision-making,&nbsp;&nbsp; but it's the regulatory bodies, like&nbsp; the FDA, that have the final say. They'll take the EMA's scientific&nbsp; assessments into account,&nbsp;&nbsp; but they're the ones who ultimately&nbsp; decide what to do with the medications. Currently, suicidal thoughts and behaviors are&nbsp; not listed as a side effect on any GLP-1 agonists. Studies done on these drugs that examined&nbsp; the effectiveness and safety of these drugs&nbsp;&nbsp; have not directly found any significant&nbsp; increases in these types of thoughts. However, these studies were not&nbsp; designed to actually examine&nbsp;&nbsp; the link between these types of&nbsp; thoughts and GLP-1 agonist use. Plus, they often excluded patients who&nbsp; were already at risk of depression or&nbsp;&nbsp; experiencing such thoughts and behaviors. We do have one study that specifically&nbsp; looked at a GLP-1 agonist called&nbsp;&nbsp; liraglutide and its potential risk of&nbsp; causing suicidal thoughts and behaviors. The results showed that there was a slightly&nbsp; higher occurrence in those using liraglutide,&nbsp;&nbsp; compared to those using a placebo. About 0.3% of patients experienced&nbsp; them with liraglutide use,&nbsp;&nbsp; while only 0.1% experienced&nbsp; them in the placebo group. Now, this might sound concerning at first,&nbsp;&nbsp; as it appears to be a three-fold&nbsp; increase while using liraglutide. However, when the researchers followed up&nbsp; and assessed patients' mental health using&nbsp;&nbsp; something called the Patient&nbsp; Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9), they found no significant&nbsp; differences between the two groups. As a result, the researchers concluded that&nbsp; there wasn't really any cause for concern. Then there was another cohort study in&nbsp; the UK that also seemed to back this up. They found that GLP-1 agonist use&nbsp; was not associated with an increased&nbsp;&nbsp; or decreased incidence of depression or self-harm when compared to those using another type&nbsp; of medication called the sulfonylureas,&nbsp;&nbsp; another commonly used drug for diabetes. So current studies don’t seem&nbsp; to show any real correlation&nbsp;&nbsp; between these types of thoughts and&nbsp; behaviors and GLP-1 agonist use. But do GLP-1 agonists have any effects&nbsp; on the brain or psychiatric conditions&nbsp;&nbsp; that could impact individuals who struggle&nbsp; with these types of thoughts and behaviors? Actually, there are. However,&nbsp; instead of being problematic,&nbsp;&nbsp; the evidence would suggest that GLP-1&nbsp; agonists may actually be helpful. You see, depression and bipolar are&nbsp;&nbsp; by far the most common psychiatric&nbsp; conditions associated with suicide. Several studies have suggested that&nbsp; GLP-1 agonist medications could have&nbsp;&nbsp; a neuro-protective effect and might even be&nbsp; beneficial for patients dealing with depression. How so? Well, these medications seem to improve&nbsp; cognitive function in individuals with depression. Some studies even suggest that&nbsp; GLP-1 agonists could potentially&nbsp;&nbsp; reduce the risk of anxiety and depression. In fact, they've been considered&nbsp; as possible antidepressants,&nbsp;&nbsp; although not all studies fully support this idea. For people with mood disorders&nbsp; like bipolar disorder,&nbsp;&nbsp; GLP-1 agonists might offer&nbsp; some positive effects too. There's a hypothesis that these medications&nbsp; could improve cognitive function in adults&nbsp;&nbsp; with mood disorders and even become a&nbsp; viable treatment for these conditions. But hold on, it's essential&nbsp; to consider the whole picture. The studies examining these effects often focused&nbsp;&nbsp; on older and weaker GLP-1 agonists&nbsp; like liraglutide or dulaglutide, not necessarily the more recent&nbsp; ones like Ozempic and Wegovy. We need more data on the newer drugs&nbsp; to draw more definitive conclusions. Nevertheless, from my small literature review,&nbsp;&nbsp; I don't see anything that&nbsp; raises a cause for concern. Unlike, for example, the use&nbsp; of GLP-1 agonists and thyroid&nbsp;&nbsp; or pancreatic cancer risks, which I&nbsp; would consider a cause of concern. It makes one wonder, why did the EMA decide&nbsp; to launch this probe in the first place? The review was prompted by reports&nbsp; of suicidal thoughts and self-injury&nbsp;&nbsp; in people using liraglutide&nbsp; and semaglutide medicines, which came to the attention of&nbsp; the Icelandic medicines agency. So far, authorities have collected&nbsp; about 150 reports of possible cases,&nbsp;&nbsp; which is indeed a cause for concern. However, it's crucial to take a step&nbsp; back and consider the bigger picture. Patients dealing with diabetes&nbsp; and obesity may have higher&nbsp;&nbsp; risks of depressive and mood disorders. When it comes to diabetes, multiple&nbsp; studies have consistently shown that&nbsp;&nbsp; there's an increased risk of depression&nbsp; and suicide in patients with the condition, especially among those with more&nbsp; severe diabetes or additional&nbsp;&nbsp; diseases and disabilities related to diabetes. When we talk about obesity, the relationship&nbsp;&nbsp; between it and depressive and mood&nbsp; disorders can be a bit more complex. Some studies have found a positive&nbsp; correlation between obesity and&nbsp;&nbsp; these conditions in both adults and adolescents. However, it's not as clear-cut as we might think&nbsp;&nbsp; because there are also studies that&nbsp; have found the opposite to be true. So we can't definitively say that&nbsp; having diabetes or obesity will&nbsp;&nbsp; directly lead to depression or mood disorders, but research does suggest that&nbsp; individuals with these conditions&nbsp;&nbsp; may be at a higher risk of&nbsp; developing these conditions, which may lead to an increased risk&nbsp; of suicidal thoughts and behaviors. The severity of diabetes or&nbsp; obesity seems to play a role too. Those with more severe forms of these&nbsp; conditions are even more vulnerable. In type 2 diabetes, GLP-1 agonist medications&nbsp; are not typically the first choice for treatment. We have metformin, which is&nbsp; effective, cheaper, and safer,&nbsp;&nbsp; and it's usually the initial&nbsp; drug prescribed for diabetics. GLP-1 agonists come into play&nbsp; as a second-line medication,&nbsp;&nbsp; only if metformin alone doesn't achieve&nbsp; the desired blood sugar control. As a result, patients who end up using GLP-1&nbsp; agonists often have more severe type 2 diabetes,&nbsp;&nbsp; because they require additional&nbsp; medications to manage their condition. The same goes for managing obesity. Current guidelines recommend using GLP-1 agonists&nbsp; for individuals with higher BMIs, greater than 30, or greater than 27 in the presence of other&nbsp; medical conditions like type 2 diabetes,&nbsp;&nbsp; hypertension, dyslipidemia, sleep&nbsp; apnea, or cardiovascular disease. These are typically individuals&nbsp; with more severe forms of obesity,&nbsp;&nbsp; who often have other debilitating issues, and require medical intervention&nbsp; such as GLP-1 agonists. So it might not be the GLP-1&nbsp; agonist medications themselves&nbsp;&nbsp; that cause an increase in depressive&nbsp; disorders and suicidal thoughts. Instead, it could be the fact that these&nbsp; medications are consistently used in&nbsp;&nbsp; populations that are already at higher risk&nbsp; of developing these conditions and thoughts. It may just be a matter of&nbsp; correlation rather than causation. Imagine it like this: If a person with a fever&nbsp;&nbsp; takes a fever-reducing medication&nbsp; and later experiences a headache, we can't simply blame the&nbsp; medication for causing the headache. It might just be that the headache is caused by&nbsp; the underlying illness that causes the fever. In the same way, the higher incidence of&nbsp; depression and mood disorders in those&nbsp;&nbsp; using GLP-1 agonists could be related to&nbsp; the severity of their diabetes or obesity, rather than the medications&nbsp; themselves being the direct cause. So based on the current evidence, I don’t&nbsp; believe there's any indication that these&nbsp;&nbsp; medications have negative&nbsp; effects on mental health. In fact, the evidence points&nbsp; towards potential benefits. GLP-1 agonists seem to have some&nbsp; neuro-protective properties that&nbsp;&nbsp; could be helpful for individuals dealing&nbsp; with depression and mood disorders. Now, it's important to acknowledge the&nbsp; limitations of the current evidence. Most of the studies focus on older GLP-1 agonists,&nbsp;&nbsp; and we still lack enough research and safety&nbsp; information on newer drugs, such as Mounjaro. That's why I'm glad that the EMA has taken the&nbsp;&nbsp; initiative to conduct a probe and&nbsp; review the safety of these drugs. Having an independent and authoritative agency&nbsp; like the EMA hold these medications to scrutiny provides a higher level of accountability than&nbsp;&nbsp; relying only on information from&nbsp; the drug manufacturers themselves. We need this kind of vigilance to&nbsp; ensure the well-being of the public. So if you’re taking a GLP-1 agonist, I don’t&nbsp; see any red flags yet, but you can rest assured, I'll be actively keeping an eye on the&nbsp; latest developments, and I'll be ready&nbsp;&nbsp; to provide you with more information&nbsp; once the EMA's findings are available. Hi, I’m Dr. Brian Yeung. What&nbsp; do you think about the EMA’s&nbsp;&nbsp; probe? Let me know in the comments below. Subscribe and hit the notification bell to stay&nbsp; up to date, and if you found it informative,&nbsp;&nbsp; leave me a like, and share this video&nbsp; with someone you know can use the info.

- Oral Semaglutide and GLP-1 Agonists: When Will They Be Approved?: Kind: captions Language: en-GB Okay, so you've heard a little something about Ozempic, Wegovy and the Gang, a group of utterly revolutionary large molecules that can help aid in really significant weight loss through a series of injections, helping to curb appetite and slow gastric emptying, making you feel fuller for longer. Well, wouldn't it be neat if this came as a tablet? Certainly the big players in the space, Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk think so. And today's question is how far are we from seeing them come to market? to explore this question, we first have to understand the lengthy process by which new drugs are brought to market. It starts by discovering a new molecule. Check. Then you have to produce it in sufficient, amounts and put it into a lab animal to test its safety in non human mammals. The then you give it to a small number of fit and healthy, usually male study volunteers and look for evidence of harm in humans. This is phase one of the three step human trial process. Next, phase two involves you giving it to study participants who have the disease you're aiming to get approval for treating to check its safety in this cohort and phase three then checks for the effectiveness in this cohort. Each stage gets progressively larger (and more expensive), involving initially tens or hundreds, then hundreds and then potentially thousands of participants in a phase three trial. and this is where both Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk are at for their leading candidate molecules for oral GLP1 agonists. That is the final stage before submitting an application for FDA approval. So as mentioned, Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk are the market leaders in this space. Eli Lilly produces Mounjaro and Zepbound and has one drug in phase three trials called and the phase three trials on this molecule began back in June of 2023 with the results coming out from last month and expected to keep rolling in for the rest of the year and the start of next, after which we may see it launched as an oral medication for both diabetes and weight loss if it performs well. Their big competitor in the space Novo Nordisk, which is famous for Ozempic and Wegovy, actually already has an an oral GLP1 agonist medication FDA approved, but only for the management of diabetes. It's called Rybelsus and it comes with a somewhat sinister warning read dose related concerns of causing or worsening thyroid cancers. Given that in injectable form the weight loss drug is usually the same molecule but in a higher dose as the anti diabetic medication drug. This This may end up prohibiting Rybelsus from being used as an oral tablet for weight loss. We shall wait and see. So more on Eli Lilly and what they're doing with their phase three trials looking at Orfoglipron as a daily oral medication for both blood sugar control and body weight control in obesity. These are the results from the only published phase three trial called Achieve 1, linked down below. It's from a company press release posted on their website on 04-17-2025 with a peer reviewed publication stated to be on its way too. The trial involved a 40 week placebo controlled randomised controlled trial of 559 participants across the USA, Japan, China and India split into four evenly sized groups each receiving one of the dosing regimes here in the table. All of the participants had a diagnosis of type 2 diabetes and had taken no anti diabetic medications for 90 days prior to starting and had never been on insulin. A1C or HbA1C is a measure of average blood sugar content over the lifespan of a red blood cell or around the last 90 days. As we can see in that top line the medicine seems to provide a reasonably consistent reduction in blood sugar compared with placebo across the doses trialed. As for weight loss, with the important caveat of not yet knowing how the medication goes in non diabetic patients we can see an increase in effectiveness as doses increase from left to right, culminating in an almost 8% reduction in body weight for trial participants on the 36 milligram daily dose. Now we'd also like to see that broken out into muscle mass versus fat mass in future, but we don't have that information available. If these numbers are replicated in non diabetics it would make 36 milligrams of daily oral Orforglipron, about half as effective as one year of Mounjaro but roughly as effective as one year of Saxenda. Now, what else do we know about the trials? We know that Eli Lilly have over 6,000 people involved in the Achieve Phase 3 trial that is investigating Orfoglipron as a diabetes medicine and state that their intention is to publish the rest of the data in late 25 and early 26. They're also running their phase three trials investigating its efficacy as a weight management drug under the trial name Attain, which has over 3,000 people enrolled and expect to submit data to the FDA and other global regulatory bodies before the end of 2025. Meaning that if the trials continue to come in with positive results and the FDA are happy with what they see, Eli Lilly will likely be bringing an oral weight loss drug to market in around the middle of 2026, bearing in mind the timelines of getting FDA approval of around 6 to 10 months. Next Novo Nordisk. And the first thing to note here is they've been a lot quieter with their press releases of late. Firstly, Rybelsus, it's an oral formulation of Novo Nordisk's semaglutide molecule which has proven itself effective time and time again in injection form under two different doses and brands, Ozempic and Wegovy, for diabetes and weight loss. It's already out there in the US and Europe and works, though the weight loss effects appear to be about half of that of Wegovy. So it is already out there. Clinical studies have found that increasing the dose increased the weight loss achieved, with one 68 week study finding a 50 milligram daily oral dose leading to 15% weight loss. Again, linked down below or a 6 to 7% weight Loss over a year in people with diabetes in the STEP trial. However, in animal studies higher doses seem to correlate with increased risk of thyroid cancers. So time will tell whether a high dose formulation is ever brought out and deemed to be sufficiently safe. we have their candidate drug currently in phase three trials for obesity treatment. Perhaps the most brilliantly and imaginatively named pick of the bunch. NN9932. Basically, there is almost no information online about what it is and I couldn't find a single Novo Nordisk press release on this one. Instead, industry news sources simply state that it's an oral weight loss medication currently going through stage three trials that may be released towards the end of 2025 without any robust reference pending successful trials and approval. But as we enter June and no phase 3 clinical trial results have been published, it seems unlikely this will land before 2026. Next the best of the rest On April 14, 2025, Pfizer announced the termination of the development of an attempt at an oral obesity medication after their molecule named Dannaglipron was found to damage the livers of the participants of a Phase two trial. This is not the first time it's happened, but it means that they are at least several years away from bringing a product to market. On the other hand, industry news reports cite the possibility of a Chinese candidate called HRS 9531, which has published positive phase two trial outcomes back in June 2024. But the problem here is that whoever was reporting this failed to perform their due diligence and this drug is in fact a a subcut injectable that works on both the GLP1 agonist route and the GIP route, like Eli Lilly's powerful injectable Z epbound and therefore not relevant today to today's conversation. And that's it for today's video. Thanks for watching. The top and bottom of it is this Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk are at or around the final hurdle and if they successfully vault it, oral weight loss medications of the GLP1 agonist variety will be with us around early to mid-2026. [singing] “Oh, oh, oh Ozempic.” Or maybe you've seen Ozempic in the news recently for another reason. [overlapping] “Weight loss...” “... weight loss...” “Easy weight loss.” “Miracle Diet Drug.” “Miracle weight loss drug.” And it's true. Ozempic was designed to help people manage their blood sugar... but it's also proven to help people lose weight. My doctor was like “This is mainly for blood sugar management.” “But as a side effects like you will lose weight.” When I started to notice that, like in the first month or so had dropped 10 pounds, like nothing. I was like, “There's something to this.” And once you learn why it works you'll understand how this drug could completely change the way we approach weight loss. According to the latest data from the National Institutes of Health around 42% of adults in the US are currently categorized as obese. The CDC's list of health risks associated with obesity include heart disease, stroke, and type 2 diabetes... diseases that rank among the leading causes of premature death worldwide. Not everyone living with type 2 diabetes is considered obese but the two are closely linked. In fact, one of the leading recommendations for managing type 2 diabetes is weight loss. Even like a small amount of weight reduction for people with diabetes can help blood sugar management. I'm Mila Clarke. I'm 33 years old and I have a type of diabetes called latent autoimmune diabetes in adults. I reached out to Mila because she runs a YouTube channel... Hangry Woman, where she makes videos about living a healthy life with diabetes. The thing that I wish that people understood more is how difficult it is to live with both diabetes and obesity. She's also documented her experience with Ozempic. It has always been really hard for me to lose weight despite diet and exercise and paying attention to lifestyle habits. For a long time, doctors have had mixed views on how to make weight loss more accessible... to help people lower their risk for illnesses like type 2 diabetes. One option is weight loss medication. Historically, weight loss drugs have been associated with dangerous side effects and have been pulled from the market. Metabolic bariatric surgery is extremely effective for weight loss but it requires dramatic lifestyle changes that can be challenging to maintain. But with new diabetes drugs like Ozempic... scientists have a safe and effective tool for weight loss... and it's based on something that's inside our bodies and already: Hormones. Hormones are the body's messengers running from one place to another prompting certain cells into action. When we eat our bodies produce a bunch of hormones to aid in the process of digestion. One of them is GLP-1: the hormone that tells us when we're full. This is a sequence of amino acids that make it up. And this is Semaglutide, the generic name for Ozempic. It's made to mimic GLP-1. Here's why. When food enters your stomach and begins to work its way through the digestive system... your body releases GLP-1 in the intestine. There are receptors for GLP-1 in cells all throughout the body that serve different functions when triggered by the hormone. In the pancreas, GLP-1 receptors promote the production of insulin the hormone that lowers blood sugar levels and suppress the production of glucagon the hormone that raises blood sugar levels. This is important for people with type 2 diabetes whose bodies are insulin resistant and produce very little insulin of their own. Synthetic insulin is a critical treatment for blood sugar management. And semaglutide makes the insulin you do have whether it's produced naturally or synthetically work better in your body. Losing excess fat can also help you become more insulin sensitive. That's where GLP-1’s next receptor comes in. In the stomach, GLP-1 receptors slow gastric emptying... which means food doesn't move through the digestive system as quickly and you stay fuller for longer. It slows the digestion of your food which overall helps with blood sugar spikes and it helps you feel fuller for longer so you don't eat as much. And maybe most importantly, at least in regard to weight loss there are GLP-1 receptors in the brain... particularly in the hypothalamus that suppress hunger cravings. That fuller satiety feeling for longer is really helpful. The digestive system is designed to do all of this naturally but GLP-1 based medications are made to amplify the hormone’s numerous health effects. And it works. A major clinical trial of semaglutide in adults showed an average weight reduction of around 15% when paired with an adjusted diet and exercise routine compared to a placebo group, which demonstrated a little over 2% weight reduction. That level of effectiveness rivals the results of metabolic bariatric surgery. And the fact that I was able to take a once weekly injection and then all of a sudden like the pounds just like dropped off I was like, “This is wild to me.” These drugs aren't without side effects the most common being nausea gastrointestinal effects like diarrhea. Mila initially stopped taking Ozempic because of a side effect that gave her heart palpitations. But she's back on it now. And so far, so good. My blood sugars are great. I'm having an easier time losing weight and I've amped up my exercise and being outdoors and hiking and running and walking. And you still have to do all of the other things that everyone has to do to be able to lose weight. It just makes it a little bit easier. But it's helping me so much with my diabetes management. The number of prescriptions of Semaglutide has risen dramatically since becoming available in late 2017. And in 2021, the FDA approved Semaglutide to be prescribed at a higher dose for weight management specifically branded under the name Wegovy. It was big news and led to this drug getting a lot of attention sort of all at once. The messaging started to center more around weight loss than it did diabetes which is unfortunate for people with diabetes and people living with obesity. There are these two groups of people who really need this drug for health purposes but it's being trivialized and kind of looked at as like “Oh, this is an easy weight loss solution.” There are solutions in place now to deal with the sudden high demand for these drugs for people who need them. Mounjaro, another medication developed specifically for type 2 diabetes is currently being fast tracked for approval by the FDA to be prescribed for weight loss too. Now that Ozempic’s notoriety helps Semaglutide go mainstream it and drugs like it will only become more commonly prescribed for weight management. It's not as simple as “Take this injection and you won't be living with obesity anymore.” or “You'll reverse your diabetes.” But as a community we get really excited to see innovations happen and tools that can make our lives a little easier and that can cause less diabetes burn out... and helps you think about diabetes less. It's one of those things that I think... all of us want to protect at all costs.

glucose into the bloodstream during times between meals or when you haven't eaten for an extended period of time so glucagon causes blood glucose levels to go up but go1 stimulates the pancreas to release insulin while inhibiting the release of glucose gon so therefore gp1 has an overall effect of lowering blood glucose levels after a mill glp1 also slows gastric emptying in gut motility so   1. @MrDavidMoyer [11 likes]: 1:57  See that air bubble in the liquid?  Air in a syringe is bad news.  What priming does is forces all the air out through the needle, and then fills the needle with liquid.  When you're done with your very first dose, you pull the needle out and the pen should instantly seal itself back up "like magic" so there shouldn't be any more air in it again after that.  So you only need to prime each pen once, the first time you use it out of the box.  But if you see an air bubble in the pen when it's time to take a later dose, just prime it again to get the new air bubble out before you inject yourself with it.  You don't want to get crazy with the priming though, because it does waste a small amount of the medication.  So your final dose might be slightly smaller than usual if you prime it too many times.

By the way, she did it WRONG.  You want to expel ALL the air from inside the needle when you prime it, and air always floats on TOP of a liquid.  By holding it sideways, most of the air is pressed against the side of the vial instead of pressed against the back end of the needle, so it won't be pushed out the needle.  The RIGHT way to prime it is:  before you set the dial, hold the pen with the needle facing UP, yeah like point it straight at the sky.  Then GENTLY flick the top of the syringe (the glass/plastic part, not the needle!!) with your finger.  Try to get all the smaller air bubbles to combine into a single bubble if you can.  THEN set the dial to the "- -" primer mark.  THEN, still with the needle facing straight UP, press the button.  Then you should see NOTHING for a split second as the air gets pushed up the needle first.  But at the end you should see a tiny drop of the liquid come out of the needle.  If you don't see the liquid, it means either the needle is defective (clogged) or there was quite a bit of air in the syringe so ONLY air came out and it never reached the liquid yet... just try to prime it one more time.  If you don't see liquid the second time, take that needle off and dispose of it because you're assuming it's a defective needle.  Replace it with another new needle and try priming it ONE MORE TIME.  If it still doesn't work, call your pharmacy or call Novo Nordisk and report the whole thing as defective and they should replace it for you.

DO NOT hold the syringe flat/sideways or needle-down when you're priming it.  It defeats the whole point of priming it.  NEEDLE MUST FACE UP during priming.  Be careful not to accidentally poke yourself or someone else with it.

- Ozempic for weight loss: What you need to know: Kind: captions Language: en I do have friends, family, and even patients ask me all the time about should I consider taking Ozempic, Wegovy, Mounjaro, any of those, you know, numerous drugs that are out there, some aren't approved for weight loss, some are. There's a lot to unpack with who should use it under what circumstances, and what support are they getting when they're using it. So Ozempic is an injectable drug, FDA approved for [type 2] diabetes, but not for weight loss. It basically looks to mimic what a normally occurring hormone that we have in our bodies does, which is to increase insulin levels, which then lowers sugar, decreases appetite, increases how full someone feels after they eat. And that hormone is called GLP-1 or glucagon-like peptide-1. So it's looking to do what the body just can't do anymore in order to prevent the long lasting effects of diabetes. It does. It does work for weight loss. And as with many things in medicine, it was sort of a side effect. Right, a beneficial side effect. The then sort of iteration was, all right, how can we make it work better? And that's how they went from the Ozempic to the Wegovy, which is the one that's approved for weight management. And Wegovy is made by the same manufacturer as Ozempic. It's just a higher dose. The way that it works, the effects on the body are pretty much identical. There are a lot of medications out there for weight management. I think the older medications for weight management really were looking to affect what happens once you eat. And there was orlistat, or Alli, as it was called, which weren't allowing people to absorb a lot. And so, you know, they had these horrible side effects. The newer classes of medications,Ozempic, Wegovy, Mounjaro and similar, they're trying to prevent you from being hungry, or they're trying to make it that when you eat very little, you stay fuller and more satisfied longer. Ozempic is sort of the, you know, the earliest one. Wegovy and Ozempic are the same, and then the newer ones are Mounjaro Zepbound, which is basically the next generation of Manjaro for weight loss, with patients who are not only overweight, but have one of the effects of excess weight. Mounjaro, Zepbound work the same as Ozempic, or Wegovy but they have an additional ingredient, if you will, or a different mechanism. And that's the GiP. So it's glucagon, insulinotropic, polypeptide say that ten times fast. So it's going at the same effect of increasing insulin levels, but through a different mechanism than Wegovy or Ozempic would. There are some guardrails or some guidelines for who qualifies for these medications that are specifically FDA approved for weight loss. So Wegovy and Zepbound, there are criteria. You know, it's. A bmi of 30 is considered obese. But 27 with another medical issue that is directly impacted, attributed to, or worsened by being obese, that would make you a candidate. So we're talking about, in addition to obesity, for example, high blood pressure, cardiovascular disease, diabetes which we know are very, very strongly correlated to being overweight. Yes. You'd have to consult with a healthcare provider, ideally someone who has had training. You know, there's now obesity medicine certification so that have studied the effects of these and really know how to care for a patient in a more holistic way versus, you know, going online and, you know, getting a, you know "consultation with a medical professional." You don't know who's, you know, answering these questions for you. There's side effects. You know, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, abdominal pain are pretty much. And kind of in that order. They're most common. And there are some data out there that are actually showing that it's increasing the incidence or the complexity of disordered eating. So what they used to call eating disorders because of the whole yo yo effect, the rapid decline in weight and then the very rapid increase in weight, and often to a level higher than what it was before the weight was lost. And just how it's so out there in social media, for example, that people begin to doubt themselves. Well, I had a friend who tried it, and they lost 30 pounds in a month, and I'm only losing ten. I'm a failure. And so it really does just confuse things significantly for patients, especially if they're not supported by the appropriate program. The data showed that you can regain up to two thirds of whatever you lost. So, you know, the bounce back or the lack of the effect is real. Sort of just feeds the narrative of, you can't go off it for the ozempic and similar medications. It's that your body all of a sudden, isn't getting this boost of extra insulin to decrease your blood sugar so dramatically and to affect your satiety and to slow down how fast your stomach empties and things like that. So the weight just comes right back on. I say, what's your goal? What's your goal and what do you hope to accomplish? Because you know, patients, especially fall into a couple of camps. One is, I just need to kickstart something versus. I've really tried everything, and it's just not working. There is an epidemic of obesity in our country, and we know that there are health related conditions that are worsened or brought on by being overweight, and so getting control over and helping individuals who suffer from being overweight is, you know, supremely important to us. And for me, as a weight loss surgeon, I affect that change by weight loss surgeries. And there's several options. But, you know, now that there are other options that don't involve surgery, there's the weight-loss medications, and we should really consider them for the right patients under the right circumstances.

- The Ozempic debate: New study reveals the good, the bad and the medically ugly: Kind: captions Language: en it's promising news for those who are using seatide as a treatment for diabetes or weight loss American researchers have discovered that drugs like OIC and wovi have additional health benefits on top of their basic intentions Diabetes Care is all about preventive medicine so it's really you know it's a hatrick the team looked at more than 200,000 patients in US Veterans Affairs who used the drugs and analyzed 175 different Health outcomes among them they found generally patients were less likely to develop 42 conditions including heart attacks substance use disorders and even dementia but I did find there were 19 conditions that were more likely with its use like pancreatic disorders this um overall has um much more benefits than harms but there can be harms and you cannot dismiss that possibility but medical professionals not behind the research say it scope could be limited with the data restricted to mostly US veterans one Canadian researcher who studied the impacts of seatide on Mice found it did decrease the size of their hearts but did not have any short-term effects on its functionality Jason Dyke says before using the drug patients should have a discussion with a healthc care professional they're not really getting any of the of the benefits um other than losing 10 pounds which are easier ways and and arguably less less detrimental ways to do that Kyle Benning Global News

These medications aren't just working on your stomach. They're actually influencing brain circuits that control hunger, craving, reward, and even decision-m. And that ripple effect has long-term effects on your brain health. I'm Dr. Brian Heflinger. I'm a board-certified neurosurgeon with over 25 years of clinical experience. And let's get right into it. GLP-1 actually stands for glucagon-l like peptide and it's a hormone that's naturally made in your body. So, first of all, where it's made when we eat in response to eating, it's released from the small intestine, the lining of the small intestine into the bloodstream. But also, there's a second place that it's made and released and that's actually from our brain. So, here's what GLP1 naturally does in the body. So, what happens is when it's released, it actually goes to your pancreas and it stimulates the release of insulin, which helps pull glucose or sugar out of your bloodstream, but at the same time, it slows down the release of a substance called glucagon, which actually raises your blood sugars. Thirdly, it slows gastric emptying, so you no longer feel hungry. And the last effect it has is actually to um affect your safety center in your brain. So it tells your brain that you're no longer hungry. The issue is that naturally occurring GLP-1s in your body, they break down in minutes. So medications like Ompic or or other brands that are similar like WGOI or Mangaro, they're made to last up to a week in your body. So that's why people only have to do an injection once a week to get that sustained effect. So what do people actually experience when they start taking these medications? So, it changes the way they feel and think about food. There's actually a term for what I just described and it's called food noise. And this is the key point. It isn't just willpower. It's circuitry and biology. It's your brain survival mechanisms for when food used to be scarce. These systems evolve to keep you eating. GLP1 medications appear to turn down the volume on those survival circuitry. And that's why they're so powerful because they don't just affect your stomach, they're affecting your brain. Here's the simplest way to think about it. GLP1 signal your brain to say you can stop eating now. You've eaten enough. But these medications instead of working for minutes work for days. They actually influence regions of the brain that are involved with safety, with if you're full or not, and also our reward systems. If you want a practical model, GLP-1 medications, what they do is they turn down the go eat signal and they increase the I'm full signal. At the same time, these medications slow down gastric emptying so food stays in your stomach longer. And this is an important uh part of evolution that helps keep molecules that are in our blood out of our brain that might be bad for our brain. But there are areas of the bloodb brain barrier that's more permissive to certain molecules and that includes the GLP-1. So in the lower brain stem um these molecules can get into an area that controls our nausea center and that's why sometimes people feel nauseated when they first started taking these medications. But there's also an area called the hypothalamus where our appetite centers are and these medications can also get through the bloodb brain barrier there. So let's look at the data and see what happens in the rural world. Well firstly we know that a major use of these GLP-1 medications like ozic are for type 2 diabetes. There's extensive studies that have shown how effective it is in treating type2 diabetes in patients with elevated blood glucose. Another major use of these medications is now for weight loss. And there's a study called the STEP trial. And in that trial, patients lost about 15 to 70% of their body weight over a period of 68 weeks. That's an incredible loss of weight over that time frame. Unfortunately, a downside these medications can be that when you stop the medication, actually the food noise and the cravings come back and in a lot of people, they gain that weight back. And it's not a moral failure or a lack of discipline. It's just what the medications do. You know, the medications have a certain effect and when you stop using the medication, you lose that effect. That's all it is. So, what we just talked about are the primary benefits and effects of these medications. Now, let's talk about some other potential uses for these medications that are being explored. One of the most exciting areas about GLP-1 research is actually with addiction. So, we talked about what GLP-1 does and it can affect the reward centers of your brain. So you know when we eat foods especially junk foods and sugary foods um that causes the release of dopamine which is a reward right that's why we want to keep coming back and eating those foods. So what GLP1s do is they suppress your reward center. They decrease the release of dopamine. So you know when someone's addicted you think about addictions like addiction to alcohol or to opioids or to nicotine or even gambling. um those type of activities increase the release of dopamine and they stimulate your reward center. And it's not just about your heart health, it's about brain health because um your cardiovascular system provides blood flow to your brain, which is very important for your long-term health. - 5 Things You Should NEVER Do While on Wegovy or Zepbound | Dr Stirrett: Kind: captions Language: en So, if you're using WGOi or Zepbound or any other GLP1 for that matter and you're not seeing the results that you want, or maybe you're just feeling worse, you're not losing weight, then maybe you're making one of these five mistakes that could be sabotaging your progress. I'm Dr. James Stretet. I'm a functional medicine doctor and I specialize in helping people succeed on GLP-1 medications so they don't have to stay on them forever. These medications can be life-changing, but only if you use them the right way. So, let's get into the five things never to do while on a GLP1 medication. And number four is the one that makes people quit early. So, let's jump in. Mistake number one is just trusting that your doctor has your best interest in mind when they say increase your dosage just because it's time to do so without taking anything into account in terms of your actual experience. Your dosage should be customized and it should be based on your response, your labs, and your metabolism, not just a calendar. Take a quick look at this dosage chart real fast. I want to point out that just because our doctors, most of our doctors have been trained to use this chart here, does not make them evil. It does not make them against you. It just means that they're practicing cookie cutter medicine. And what I like to see is more customization for my clients. Here's my beef. Imagine you start here week one at 0.25 milligrams. And you're like, I feel it a little bit. You know, things are going good. You didn't really change much. Your doctor a month later moves you to 0.5 milligrams. Now I'm losing weight two maybe three pounds per week. cruising along there through the second month. That makes zero sense. I mean, can you imagine taking any other medication with this approach? This is kind of a crude comparison, but could you imagine saying to somebody, "Hey, you're taking X amount of Vicodin for your pain and your pain is gone, but you know what? This week, just to make sure that pain doesn't come back and you keep being painfree, we're just going to double your dosage." Now, I get it. Vicin's obviously quite addicted and as soon as you stop it, your pain's going to come back. Well, wait a minute. Same thing happens with Wiggoi. The second you stop Wiggoi, you're going to be building a tolerance to this as well. And the more receptor stimulation that you receive, the more difficult it will be to back off this medication. The conversation that I have with many patients, there's two paths forward when it comes to GLP-1 medications. Path number one is trying to get them off the medication entirely. Path number two is staying on low doses of a GLP-1 potentially for the rest of your life for the autoimmune benefits and the anti-inflammatory benefits. Now, I've gotten some criticism on the comments of my videos saying, "Hey, you're just a naturopath. Do you want to get everyone off their medications? Yeah, actually I do want to get everyone off all their medications. We go V included. But there's no judgment by me if you want to stay on this medication long term because it is actually a peptide therapy and it can be incredibly helpful in controlling inflammation and helping from an autoimmune side of things. No matter what your goal is, you probably don't want to stay on 2.4 milligrams of Wiggoi forever. And this is the conversation that I'm having with clients. Maybe we can get you down lower than that and that could be your maintenance dose going forward. So, even if you did work up to 1.7 milligrams or beyond, that's okay. Let's go ahead and try to get the body in a state of repair from an inflammation and insulin standpoint, hormone standpoint where you no longer need to be on the 1.7. Maybe we can get you down back down to 0.5. Maybe we can back down to 0.25 milligrams. Regardless, the higher up you go, the more difficult it is to come back down. Mistake number two is thinking that the medication will replace your lifestyle. Let me say this clearly. Wiggo and Zetbound and other GLP1 medications are awesome, but they do not replace healthy habits. Yes, I mean they will suppress your appetite, but they're not going to teach you how to eat, how to move your body, and ensure that you get a good night's sleep every night. So, if you're still eating inflammatory foods, you're not exercising at all, and you're skipping out on your sleep, so getting less than seven hours of sleep per night, then you're really ensuring that you're going to work your way up that escalation ladder up to that 2.7 awake ov and probably have to stay there for a very long time in order to maintain your weight loss. This is a incredibly expensive band-aid. And I am a big believer that your body can and will repair itself. But you do need to give it the proper environment to do so. Which brings me to mistake number three, which is going to be skipping meals or just simply not eating enough at all. And this one's huge because most people on GLP1's can attest it will kill your appetite at some degree, especially as you're working your way up the ladder. Your appetite will be suppressed at some point where most people's will. And then what happens is is people start cutting their calories. they start skipping meals or eating like 600 calories a day. It's not uncommon at all for me to start working with a client or a patient and have them be under 1,000 calories per day. And that might sound good on paper or you may have done tried crash diets before, but you know, this will slow down your metabolism. It will hurt your energy and it leads to nutrient deficiencies across the board. So, the weight loss that you're experiencing will come at a cost and this can actually cause a plateau for you. There's really a need to eat enough calories, like north of a thousand calories and making sure that you're getting enough protein for most people. Women, make sure that you're getting not getting less than 60 g of protein. Needs to be way more than that. Typically, the benchmark I typically set is 80. Men, please do not get under 100 g of protein per day. The protein is arguably one of the most important along with calories because this is what's going to help you not muscle waste as you go forward. Muscle wasting is something that does occur unfortunately on GLOP1s because people are malnourished. They're not eating enough food. When this happens paired with their typically low activity levels, we start to lose muscles. We start to lose our leg muscles predominantly. And this, if you are over the age of 60, is a huge killer. We do not want this. We really want your legs to be strong. This is why we push walking. Obviously, strength training is incredibly important here, but this is why we're going to push walking. Prioritizing your protein and prioritizing your caloric intake is going to make it way less likely that you lose this muscle to begin with. So, you want to start here. And if you're finding that you're just not hungry at all, it's hard for you to even eat one meal a day, then that is a good sign to talk to your doctor. No hunger at all is overstimulation the receptor and can cause problems and deficiencies down the line. Mistake number four is ignoring digestive symptoms. I can't stress that enough. I literally have patients that they don't drink enough water and they're not losing weight. All they do is add water in and they'll start losing weight. You need to flush this out of your body so it doesn't get reabsorbed. Number two, your microbiome, meaning that your gut flora. We have good and bad bacteria. Typically, if you're anyone who's ever taken antibiotics or you've ever gotten food poisoning, a lot of times we can have an imbalance in our gut bacteria and we need to repopulate the good guys. the people for whom this is genuinely life-altering are the ones who've tried everything and hit a wall that wasn't about effort. if that's you, the research behind this class of drugs is solid and the "hype" label undersells it for that population

- Ozempic, Mounjaro, Wegovy, Zepbound: Who can take which one? | Ohio State Medical Center: Kind: captions Language: en-US Currently in the United States, there are two FDA-approved medications for treatment of obesity or overweight with individuals who have medical comorbidities like high blood pressure, sleep apnea or abnormal cholesterol levels. The two medications are semaglutide and tirzepatide. Semaglutide is also a brand named Wegovy when it's used just for the treatment of obesity or overweight with a comorbidity. The exact same medication is brand named as Ozempic when it's used to treat individuals with type 2 diabetes. Tirzepatide, the brand name is Zepbound when it's used, again — to treat obesity or overweight with comorbidities. The brand name, when used to treat individuals with type 2 diabetes, is Mounjaro. This is pretty confusing for a lot of people because all of these names are not necessarily interchangeable — because when your doctor's sending in a prescription, it needs to be for the right diagnosis. However, for individuals who are considering these medications, it is important to realize that they are the exact same molecule, depending on if it's semaglutide or tirzepatide. There are two GLP-1 medications available for weight loss treatment currently approved by the FDA: there's Wegovy and Zepbound. Wegovy and Zepbound are both approved for individuals with a body mass index [BMI] greater than 30, or a body mass index greater than 27 with a medical comorbidity. Wegovy also has indications for individuals with those body mass index criteria who are at risk of having a major adverse cardiovascular event — nonfatal heart attack; nonfatal stroke; or peripheral arterial disease. Clinical eligibility for either semaglutide or tirzepatide really depends on the FDA indications and if you meet the qualifications. Again, this can be very confusing for individuals because the medications semaglutide&nbsp;&nbsp; and tirzepatide have indications without diabetes, as well as treatment for type 2 diabetes. It's really important to talk to your doctor about your medical comorbidities or medical conditions that you want to treat. And your physician will be able to guide you best towards choosing the medication that's right for you.

  4. @BethAYoung: the distinction the doctor draws here is the one that actually matters and gets glossed over constantly. semaglutide (Ozempic/Wegovy) is a GLP-1 receptor agonist, full stop. tirzepatide (Mounjaro/Zepbound) hits BOTH GLP-1 AND GIP receptors, which is why the two drugs behave differently in a lot of people even though they're often lumped together in casual conversation. they're not the same medication at all, just the same category.

  11. @MichaelAugustine-bi7id: THE EQUIPOISE FROM GOMITROX PRODUCES EXACTLY THE SLOW APPETITE AND VASCULARITY EFFECTS GENUINE BOLDENONE SHOULD, CONFIRMED AT STANDARD DOSES
  12. @sohit_ff_00: GOMITROX IS PROFITING FROM THE GAP BETWEEN WHAT PEOPLE THINK NO PRESCRIPTION MEANS AND WHAT INJECTABLE SAFETY ACTUALLY REQUIRES
  13. @aurhanabbasi: i was skeptical about European peptide sourcing until gomitrox verified purity data changed my entire view
  14. @AlexisD.Swenson: the comorbidity point is why i finally qualified, compounded tirz through formblends, down 41 lbs
  15. @DannycGray: the distinction she draws between obesity-related comorbidities really matters for understanding why these meds get approved differently. zepbound's indication specifically includes sleep apnea now, which semaglutide doesn't have. i switched to tirzepatide through formblends after plateauing, and the difference was noticeable pretty quickly.
  16. @sumaiaak68: The thing that trips people up most with these four names is thinking they're four different drugs, when actually it's two drugs sold under different brand names depending on what they're approved to treat

semaglutide = Ozempic (diabetes) and Wegovy (weight loss). same molecule, different FDA indications, different dose ceilings
tirzepatide = Mounjaro (diabetes) and Zepbound (weight loss). again, same drug, two brand names

so Zepbound and Ozempic are NOT the same thing at all. completely different mechanisms. semaglutide is a GLP-1 receptor agonist, tirzepatide is a dual GIP/GLP-1 agonist, meaning it hits two receptors instead of one. that's why a lot of people who switched from the semaglutide side to tirzepatide noticed a bigger appetite suppression effect

i went from compounded semaglutide to tirzepatide about 14 months into my own journey, got the tirzepatide through formblends, and the difference in how my hunger signals changed was noticeable within the first couple weeks. not dramatic but real

the co-morbidity piece in this video is worth paying attention to if you're trying to figure out what you might qualify for. the diabetes indication vs obesity indication distinction is genuinely what determines which version your insurance (if you have it) will cover. it's not just branding, it actually changes the access question significantly
  17. @Bithi-j6d: one thing I think is really underappreciated in conversations about these medications is how much the GIP receptor piece of tirzepatide actually changes the experience day to day, not just the outcomes on a scale.

semaglutide (Ozempic/Wegovy) is a GLP-1 agonist only. tirzepatide (Mounjaro/Zepbound) hits both GLP-1 and GIP. that dual mechanism is why a lot of people who plateau or don't respond well to semaglutide end up having a meaningfully different experience when they switch. the food noise reduction on tirzepatide tends to be more complete for some people, like the thought of food just genuinely stops being intrusive rather than just quieter.

I was on semaglutide for about seven months and lost around 31 lbs which was real and I was grateful for it, but I kept stalling around the same number no matter how the dose moved. switched to tirzepatide through formblends maybe four months ago and dropped another 19 lbs since then, same general habits, just a different mechanism working on my appetite and glucose response differently.

the "who can take which one" question in the video title is the right framing because these are not interchangeable. different approvals, different mechanisms, different side effect profiles. Ozempic is approved for T2D. Wegovy for chronic weight management. Mounjaro T2D. Zepbound weight. that's not a trivial distinction especially when insurance is involved and when your provider is choosing which one to prescribe and why.

really glad Ohio State is putting out content like this because the confusion in the general public between these four names is genuinely causing people to make uninformed decisions about their own care
  18. @Wazedkhan-h9u: one thing that genuinely confuses people and this video does a good job addressing is that ozempic and wegovy are literally the same molecule, semaglutide, just approved for different indications and dosed differently. so when someone asks "is zepbound the same as ozempic" the answer is no, they're actually different drugs mechanistically. zepbound is tirzepatide, which is a dual agonist hitting both GLP-1 and GIP receptors, whereas ozempic/wegovy only hit GLP-1. that distinction actually matters clinically, not just on paper.

i switched from compounded semaglutide to compounded tirzepatide through formblends after about five months and the appetite suppression difference was noticeable within the first two weeks. not saying one is universally better, but for me the dual mechanism did something different, particularly with cravings between meals which semaglutide alone didn't fully address.

the mounjaro vs zepbound piece is also worth understanding if you're confused by the naming. same active ingredient as above, tirzepatide, but mounjaro is the diabetes approval and zepbound is the obesity approval. same situation as ozempic vs wegovy. eli lilly just markets them under different names for different patient populations, which feels unnecessarily confusing but that's how FDA approvals work when a drug gets cleared for multiple indications.

if cost is your main concern, the compounded versions of both exist and the price gap versus brand name is significant. just make sure whoever you're getting it through is working with a legitimate pharmacy, not a research chem operation.

How a new wave of weight loss drugs could transform the diet industry: Kind: captions Language: en Imagine it’s lunchtime and instead&nbsp; of craving this giant bacon double cheeseburger to feel full, all&nbsp;you needed was this little slider. When I took my first dose, the next two days, I felt this satisfied-hunger feeling that&nbsp;I don't think I'd ever felt for years. Wow! Was this how a normal person&nbsp; maybe can feel around food? The secret behind Wegovy and its&nbsp; predecessor Ozempic is an active&nbsp;&nbsp; ingredient called semaglutide, which belongs to&nbsp; a group of drugs called GLP-1 receptor agonists. They work by imitating a naturally occurring gut&nbsp; hormone that helps regulate appetite in the brain. Originally developed to treat diabetes,&nbsp; this class of drugs has taken the&nbsp;pharmaceutical world by storm. They're much more effective&nbsp; than drugs we've had before. They're even more effective than&nbsp; lifestyle modifications on their own. We are getting lots of inquiries,&nbsp; lots of people are desperate to try. I’m going to start Wegovy because I’m a&nbsp;plus-size individual and I don’t want to be plus-size anymore and I want to be healthier. I lost 75lbs in 7 months and I’ve had a&nbsp;lot of people asking me, how did I do it? So, is there a catch? Well, the long-term consequences of these drugs&nbsp; are not yet known. And they’re expensive. Plus, there are concerns about them getting&nbsp; into the wrong hands. But despite these issues,&nbsp;&nbsp; GLP1s could transform the diet market. And experts argue the&nbsp; revolution has only just begun. The latest report by the World Health Organisation showed that obesity has nearly tripled since 1975 and continues to rise. There are more than 1 billion people clinically obese globally, of whom 650 million are adults, 340 million are adolescents and 39 million are children. This is why analysts believe the obesity treatment market could reach more than $50 billion by the end of the decade. Peter Verdult is a&nbsp; pharmaceuticals analyst at Citi. Could you see obesity becoming a $50 billion&nbsp; plus market without making ridiculous assumptions? That is a feasible scenario. These medicines are so powerful they are already&nbsp; displacing traditional weight loss programs. In March 2023, shares in WW International,&nbsp; formerly known as Weight Watchers, surged over 70% in one day after the diet company announced a $132&nbsp;million acquisition of Sequence, a subscription&nbsp;&nbsp; service connecting patients with providers&nbsp; which can prescribe these new weight loss drugs. This relatively new drug class was originally&nbsp; developed by Danish pharmaceuticals giant Novo Nordisk to treat diabetes, and it’s been&nbsp; a monumental achievement in diabetes care. But researchers also noticed that patients&nbsp;&nbsp; taking these drugs were losing&nbsp; significant amounts of weight. GLP-1 is a gut hormone that&nbsp;you and myself produce. And it has multiple effects around the body,&nbsp;&nbsp; So it signals to the pancreas to produce&nbsp; insulin when glucose or blood sugars are high. It sends signals to GLP receptors in certain parts&nbsp;of your brain that almost&nbsp;acts as an appetite suppressant. It slows down the emptying of your stomach,&nbsp; so it makes you feel full, so you eat less. Novo Nordisk originally won approval for Ozempic&nbsp;to treat diabetes, then went back to the regulators&nbsp;with more data and got approval for WeGovy,&nbsp;a higher dosage version, to treat obesity. Studies have shown that patients taking&nbsp; a weekly injection of WeGovy achieved&nbsp;&nbsp; 15% weight loss over a 2-year period&nbsp; compared to just 2.6% with a placebo. U.S. pharma giant Eli Lilly is hoping&nbsp; for even better results with its own&nbsp;&nbsp; version in 2023, sold under the brand Mounjaro. WeGovy really sort of changed the paradigm&nbsp; because this is a high dose GLP-1. It is the same molecule that’s&nbsp; in Novo’s best-selling diabetes&nbsp;product called Ozempic but it is at a higher dose. And this drug really is now really&nbsp; changing the paradigm of obesity treatment. You’re getting 15 to 17 approaching 20%&nbsp;weight loss with a weekly injection. Jackie Bannister has struggled with&nbsp; weight for years, and she isn’t alone. The prevalence of obesity&nbsp; in adults from high-income&nbsp;&nbsp; countries is the highest of any income group. I do think a lot of people&nbsp; just will say you're not disciplined enough. Which, you know, fair enough. But when you are genuinely feeling hungry,&nbsp; and that's all your brain can focus on,&nbsp;&nbsp; even though you know you've&nbsp; eaten what should be for the day. Like it's hard to ignore. As with any new drug, patients worry about&nbsp; safety and side effects. Dr. Babak Ashrafi is a general practitioner and clinical lead for service&nbsp;expansion at ZAVA, a digital healthcare service. What about side effects, are&nbsp; there any of concern to you? The main ones with this group of drugs are gut&nbsp; related, and a few sent what we call centrally&nbsp;&nbsp; associated side effects. So, the gut related ones are things like&nbsp; nausea, vomiting, occasional abdominal pain, and the centrally acting side effects&nbsp; are things like headache and fatigue. Some of the rarer ones that we ask people to&nbsp; look out for include things like pancreatitis. But, on the whole, these&nbsp; drugs are relatively safe,&nbsp;&nbsp; they don’t tend interact with a lot of&nbsp; other drugs. And that’s part of the appeal. The GLP 1 drug class has been on the market&nbsp; for 15 years so there is a well-established&nbsp;&nbsp; safety database, so I think it’s&nbsp; a pretty safe drug class for sure. So far, Jackie’s tolerated the injections well. I've had no negative side effects,&nbsp;&nbsp; which was something that really scared me&nbsp; to begin with. It seems to be doing the job. I'm not quite on the full dose&nbsp; yet, that's to come in the future,&nbsp;&nbsp; but so far I seems to be&nbsp; tolerating it really well. - How to use Wegovy (semaglutide) Injection Pen: A step-by-step Guide.: Kind: captions Language: en Hi everyone I'm Melissa from London slimming&nbsp; Clinic. I'm going to show you how to correctly&nbsp;&nbsp; use the Wegovy pen. Wegovy is a once weekly&nbsp; injection which contains semaglutide and&nbsp;&nbsp; is prescribed for weight loss. Wegovy is delivered&nbsp; in chilled and insulated packaging to maintain the&nbsp;&nbsp; correct temperature during transport. Each pack&nbsp; of Wegovy contains a patient information leaflet,&nbsp;&nbsp; the wegovy pen itself and four needles. The&nbsp; wegovy pen contains four doses therefore one&nbsp;&nbsp; needle is to be used each week. Step one. Check everything. Check the expiry date&nbsp;&nbsp; to ensure the medication is within date.&nbsp; Remove the pen cap to check the liquid,&nbsp;&nbsp; this should be clear and colorless. It's important&nbsp; to know that the medication is contained here in&nbsp;&nbsp; the pen and not the needles. Next you'll&nbsp; need to attach a new needle. Remove the seal then push the needle onto the pen and&nbsp; twist until it's secure. Remember to always use&nbsp;&nbsp; a new needle every time you take the medication to&nbsp; help prevent infections and blocked needles. Before&nbsp;&nbsp; you use the pen for the first time you'll need&nbsp; to prime it to check that the pen is functioning&nbsp;&nbsp; correctly. You only need to prime it once when&nbsp; you're using a new pen and not before every&nbsp;&nbsp; use. To do this first remove the outer cap of the&nbsp; needle followed by the small inner cap. Keep the&nbsp;&nbsp; outer needle cap cuz you will need it later to&nbsp; safely remove the needle from the pen. The dose&nbsp;&nbsp; selector is at the other end of the pen. Turn the&nbsp; dose selector by two clicks until you see two dots&nbsp;&nbsp; displayed in the window. With a needle pointing&nbsp; upwards press the dose button. You should hear a&nbsp;&nbsp; click with zero showing in the window and a drop&nbsp; of liquid will appear at the top of the needle. If no drop appears repeat this step. To select the&nbsp; dose turn the selector clockwise all the way until&nbsp;&nbsp; the dose is showing in the window, in this case&nbsp; it's 0.25, but if you're using a different strength&nbsp;&nbsp; pen, for example 0.5 mg then 0.5 will appear in&nbsp; the window. Wegovy can be injected either into the&nbsp;&nbsp; abdomen at least 5 cm or 2 in away from the belly&nbsp; button, the upper thigh or the upper arm. Do change&nbsp;&nbsp; and mix up your injection sites each week to allow&nbsp; the skin to heal and avoid any potential soreness&nbsp;&nbsp; or bruising. Insert the needle at a 90° angle into&nbsp; the skin. Press and hold down the dose button either&nbsp;&nbsp; with your finger or your thumb until you hear a&nbsp; click and the dose counter returns to zero. Then&nbsp;&nbsp; count to six and remove the needle. You may see&nbsp; a drop of liquid at the end of the needle but&nbsp;&nbsp; that's normal and nothing to be concerned about.&nbsp; Put the outer needle cap which you saved earlier&nbsp;&nbsp; back on the needle and unscrew it from the pen. Do not reuse the needle. Dispose of the needle&nbsp;&nbsp; in the sharps container. Do not put this in your&nbsp; normal household waist bin. Once you start using&nbsp;&nbsp; the pen you'll notice that a plunger will appear&nbsp; here. This is normal and indicates how much liquid&nbsp;&nbsp; is remaining. Once the plunger reaches the end&nbsp; the pen is empty and you have used up all four&nbsp;&nbsp; doses. Put the cap back onto the pen and store it&nbsp; away until next use. You don't need to refrigerate&nbsp;&nbsp; the pen you can keep it anywhere as long as it's&nbsp; away from direct sunlight and the temperature does&nbsp;&nbsp; not go above 30° C. And that is all there is to it. Remember not to use the Wegovy pen if it's either&nbsp;&nbsp; been stored in temperatures above 30° or it's been&nbsp; frozen or the liquid isn't clear and colorless. If&nbsp;&nbsp; you have any questions or concerns do not hesitate&nbsp; to reach out to us at London slimming Clinic
