- 3 Tips to Use Rybelsus for MAX Results: Kind: captions Language: en-CA Rybelsus is basically the pill version of Ozempic. While both medications are&nbsp; considered similarly effective,&nbsp;&nbsp; there are different ways you&nbsp; can take oral semaglutide that can greatly impact its effectiveness&nbsp;&nbsp; compared to the injectable forms of&nbsp; semaglutide like Ozempic or Wegovy. So, what are the different ways you can&nbsp; take it to maximize its effectiveness? To get the most out of oral semaglutide, you’ll&nbsp; be told to follow a number of specific steps. First, you’ll need to take it on an empty stomach. Second, you can take it with no&nbsp; more than half a glass of water. Third, you’ll need to avoid eating for at&nbsp; least 30 minutes after taking the pill. But why are these steps important? And&nbsp; can we improve on these steps? Let's&nbsp;&nbsp; break down each instruction to understand better. Taking oral semaglutide on an empty&nbsp; stomach is probably the most important. When you take the pill, it&nbsp; disperses in your stomach,&nbsp;&nbsp; which is where it's primarily&nbsp; absorbed into the bloodstream. Studies show that if you take&nbsp; oral semaglutide with food,&nbsp;&nbsp; it essentially isn’t absorbed at all&nbsp; and barely shows up in your blood. However, when taken on an empty stomach,&nbsp; semaglutide is absorbed effectively. So, taking oral semaglutide before you’ve eaten&nbsp; anything, for example first thing in the morning,&nbsp;&nbsp; is essential. If you take it with&nbsp; food, it might not work at all. The next important step is&nbsp; to take oral semaglutide&nbsp;&nbsp; with no more than half a glass of water. Research has shown that semaglutide is much&nbsp; better absorbed when taken with less water. Studies show that there's about&nbsp; a 40% decrease in absorption when&nbsp;&nbsp; taken with a full glass (240 ml) of water&nbsp; compared to a quarter cup (50 ml) of water. Later studies found that taking it with 50&nbsp; ml of water is about the same as 120 ml,&nbsp;&nbsp; which is why the current recommendation is to&nbsp; use it with no more than half a glass of water. Finally, you’ll need to wait at least 30 minutes&nbsp; after taking oral semaglutide before eating. Studies comparing different waiting times of&nbsp;&nbsp; 15, 30, 60, and 120 minutes found that the longer&nbsp; you wait to eat, the more semaglutide is absorbed. For example, waiting 120 minutes resulted in over&nbsp;&nbsp; three times more absorption&nbsp; than waiting only 15 minutes. The final recommendation of&nbsp; 30 minutes is likely a balance&nbsp;&nbsp; between effective absorption&nbsp; and practical waiting time. I mean, it might be pretty difficult to not&nbsp; eat for 2 hours after taking oral semaglutide. To sum it all up, the absorption of oral&nbsp; semaglutide is significantly affected by&nbsp;&nbsp; how and when you take it. If you take it with&nbsp; food, absorption is almost completely blocked. Taking it with a full glass of water instead of&nbsp;&nbsp; the recommended half a glass can&nbsp; reduce absorption by up to 40%, making a 14 mg dose feel more like 8 mg,&nbsp; or a 50 mg dose feel more like 30 mg. If you wait only 15 minutes instead of&nbsp; the recommended 30 minutes before eating,&nbsp;&nbsp; absorption can be cut in half, so a 14 mg dose would feel more like 7&nbsp; mg, and a 50 mg dose more like 25 mg. Doing both—taking it with a full glass of&nbsp; water and eating only 15 minutes later,&nbsp;&nbsp; would make absorption even worse. On the other hand, waiting 1 hour instead of the&nbsp;&nbsp; recommended 30 minutes before eating&nbsp; can increase absorption by up to 25%. This means a 14 mg dose would feel more like&nbsp; 18 mg, and a 50 mg dose more like 63 mg. Waiting 2 hours could increase&nbsp; the absorption by up to 70%,&nbsp;&nbsp; so a 14 mg dose would feel more like 24&nbsp; mg, and a 50 mg dose more like 85 mg. Now, waiting 2 hours before&nbsp; eating might seem tough,&nbsp;&nbsp; but it could be easier if you're also&nbsp; intermittent fasting and skipping breakfast. For example, a theoretical dosing&nbsp; schedule to maximize absorption could be: wake up in the morning at 9 am,&nbsp; take Rybelsus with no more than&nbsp;&nbsp; half a glass of water, and then&nbsp; don't eat until lunch at noon. This would improve the absorption of your&nbsp; semaglutide and combine the benefits of&nbsp;&nbsp; intermittent fasting, making both more effective. It's important to note that these calculations&nbsp; are theoretical. My recommendation is to&nbsp;&nbsp; always follow your doctor's advice&nbsp; and the normal dosing guidelines. However, theoretically speaking, increasing&nbsp; the time you wait before eating after taking&nbsp;&nbsp; oral semaglutide could boost its effectiveness. Now let’s get into why this happens.&nbsp; Unlike food or typical medications,&nbsp;&nbsp; oral semaglutide is primarily absorbed&nbsp; in the stomach, not the intestines. Studies involving dogs provide&nbsp; insight into this process. They compared semaglutide levels in dogs&nbsp; with their stomachs blocked off from their&nbsp;&nbsp; intestines to those with normal stomachs&nbsp; and found similar absorption patterns. This shows that semaglutide&nbsp; does not need to enter the&nbsp;&nbsp; intestines and is absorbed through the stomach, and this absorption can be easily disrupted by&nbsp; food or any other substances in the stomach. Many clinicians consider Ozempic and&nbsp; Rybelsus therapeutically similar. However, people often find oral semaglutide like&nbsp;&nbsp; Rybelsus less effective because they don't&nbsp; strictly follow the dosing instructions. The strict requirements for taking&nbsp; Rybelsus—on an empty stomach,&nbsp;&nbsp; with limited water, and waiting before eating mean it can be much less effective if these&nbsp; instructions aren't followed precisely. In contrast, injected semaglutide like&nbsp; Ozempic or Wegovy is easier to use.&nbsp;&nbsp; It requires just one injection per week,&nbsp; with no strict rules about food or water. This makes it more convenient and ensures nearly&nbsp;&nbsp; 100% absorption regardless of&nbsp; your eating habits or schedule. So oral and injected semaglutide are&nbsp; therapeutically similar if used correctly. However, if you prefer not to worry&nbsp; about strict dosing instructions,&nbsp;&nbsp; the injected versions might&nbsp; be a better option for you. Another interesting difference between&nbsp; injected and oral semaglutide stems&nbsp;&nbsp; from the fact that semaglutide is a fragile&nbsp; molecule, easily destroyed by stomach acid. Many of these GLP-1 agonists like&nbsp; semaglutide are vulnerable to the&nbsp;&nbsp; acidic environment and digestive&nbsp; enzymes in the stomach and gut. That's why many GLP-1 agonists,&nbsp; such as Mounjaro, Trulicity,&nbsp;&nbsp; and Ozempic, are injected—to protect&nbsp; them from stomach acids and enzymes. So how does oral semaglutide&nbsp; manage to survive? The answer&nbsp;&nbsp; lies in a compound called SNAC (sodium&nbsp; N-(8-[2-hydroxybenzoyl]amino) caprylate). SNAC acts as an absorption enhancer,&nbsp; protecting semaglutide from the harsh&nbsp;&nbsp; gastric environment and aiding its&nbsp; absorption through the stomach lining. Studies show that SNAC significantly&nbsp; improves the absorption of oral semaglutide&nbsp;&nbsp; into the bloodstream compared to&nbsp; taking semaglutide without it. Currently, there are three&nbsp; versions of semaglutide,&nbsp;&nbsp; with a fourth pill version&nbsp; for weight loss coming soon. This means that there will be one injected and&nbsp; one pill form for both diabetes and weight loss. While the drug itself is the same across&nbsp; all versions, the dosing methods vary. Semaglutide is always started at a lower dose&nbsp;&nbsp; and gradually increased until&nbsp; you reach the maximum dosage. Brands approved for weight loss have more steps&nbsp;&nbsp; and a higher maximum dose compared&nbsp; to those approved for diabetes. Though people sometimes use semaglutide&nbsp; off-label for different purposes,&nbsp;&nbsp; FDA-approved uses are important&nbsp; for insurance coverage and cost. Semaglutide is expensive regardless of use,&nbsp;&nbsp; but the pill and injected forms of&nbsp; semaglutide cost about the same monthly. It's when it’s prescribed for weight loss, as&nbsp; with Wegovy, that the cost is usually much higher. Hi, I’m Dr. Brian Yeung. Do you use&nbsp; oral semaglutide like Rybelsus? And&nbsp;&nbsp; what are your experiences? Let&nbsp; me know in the comments below. Subscribe and hit the notification bell to stay&nbsp; up to date, and if you found it informative,&nbsp;&nbsp; leave me a like, and share this video&nbsp; with someone you know can use the info.

- Ozempic in a PILL?!  Rybelsus Full Review from a Diabetes PA: Kind: captions Language: en for the last 15 years if you wanted to treat your type 2 diabetes with a medication in the glp1 class you were stuck actually you had to get stuck get it because it's because it's needles like like shots no you're right that's stupid i'll edit that out today's sugar high video is all about the only oral glp1 medication ribelsus let's talk about how this medication works important things you need to know about taking it and how a glp-1 diabetes medication finally made it from a needle to a pill [Music] what's up guys i'm pa david i'm a board-certified and licensed diabetes specialty pa practicing in southern california and sugar high is your channel for relatable and reliable diabetes information that is always easy to understand if this is your first time to sugar high i hope you will consider subscribing because i think you'll learn a lot and i think you'll have fun too we're going to be talking today about a medication called right bellicis and this video is not sponsored in any way by the company that makes that everything we talk about here today is based on either clinical trial evidence or my own honest opinion about where this medication fits into the management of type 2 diabetes after metformin meds in the glp-1 family are amongst the most highly recommended type 2 diabetes medications by professional diabetes organizations the only problem is that since the first one was released all the way back in they've only been available in injected form they're not insulin but because of the fact that the stomach acid tends to digest and break down this type of medication if you swallow it they can only be given through a needle thankfully they've managed to develop formulations of these meds that only need to be injected once a week so it's not quite as much of a burden on people but some people just can't handle the idea of giving themselves an injection well guess what we now have the first glp-1 medication ever to be available in pill form that's right no more injections no more needles it's a pill rypelsis is actually the same medication as another medication in this family called ozempic they're both semi-glutide they just have different names whether it's in the injected form or the oral version now this is the one two three fourth video that i made in a series on these types of medications so if you're a sugar high subscriber and you've seen those other videos you've heard me explain over and over again what gop one is and what it does which is good by the way i'm a firm believer that it's a good thing for people to know all about how their medications work but since we've covered it so much on this channel i promise that i won't go into crazy detail again but just in case you're new to sugar high the short version is that glp1 is a hormone that your intestine naturally releases when you eat that signals your pancreas to release insulin decreases feelings of hunger decreases glucose production by the liver and it slows down how quickly your stomach digests and empties out these medications basically add more of this hormone to your system since your own glp one doesn't work as well when you have type 2 diabetes if you want to know the full story on how glp1 works and how these medications fit into diabetes management including a complete overview of the side effects check out this video right here and i'll also link this video at the end in case you don't want to bother scrolling back to this point now i don't think anybody came here because they're looking for a boring lecture on pharmacokinetics but let's talk a little bit about how they managed to do what no other medication in this class could take an injected medication and put it in the pill form the reason that i want to talk about this is that understanding how they manage to make this happen will help you to understand a few important caveats when it comes to how this pill needs to be taken the only way that rybel says was able to be put into pill form was by pairing the medication up with another chemical that helps protect it from being broken down by stomach acid what this extra ingredient does is it sort of neutralizes the stomach acid surrounding the pill in order to protect it long enough for the medication to absorb through the stomach lining and into your bloodstream most medications when you take them just dissolve and mix in with your food and when the food moves into the intestine to be absorbed the medication absorbs too but not ribelsus ribelsus has to stick to your stomach wall get protected by that extra ingredient and then absorb through the stomach lining not the intestine where there are other digestive enzymes that could also break it down there too that means that if you take ribosomes with food the pill is going to be mixed in with your meal and not absorbed through the stomach if you take it with too much water the medicine will dissolve into the water and the pill won't stick to the stomach lining where it has the ability to absorb if you take it with other medications as the stomach churns those other pills may knock the right bellicis pill off the stomach wall and you won't observe that either and for that reason this medication needs to be taken first thing in the morning on an empty stomach with no more than half a glass of water not milk not orange juice not coffee again it's not like it's gonna cause some horrible side effect if you take it with food it's just gonna waste the medication because you won't be able to absorb it properly and it's not gonna work i hope that helps it makes sense because i know it can be annoying when your healthcare provider or your pharmacist tells you that it's important to take a medication a certain way or at a certain time of the day but if you know the reason behind why those limitations are put on it then it starts to make sense and it makes it a little easier to go along with that plan rybilisis comes in three different doses three milligrams seven milligrams and fourteen milligrams but only the seven and the fourteen milligram doses are considered to be effective therapeutic doses this three milligram pill is meant to be just an introductory dose for you to start on for the first month while your body gets used to the medication there's nothing like particularly dangerous about starting at the seven milligram pill but your chances of having side effects like nausea are gonna be way higher if you don't pace yourself out a little bit and get used to that lower three milligram dose first so since three milligrams isn't all that effective in reducing blood sugar we don't start you at three milligrams and then just leave you there the plan is that we take that three milligram pill for 30 days just to get used to it and then we increase to the seven milligram pill if the side effects are manageable at the end of that first month and after you've been on that seven milligram pill for at least a month if you need extra help to get your blood glucose down further your health care provider can opt to increase it to that 14 milligram dose since this isn't an injectable medication there really isn't going to be a demonstration on how to do it like there was in the videos with the other medications in this class hey you're off the hook kevin but i do want to show you a little bit about the packaging with ribalysis most of the time when you get a medication from a pharmacy you get like a bottle of pills right you can leave these in the bottle and then take them when you're ready or if you want you can divide like a week's worth out into a pill divider like this one well rybelsis comes in cardboard blister packs where each pill is separated into its own blister leave those pills in the blisters until the morning you're ready to take one because these pills are moisture sensitive and if you pop them all out of the blisters into a pill divider they may not be good by the time you actually get around to taking them the box is color coded for the dose of the pill the three milligram pill has this green label the seven milligram is red and the 14 mg has a blue color on the box of course the dose is also printed on it as well each box has three blister cards in it and each blister card has 10 pills for a total of 30 pills in a box so this ought to last you exactly one month unless it happens to be january or february or march in may july august you know what i mean since rybelis is a prescribed medication even the blister packs still need to be child proof you'll notice that you can't simply pull the slide card out and remove the medication so to open it put your thumb on the number one press down and then with your other hand grab and pull by this number two then it'll slide right out you'll notice that written right here on the card as soon as you pull it out are those really important instructions that we talked about earlier take the pills on an empty stomach first thing in the morning take it with no more than four ounces or half a glass of water and wait 30 minutes before eating anything else or taking other medications i like that they remind you of this as soon as you open it because otherwise it can be kind of easy to forget when you flip it open you'll see your blister packs in there you want to keep them in the blister packs until you're ready to use it pop out the one that you're going to take there he is on the table right there and he can be taken in accordance with the directions that we just talked about so there's your dosing guide let's talk about how well this stuff works in most clinical trials ribelsus reduced a1c by an average of about 1.3 points it's even been tested in head-to-head clinical trials against a few other very commonly prescribed oral diabetes medications and it turns out that it does pretty well the most commonly prescribed non-generic type 2 diabetes medication in the world is genevia well in a direct comparison trial genovia reduced a1c by only about 0.8 points compared to rybelis's 1.3 it's also been tested against giardians which in that study lowered a1c by 0.9 points compared to bribella since it's 1.3 so when it comes to effectiveness ribosome seems to hold up pretty well now ribosis isn't meant to be used as a weight loss medication but it is a nice side effect that most people do tend to lose a little bit of weight when using it especially when you consider the fact that some of the older diabetes medications tend to cause weight gain which can make diabetes worse in the long run in most clinical trials ribelsus brought between seven to nine pounds of weight loss and that's approximately three to four kilograms for my international viewers now one of the reasons we often like to use medications in this category is that many of them have been proven in clinical trials to reduce the risk of heart disease and cardiovascular events like heart attack and stroke in the clinical trial testing this in there was no increase in risk in fact fewer people had cardiovascular events with ribosomes than placebo but it wasn't enough of a difference to be considered statistically significant so as of the date of this video ribalysis does not have the fda recommendation for reduction of cardiovascular risk okay so i know what you're thinking i can feel your thoughts coming through my wi-fi you're thinking wait a minute pa david you said a minute ago that ribalysis is the same medication as ozempig just one's an injection and one is a pill and i watched your video on ozempig because i'm a super cool sugar high subscriber and in that video you said that ozembik does have the recommendation for cardiovascular protection so how can the same medication protect the heart in injected form but not in pill form okay good question it all comes down to study design and statistics and there's a lot of boring math involved so i'm not going to do that to you but the short version of the story is that the cardiovascular study for ozempic was longer than the ribelsa study so they had more data the ribelsus study was never designed to be big enough or long enough to gather that kind of data to prove a superior risk reduction the goal was just to satisfy the fda requirements proving that it doesn't cause harm to the heart so that they can make ribalysis available in your pharmacy there's another study underway right now secret information that's bigger longer in duration and designed to prove definitively whether ribosys actually offers cardiovascular risk reduction that study won't be completed for a few more years but depending on the results we may or may not see that cardiovascular recommendations show up so stay tuned so what about side effects the common side effect of pretty much every medication in this category are the gastrointestinal symptoms like nausea vomiting diarrhea and abdominal discomfort remember that this medication is replacing a hormone whose job it is to make you feel full so these side effects are really sort of tied into the primary function of the hormone aren't they it's a feeling of being very full not wanting to eat as much and sometimes that can come across as nausea the frequency of the common side effects with ribalysis isn't really more or less than the other glp1 medications with the exception of by durian which as we've discussed in a separate video has the lowest frequency of side effects in this class you'll notice that in each one of these by durian has less frequent side effects than all of the others nausea happens in about 20 percent of people using it which is consistent with most of the others in this class about 10 of people get diarrhea and that's a little more than some glp1 drugs a little less than others but only a slight difference most of these medications cause vomiting in about nine percent of people and right balances is pretty close to that with about eight percent ribelsus has all of the standard fda warnings for almost all glp1 medications if you open up and read that package insert that comes with the medication one of the first things that you'll see is a black box warning about medullary thyroid cancer that is based entirely on the fact that when those medications were tested in rats before it ever got to human trials the rats tended to develop a specific type of thyroid cancer now keep in mind rat thyroids are very different than human thyroids and there's no evidence that these cause medullary thyroid cancer in humans but because of the fact that there hasn't been a dedicated clinical trial proving that it doesn't cause it in humans the fda advises that if you or if someone in your family has this very rare type of thyroid cancer not the more common types of thyroid cancer then you should use another medication instead now since one of the main effects of this medication is on the pancreas in really rare cases the pancreas can get irritated in a condition called pancreatitis in the thousands of patients that i've treated for diabetes i've only seen this happen in a couple of my patients it is not common but if you were to feel a sudden severe through and through stabbing kind of pain right in the middle of your abdomen with nausea and vomiting call your doctor to get checked out if it's actually caused by the medication then we stop using it and things usually go back to normal so what are my personal thoughts on rybilisis well i've had a lot of good luck with ribalysis and my patients that are using it have had mostly good experiences with it in terms of a1c improvement and the side effects i have had a couple of people tell me that the nazi was just too much and they stopped taking it and that's okay this pill isn't for everyone no medication is but thankfully most people do seem to do pretty well with it and most of them don't have any huge concerns you might be wondering how this fits into a world where the same already available in that injected option of ozempic now that there's an oral option are the older injectable versions now obsolete no so far that does not appear to be the case in my experience i found that both oral and injected glp1 medications actually have a place in diabetes management and i usually let my patients choose which option fits their lifestyle and their preference is the best if i ask people would you prefer a pill or an injection they will always say a pill but when i give them the full story that the pill has to be taken in a very particular way and that the injected option is a very tiny needle that only has to be used once a week a lot of people say you know what that injection doesn't look so bad and it is only once a week i think i'd rather do that and for those folks who are absolutely no way to a needle not today not never well now we have an oral option it is completely a person by person choice in my opinion and there's your full review of ribelsus i hope that was a helpful and balanced overview of the medication and how to use it if there's anything that i left out or if you still have questions about this medication hit me up in those comments i usually try to respond to people within a couple of days if you enjoyed this video or you found it helpful i'd really appreciate it if you could click that like button since it really actually does help this channel to reach as many people as possible and make sure that - Doctor Reviews Oral Ozempic (Rybelsus) - Better Than Injections?: Kind: captions Language: en Imagine a zmpic oil goi but in a pill. That's rubbelis. And in this video I'm going to review this medication and see if it can say goodbye to injections. Hi guys, I'm Dr. Kid and in this video I'm going to break down three key things. The first of which is how does rebelsis survive in the stomach when other GLP1s can't? How rubbis weight loss actually compares to zepicovi? And I'm also going to compare rebelsis to other common weight loss oral medications such as fentamine and contra. So what is robelis? Well, rebelsus is the brand name for oral semiglutide which is the same active ingredient that's seen in ompic and wgoi. Instead of weekly injections, rubbelis you can take as a daily oral tablet. So how does rebelsis work? Well, simaglutide is a GLP1 agonist. This means it mimics a normal hormone in our body called GLP1. This hormone regulates appetite, slows gastric emptying and increases insulin release. The result is you eat less and feel fuller for longer so you lose weight. However, semiglutide is a peptide which means it's usually broken down in the gut. So, how does Ribbelis survive digestion? Ribbelis uses a clever delivery enhancer called snack. Snack changes the local pH of the stomach to protect semiglutai from being broken down and also helps absorb it through the stomach wall. That's why rebels has strict instructions. Take it first thing in the morning with a small sip of water, no food for at least 30 minutes. So, the biggest question is how does the weight loss of ribbs compare to its injectable counterparts? This graph compares the different options in head-to-head comparisons where goi, which uses higher dose of magglutide up to 2.4 4 millig leads the pack with nearly 15% body weight loss after 68 weeks. Ampic which has semigly up to 1 millig falls in the 6 to8% range after 68 weeks for our belsis after 68 weeks there's about a 4 to 5% weight loss with the 40 mig dose. So why the difference? Mostly due to dose limitations or mluta can't yet reach the higher doses seen in rogoi because of absorption limits and gastrointestinal side effects. Now though rebels doesn't give as much weight loss as it injectable counterparts. How does it fare against other oral weight loss medications? This is a graph comparing ribbels to two other common oral weight loss medications, Contrave and Fentamine. Fentamine, a stimulant appetite suppressant, delivers 5 to 10% weight loss in 12 weeks, but longer studies show it can achieve 7 to 13% weight loss over 24 weeks. However, recent research suggests that while longerterm use is possible, effectiveness may plateau and some weight regain often occurs beyond 12 months. Contra is a medication that takes a little longer to work and takes 56 weeks to achieve its 5 to 9% weight loss. a medication that works through both suppressing appetite and suppressing the reward pathways. Ribbelsis, as we already know, gives about 4 to 5% weight loss after 68 weeks. So, compared to other common oral weight loss medications, Ribbelsis still achieves the least amount of weight loss on average. So, what are the side effects of Relis? Like all GLP1s, the most common side effect is nausea and vomiting followed by diarrhea. In rare cases, you may get pancreatitis. So, what's the final verdict on rebelsis? Well, if your goal is weight loss, honestly, there are probably better options. The injectable versions are far superior for weight loss. And if you really had injections, there are other oral medications that are slightly more effective for weight loss, such as fentamina contra than rebels. However, it may be an option to consider if you really had injections and you get side effects from the other oral weight loss medications. So, I hope you've enjoyed this concise review of Rebelsis. If you did, don't forget to like and subscribe to the channel. It will really help me out a lot. If you've ever wondered which GLP1 injectable actually gives the most weight loss out of a Zenic, Wgoy, and Waro, check out this video here and I'll see you in the next video.

- Wegovy, Ozempic, or Rybelsus: Which Semaglutide is Right for You?: Kind: captions Language: en-CA Semaglutide is a medication found under 3, and soon to be 4 different brand names, and if you’re planning on using it for weight loss, there are few things you should know about these different brands. Hi, I’m Dr. Brian Yeung. Let’s talk about semaglutide and what you should know before taking it. Semaglutide works by stimulating a hormone called GLP-1. This hormone does a lot of different things in our body, but the main two things we currently use it for are: 1.) To stimulate the release of insulin, and 2.) To make you feel full. Firstly, by stimulating the release of insulin, it allows your body to better use glucose in your bloodstream, and control blood sugar levels. This effect is what makes semaglutide a medication that’s useful for diabetics, or those with poor insulin control. Secondly, by helping to stimulate parts of our brain and digestive system that are related to feeling full, it reduces the desire and appetite to eat. This appetite suppressive effect is what makes semaglutide also a medication that’s useful for weight loss. There are other potentially interesting effects of stimulating GLP-1, such as protecting certain organs from chronic damage and disease, but these are too experimental to be useful at the moment. Right now, there’s a lot of interest in how it can work as an appetite suppressant. Now it’s not the only drug that suppresses appetite by stimulating GLP-1, there are few others out there that have been around for much longer than semaglutide. However, one likely reason for its popularity, is how effective it seems to be for weight loss while being at least somewhat tolerable to take. I’ve actually compared all the different GLP-1 medications in another video before, so if you’re interested I’ll link the video down below. Some of the most common side effects from taking semaglutide are nausea, vomiting, and stool changes like diarrhea and constipation. You see, semaglutide slows down your digestive system, and any food you eat takes longer to digest and break down. The result is that you won’t be able to eat as much or as quickly or else you’re going to get sick. Some people also get headaches or dizziness. Semaglutide has a number of potential serious side effects as well. It might cause thyroid cancers, or cause gallbladder or kidney damage. These types of side effects are pretty rare, however depending on your medical history, you might need to have your thyroid or kidney checked from time to time. There exists multiple brands of semaglutide, all owned by Novo Nordisk, and you can expect that the weight loss approved brands of semaglutide will have more side effects than the diabetes brands, simply due to the increased doses. So Wegovy, one of the weight loss brands of semaglutide, is going to have more side effects than Ozempic, which is one of the diabetes brands of semaglutide. There are currently 3 brands of semaglutide, however a 4th is in the works, and is in the final stages of testing, although it hasn’t been named yet. There are no generics for semaglutide, so there aren’t going to be any other brands, and we likely won’t see any others on the market for a while since the patent on semaglutide won’t expire until around 2032. The drug is exactly the same for all four brands, however, how they’re dosed are all different. Semaglutide is dosed in a step-up fashion. This means that when you start semaglutide, you’ll be given a lower dose at first. Once you’ve taken that dose for a while and you don’t feel too bad, you’ll be given a slightly higher dose. This continues until you hit the maximum dosage. They also have different approved uses. Now this doesn’t necessarily stop people from using it off-label for another purpose, however it does affect insurance coverage and the cost of the drug. They’re all pretty expensive to start with, however when used for weight loss, semaglutide is dosed much higher, and costs much more. The higher cost sometimes drives patients who are trying to obtain semaglutide for weight loss to seek the cheaper alternative, even though it’s technically supposed to be used for diabetes. This presents a problem for patients who are actually using semaglutide for diabetes, as the recent interest in semaglutide for weight loss has created supply shortages of this diabetic medication. Semaglutide can be administered in two different ways: injected once a week under the brand names Wegovy and Ozempic, which is typical of GLP-1 drugs, and as a pill once a day under the brand name Rybelsus, which is pretty unique, since it’s currently the only GLP-1 drug that can be taken as a pill. Studies have shown that both oral and injectable forms are approximately equal in terms of strength, and mostly equal in terms of side effects, with the oral form perhaps being a little more tolerable. There also isn’t really a difference in cost if you’re talking about a monthly or yearly overall cost. However, there are subtle differences based on patient preferences. The pill form requires more vigilant dosing. You need to take it every day, on an empty stomach, with just a little bit of water, not too much, and at least 30 minutes before eating. That’s a lot of instructions, and if you don’t follow them correctly, you could reduce the effectiveness of the pill. The injectable form is only taken once a week, at any time of the day, with or without meals. It’s easier to remember and less affected by whatever else you do, or forget to do. So for some people, injecting provides a more stable and reliable dose of semaglutide. On the other end, the injectables require refrigeration, and you’ll need to deal with needles. So if you’re scared of needles or bruise easily, pills provide a pain-free way to get semaglutide. Regardless of how patients use semaglutide, it is one of our stronger medications to help with weight loss. When used with an ideal dietary and lifestyle plan, patients can expect around a 10-15% weight loss. In my experience, it really depends on how committed the patient is to managing their eating habits, and many patients don’t end up losing that much, maybe closer to 5-10%. However, patients that are willing to implement a stricter dietary plan can lose upwards of 15%. There is no specific diet you need to follow with semaglutide, but by implementing a stricter dietary plan, the reduced appetite makes it easier to follow. You can combine other appetite suppressing strategies such as having more protein and fat or consuming a soluble fiber supplement like glucomannan before your meals to help improve the effects of semaglutide. But avoid extreme diets. Make sure your dietary plan has a good variety of macronutrients and micronutrients, for example by following a low-carbohydrate, calorie controlled Mediterranean Diet. Proper nutrition is needed for proper fat metabolism, and inadvertently depriving yourself of these nutrients can make it harder for your body to metabolize fat, and actually slow your weight loss progress. At the end of the day, if you’re trying to lose weight, you’ll still need to follow a good weight loss diet, however it can make that diet easier to follow. If you deal with emotional and stress eating, you’ll still need behavioral and lifestyle interventions to deal with those, however it can help reduce the food cravings. Semaglutide is just a weight loss medication, not a miracle. It should not, and will not fix your poor eating habits once and for all or manage your emotional eating for you. Like other weight loss medications, the appetite controlling effect of semaglutide will wear off over time, with some patients reporting no more effects on appetite as quickly as within a few months of continual usage. So I would say that semaglutide tends to be most effective when you first use it and hit your maximum dose, and becomes progressively weaker over time. It’s very common for patients who don’t sufficiently develop good eating or living habits to regain weight once they’ve stopped their weight loss medication or once the medication has stopped working for them. Weight loss is a complicated journey, and while I have no problems with using semaglutide as a supportive tool, you may find that you’ll get more long-term benefit from trying to improve your own dietary, behavioral, and lifestyle habits, rather than trying to find new weight loss medication to rely on. Hit the notification bell if you want to stay up to date, and share this video with someone you know can use the help.

  6. @TheEasyDoc: TLDR: Rybelsus is an oral medication for type 2 diabetes that can potentially reduce body weight and may be approved for overweight individuals to prevent diabetes onset or as an add-on medicine for those with diabetes, but it has several potential side effects and warnings that need to be considered.

00:00 💊 Rybelsus is an oral medication for type 2 diabetes, part of the GLP family, potentially reducing body weight by 15-20% and may be approved for overweight individuals to prevent diabetes onset or as an add-on medicine for those with diabetes.
03:55 💊 Take Rybelsus with water before first meal to avoid absorption issues, it regulates insulin, decreases gastric emptying, promotes fullness, and lowers blood sugar without causing hypoglycemia or medication interference.
06:09 📌 Rybelsus can cause weight loss but has warnings for thyroid tumors and requires caution for those with a history of medullary thyroid cancer or multiple endocrine neoplasia syndrome type two, and is not studied in patients with a history of pancreatitis or type 1 diabetes.
08:00 🩸 Rybelsus may worsen diabetic retinopathy and cause pancreatitis, but does not significantly impact incidents in those without a history of retinopathy; reduce certain medication doses, monitor for kidney injury and dehydration, and switch to injectable form if needed.
10:00 🩸 Be cautious when switching from Ozempic to Rybelsus, as it may cause low blood sugar and gastrointestinal side effects, but severe hypoglycemia is rare and the significance of neutralizing antibodies is unknown.
12:38 💊 Women planning to become pregnant should stop taking Rybelsus at least two months before conception, as it may cause harm to a fetus and lead to major birth defects in type 2 diabetic individuals with glycosylated hemoglobin levels below seven percent.
15:14 📌 Rybelsus is not studied in children under 18, may require caution in older patients, and does not need dose modification for renal, liver, or upper gastrointestinal disease, with a long half-life, produced in yeast fermentation, stabilized against degradation, and low absolute bioavailability.
17:57 📉 Rybelsus is a drug that lowers glycosylated hemoglobin and fasting blood sugar, leading to weight loss in patients with inadequately controlled diabetes, but it can delay gastric emptying and change the absorption of oral medicines and is expensive.

- Rybelsus Review: Is Oral GLP-1 Worth It vs Injectables for Weight Loss?: Kind: captions Language: en So, I get a lot of questions about oral GLP-1s and whether or not an oral GLP1 is worth taking. So, I'm Tiffany. I am a physician assistant, but I'm also a GLP1 advocate and I help people get access to GLP1s that maybe don't have access through traditional insurance coverage. So, let's get into oral GLP1s and whether or not that's something that you should invest in. So, the main issue with a GLP-1 is that when you take a GLP1, whether or not you take it through an injection form or through a pill is it's got to be available to your body uh to actually work. And so, we call that bioavailability, meaning that how much of the drug once it gets into your body is actually going to be working. Because GLP1s are peptides, uh it's a little bit problematic for them to be in an oral form because your stomach acid can create a problem with bioavailability. This is true of lots of different hormones. So, it's really kind of hard for people to take a lot of hormones by mouth. So, although GLP-1s do come in an oral form, there is an oral form of semiglutide. Uh it's called rebelsis and it's been out for a while, but the bioavailability of rebels or oral semiglutide is not good. So when people take it by mouth, only about 1% of the drug is actually active after it goes through your stomach. So that's much different than when you inject the drug where a very large portion of the medication is actually active. Also, when you're looking at taking a medicine that's oral in a GLP1 forum, it's really really important to not eat or drink when you take the medicine. And you need to not eat or drink for at least 30 minutes to up to an hour in order to get the best bioavailability. So that also has a lot of restrictions. uh sometimes it's hard for people to follow those rules. So when we look at the studies on rebelsis or oral semiglutide we look in probably about 5 to 10% of uh weight loss uh percentage of weight loss happens with that. So although we do see some definitely not as good as the injectable forms of GLP-1s. So although this is not a horrible option for people these medicines are still not cheap. So um your better bang for your buck is definitely the injectable forms of DLP1s. Now there is a new drug that is coming to market soon. It's called or forg and it is actually a nonpeptide oral medication and so this is kind of exciting because because it's not a peptide the bioavailability is going to be much better. This is currently in phase three clinical trials so not available yet but it look the clinical trials are looking good. It is owned by none other than Eli Liy. So uh to be uh determined this drug is coming out um hopefully sometime next year for diabetes and weight loss. So but right as of right now our oral versions of GLP1s are semiglutide and if you're asking my personal opinion probably not worth the money. Um I have seen some oral versions of tzepatide uh that's been um advertised to be honest with you. I don't really know much about those. That is not something that has been studied. So, if they're making a version of that, then I think that that is not really something I know much about and I don't really want to speak on. So, my personal opinion is if you were going to pay for GLP1, I would personally pay for the injectable version. Um the if you're nervous about doing an injection, the needles are so tiny and it's once a week. साइड इफेक्ट से बच सकते हैं और जिससे कि आपकी दवाइयां भी अच्छे से कंटिन्यू रह पाए आपको उसका मैक्सिमम फायदा भी हो और एट द सेम टाइम नुकसान भी कम हो क्या है वो टिप्स एंड ट्रिक्स उसी के बारे में आज हम बात करेंगे नमस्ते दर्शकों स्वागत है आपका मेरे इस के बारे में उनको बताया जाता है और साथ-साथ में वह साइड इफेक्ट को कम कैसे किया जाए उसके बारे में भी डिस्कशन हुआ रहता है लेकिन जरूरी नहीं है कि जो भी डॉक्टर आपको ट्रीट कर रहे हैं वह शायद से आपको इतना टाइम दे पाए और आपको इसके बारे में डिटेल इंफॉर्मेशन द और इसीलिए और बहुत सी बार डॉक्टर की चाह तो होती है आपको बताने की लेकिन टाइम की पाबंदी के वजह से बहुत सी बार वो रह जाता है तो यह वीडियो का माध्यम इसीलिए है ताकि वह जो गैप है कम्युनिकेशन गैप है उसको हम कम करें और आपको रिबेल्स के बारे में ज्यादा जानकारी दे तो सबसे पहले हम यह समझते हैं कि यह दवा का जो साइड इफेक्ट है जी मचलना और उल्टिया कब ज्यादा होता है दो सिचुएशन में यह प्रॉब्लम ज्यादा पाई जाती है पहला जब यह दवाई नई नई आपके शरीर में शुरू की गई है तब आप ये दवा कभी नहीं रहे थे अचानक से दवाई आई है तो नई शुरुआत हुई है तब यह प्रॉब्लम आता है दूसरा प्रॉब्लम तब आता है जब डॉक्टर ने आपकी दवा का खुराक बढ़ाया है जब डोज को बढ़ाते हैं तभी भी बहुत से मरीजों को यह प्रॉब्लम्स आते हैं तो यह दो सिचुएशन में यह प्रॉब्लम का आना सबसे ज्यादा कॉमन होता है और यह जो प्रॉब्लम जो है ना यह भी कुछ दिनों के लिए ही रहता है यह जो प्रॉब्लम है ये जो साइड इफेक्ट है ये लाइफ फलंग का नहीं है तो आप डिस्कस मत होइए यह कुछ दिनों के लिए ही रुकने वाला है धीरे-धीरे जैसे ही वो दवा आपके शरीर में एडजस्ट होती है आपको यह वाले साइड इफेक्ट भी चले जाएंगे और इसीलिए आज के इस वीडियो में मैं जो टिप्स एंड ट्रिक्स बता रही हूं हो सकता है आपको कुछ दिनों के लिए ही व तराज रखनी है धीरे-धीरे आप वो एहतियात से दूर भी हो सकते हैं ओके तो सबसे पहली टिप क्या है वो है आपका दवाई का टाइट्रेस जी हां जैसे कि वीडियो के शुरुआत में मैंने बताया यह दवा तीन खुराक में आज की तारीख में अवेलेबल है 3 मिग्रा 7 मिलीग्राम और 14 मिग्रा तो जब आप ये दवा पहली बार शुरू कर रहे हैं तो आप ये दवा का खुराक सबसे कम से कम क्वांटिटी यानी 3 मिलीग्राम की गोली से शुरू कर सकते हैं ऑफकोर्स जरूरी नहीं है कि यह डोज आप खुद तय करने वाले हैं ये आपके ट्रीटिंग डॉक्टर तय करने वाले हैं ओके ऑफकोर्स आपके डॉक्टर को यह दबा के ये साइड इफेक्ट के बारे में जानकारी है उनको पता है और इसीलिए अक्सर डॉक्टर छोटे डोज से ही शुरुआत करते हैं और जैसे ही छोटे डोज से शुरुआत किया है जैसे ही वो दवा आपके शरीर में एडजस्ट होती है उसके 30 दिन यानी कम से कम एक महीने के बाद में ही हम यह दवा को धीरे-धीरे करके बढ़ाते हैं अगर दवाई बहुत तेजी से इसके डोज को बढ़ा दिया जाए तो यह प्रॉब्लम आ सकता है तो आप आपके ट्रीटिंग डॉक्टर को मिलिए और बात कीजिए कि दवा कैसे आप अप टाइट्रेट करने वाले हैं ठीक है दूसरी पॉइंट इंपॉर्टेंट है आपकी डाइट के ऊपर देखिए यह जो दवा जो है ना यह कार्य कैसे करती है जभी भी कोई भी मरीज सेमा ग्लूटाट की दवा लेता है तो उसकी असर होती है उनके पाचन तंत्र के ऊपर उनका डाइजेशन जो होता है उसके ऊपर दवा लेने के बाद में आपको भूख कम लगेगी और यह स्वाभाविक बात है और यह अक्सर सभी लोगों में होता है और इसीलिए यही एक दवा का मेन मैकेनिज्म ऑफ एक्शन है यह मैं खास क्यों कह रही हूं क्योंकि अक्सर डायबिटीज जब बहुत लंबे वक्त का रहता है ना तो लोगों के दिमाग में कुछ चीजें जो है बहुत स्ट्रांग प्रिंट हो जाती है जैसे कि अगर मुझे डायबिटीज है तो मुझे दिन में कम से कम तीन से पांच बार खाना है दोदो घंटों पर मुझे खाते रहना है मैं नहीं खाऊंगा तो मेरा शुगर डाउन हो जाएगा मुझे इतना तो कम से कम खाना खाना ही है यह सभी बातें और यह सभी पॉइंट मरीजों के और उनके रिलेटिव के दिमाग में बहुत स्ट्रांग छपे हुए रहते हैं जब शुरुआत जब की जाती है रिबेल्स की जैसी दवाइयों से जिससे भूख कम लग जाती है तो लेकिन मरीज को भूख तो कम लगी है लेकिन उनका दिमाग यही कहता है कि मुझे तो पांच बार खाना है मुझे तो हर दो घंटे प खाना है मुझे इतना खाना ही है वरना तो मुझे प्रॉब्लम होगा और जब यह दो चीजें का इंबैलेंस होता है उल्टिया जी उबल बहुत ज्यादा कॉमन हो जाता है इसीलिए आपको यह सब बातों को ख्याल में रखना जरूरी है कि जबी भी आप सेमा ग्लूटाट को लेते हैं तो आपकी भूख कम होने वाली है और इसको आप मेंटली एक्सेप्ट कीजिए ओके आपको कब खाना है खाना तब है जब आपके शरीर में सच में भूख लग रही है आपको आपकी बॉडी के इंडिकेशन को सुनना है अगर आपकी बॉडी कह रही है कि मुझे भूख नहीं लगी है तो आपको उस वक्त प खाना नहीं खाना है भले ही दोपहर का 1 बज गया लंच टाइम हो चुका है आपको घड़ी के हिसाब से नहीं खाना है आपको आपकी बॉडी के इंडिकेशन के हिसाब से खाना है अक्सर लोगों की भूख कम लग जाती है खाना तभी खाइए जब आपको भूख लगी हो ओके लेकिन इसके साथ-साथ में आपको एक और इंपॉर्टेंट पॉइंट मैं कहना चाहती हूं बहुत से मरीजों को डायबिटीज को ठीक करने के लिए इंसुलिन है या तो फिर हाइपोग्लाइसेमिक जैसी दवाइयां जैसे कि ग्लिम पराइड ग्लिकलाज इड ऐसी गोलियां दी जाती है ओके जो दवाइया अपने आप में लो शुगर कर सकती है तो प्लीज आप आपके ट्रीटिंग डॉक्टर को पूछिए कि यह दवाइयों के साथ में आपको डाइट में कैसे चेंजेज करना है क्योंकि अगर आपने खाना नहीं खाया और ऐसे हाइपो ग्लामिक दवाइयां ले रहे हैं तो हो सकता है आपका शुगर डाउन जाए और आपको प्रॉब्लम लेके आए और इसीलिए इंडिविजुअल केस टू केस आप आपके ट्रीटिंग डॉक्टर से भी बात कर सकते हैं अगर आप सिर्फ और सिर्फ सेमा ग्लूटा इड वाली दवा ले रहे हैं तो फिर आपको आपके डाइट में मेजर चेंजेज करने जरूरी हो जाएंगे ओके तो खाना कब खाना है जब शरीर में सच में भूख लगे तब ना कि घड़ी में टाइम हो गया है लंच टाइम का तो तब ठीक है दूसरा जैसे ही आप खाना खाएंगे आपकी भूख कम हुई है तो समझिए कि एग्जांपल के तौर पर आप रोज तीन रोटी खा रहे हैं ओके क्योंकि आपने सेमा ग्लूटाट लिया है तो आपकी भूख कम हो गई आपने एक रोटी खाई और पेट भर गया तो जैसे ही आपकी बॉडी ने आपको बताया कि पेट भर चुका है तो उस वक्त प आप रुक भी जाइए जबरदस्ती अगर आप ज्यादा खाएंगे ओवरईट करेंगे तो जी मचलना और उल्टिया ज्यादा कॉमन होती है ओके यानी आपको खाना तब है जब बॉडी मांग रही है तब खाना रुक भी तब जाना है जब आपकी बॉडी इंडिकेट कर रही है तब लोगों को लगता है तीन रोटी मैं रोज खाता हूं आज नहीं खाऊंगा तो मेरा शुगर डाउन हो जाएगा ऐसे डिफेंसिव ईटिंग से भी आपको बचना है ओके तो खाने पे आपको ज्यादा ध्यान देना जरूरी है सेकंड जब आप खाना खाते हैं तो खाने को अच्छे से चू करना है चबाना है जितना आप खाना अच्छे से चबाए उतना ही उसका का डाइजेशन ठीक होगा और क्योंकि सेमा ग्लूटाट डाइजेस्टिव सिस्टम पर एक्ट करती है इसीलिए जितना अच्छे से चबा के खाएंगे आपका वोमिटिंग और नोजिया सेंसेशन कम हो जाता है ओके नेक्स्ट क्या खाना खाना है ये भी बहुत मायने रखता है जितना हो सके आप स्टीम किया है उबाला हुआ है ग्रिल किया हुआ है ओवन में पकाया हुआ खाना है ऐसा खाना खाइए ओके तला हुआ बहुत ज्यादा घी बटर चीज मक्खन डाला हुआ हां ऐसे ग्रीसी फूड से आपको बचना है आजकल लोग सैलेड खाते हैं लेकिन उसमें भी बहुत ज्यादा मेयोनेज़ डाला हुआ सैलेड ड्रेसिंग खाते हैं ऐसे चीजी ग्रीसी खाओगे तो नोजिया वोमिटिंग ज्यादा होता है जितना हो सके सिंपल खाना आपको खाना है ओके सेकंड डे जब आप खाना खा रहे हैं तो घर पे बनाया हुआ घर का पकाया हुआ खाना ज्यादा डाइजेशन सिस्टम के लिए इ होता है वैसा खाना खाए बाजार में से मिला हुआ रेडी टू ईट है प्रोसेस्ड फूड है हां या तो रेस्टोरेंट में बना हुआ बहुत ज्यादा तेल चीज बटर मक्खन से डाला हुआ बहुत ज्यादा स्पाइसी खाना खाओगे तो आपको नोजिया वोमिटिंग की शिकायत ज्यादा होती है ओके नेक्स्ट आपकी बॉडी पोजीशन कैसी है खाने के बाद में वो भी मैटर करती है बहुत सी बार लोगों को आदत होती है खाना खाने के बाद में कुछ आधा एक घंटा लेट जाए सो जाए ओके है अगर आप ऐसा करेंगे तो आपको नोजिया वोमिटिंग ज्यादा हो सकती है क्योंकि हमारी जैसे बॉडी पोजीशन चेंज होती है ना वैसे हमारा स्टमक और हमारा जो सोफे गस है जो खाने की जो नली है उसके बीच में एक वाल्व आता है वो वाल्व की पोजीशन चेंज हो जाती है अगर आप लेट जाएंगे तोय वाल्व की पोजीशन चेंज होगी और ज्यादा करके लोगों को लगता है खाना ऊपर आ रहा है और इसीलिए खाने के बाद में इमीडिएट आप लेटने से बचे उतना ही नहीं बहुत लोगों को आदत होती है खाना खाने के बाद में 10-15 मिनट ब्रिस्क वॉक करें बहुत सी बार कुछ लोगों को ऐसे ब्रिस्क वॉक करने के बाद में भी खाना ऊपर आता है ऐसा महसूस होता है तो इससे भी बचे तो खाना अगर आपने खाया है रुकिए थोड़ा वक्त उसके बाद में आप वॉक कर सकते हैं तुरंत खाना खाने के बाद में आप वॉकिंग से बचे ओके डेफिनेटली मैं ये नहीं कह रही हूं कि आपको वॉकिंग नहीं करनी है वॉकिंग करना है लेकिन टाइमिंग ऑफ द वॉकिंग पे आपको ध्यान देना जरूरी है ओके सेकंड बहुत सी बार लोग जब भी खाना खाने के टाइम पर लिक्विड का इस्तेमाल करते हैं तो लिक्विड में भी आपको कुछ बातों का ध्यान देना है ओके जब लिक्विड पी रहे है तो जितना हो सके बहुत ज्यादा स्ट्रांग स्पाइसे डाला हुआ लिक्विड से आप बचे लिक्विड पीने के वक्त पे आपको बहुत ज्यादा स्ट्रॉ काम उपयोग नहीं करना है उतना ही नहीं हाइड्रेशन के लिए एडिक्ट हाइड्रेशन के लिए आप क्लियर वेजिटेबल सूप है अगर आप नॉनवेज खा आते तो नॉन वेजिटेरियन सूप आप ले सकते हैं ज्यादा तले ज्यादा शक्कर डाले हुए कोल्ड ड्रिंक है ओके या तो फिर एनर्जी ड्रिंक्स है उससे आप बचे उससे प्रॉब्लम और बढ़ती है ओके तो इन चीजों को आपको ध्यान देना है सेकंड बहुत बार लोगों को आदत होती है रात को सोने के पहले बेड टाइम स्नैक खाते हैं तो बहुत बार बेड टाइम स्नैक के बाद में भी लोगों को नोजिया वोमिटिंग ज्यादा होती है तो उससे भी आप बच सकते हैं ओके सेकंड क्योंकि जी मचलना है यह जरूरी है कि खाने से ही हो यह खाने की खुशबू से भी हो सकता है खाने खाना देखने से भी हो सकता है और इसीलिए बहुत ज्यादा जहां प खुशबू आ रही है जैसे कि किचन में तड़का लग रहा है ओके बहुत ज्यादा लहसुन प्याज का छौ का लगा है ऐसे स्ट्रांग स्मेल से भी बहुत लोगों को नोजिया वोमिटिंग ट्रिगर होती तो अगर आपको ऐसा हो रहा है तो उसे थोड़े दिनों के लिए बचे ओके तो ये सब आप ट्राई कर सकते हैं प्रॉब्लम से बचने के लिए ऑलराइट अगर आपको वोमिटिंग हो रही है तो क्या करेंगे डेफिनेटली देखे कि वोमिटिंग कितनी सीवियर है उस हिसाब से आप आपके ट्रीटिंग डॉक्टर से बात कीजिए अगर वोमिटिंग हो रही है तो बॉडी में फ्लूइड का कम हो जाना कॉमन सी बात है वैसे केसेस में फ्लूइड को रिप्लेस कीजिए ज्यादा से ज्यादा मात्रा में पानी पिए क्लियर अ लेमन वाटर है वैसा पी सकते हैं सूप ले सकते हैं ओके जितना आपका बॉडी लिक्विड थ्रो गलो आउट कर रहा है उतना आप इलेक्ट्रोलाइट भरा हुआ रिप्लेसमेंट करें वरना तो आगे चलके यह आपको डिहाइड्रेशन और किडनी पर भी असर ला सकता है ओके सेकंड अगर नोजिया फीलिंग आती है तो बहुत लोगों को कुछ टिप्स है वो ज्यादा हेल्प करती है मुंह में आप छोटा सा इलायची है लौंग का या तो फिर आप अदरक का टुकड़ा रख सकते हैं बहुत लोगों को ये फायदा करता है कुछ-कुछ लोग अपने मुंह में तुलसी का पत्ता है या तो फिर पुदीने का पत्ता रखते हैं वो भी उनको काफी हेल्प करता है तो देखिए आपको कुछ ऐसे आपको कोई यूनिक फूड प्रोडक्ट अगर आपने मुंह में रख दिया कुछ वक्त के लिए तो आपको नोजिया अगर कम हो रही है तो बहुत अच्छी बात है और अगर आपके खुद के कुछ ऐसे टिप है जो आपने खुद ने इस्तेमाल किए और आपको अगर उसका फायदा हुआ है तो नीचे कमेंट बॉक्स है उसमें लिखिए क्योंकि बहुत लोग ये कमेंट्स पढ़ते हैं उनको भी आपकी ये टिप से फायदा हो सकता है और उनका भी भला हो जाएगा तो आप डेफिनेटली लिखेगा ओके तो ये कुछ टिप्स एंड ट्रिक्स है जो अगर आप बराबर से एति आद रखेंगे तो रिबेल्स और सेमा ग्लूटाट से आने वाले यह साइड इफेक्ट से आप बच सकते हैं ओके डेफिनेटली दवा का यह साइड इफेक्ट बहुत कॉमन है तकरीबन सब लोगों को होता है अकेले आप ही को हो रहा है ऐसा नहीं है तो प्लीज शुरुआत के स्टेज में थोड़ी धीरज रखिए पेशेंस रखिए ताकि दवा आपके शरीर में एडजस्ट हो जाए और धीरे-धीरे दवा एडजस्ट हो ही जाती है और ये जो साइड इफेक्ट है ये भी निकल जाते हैं ये साइड इफेक्ट फैक्ट जैसे कि वीडियो के शुरू में मैंने बताया दो ही सिचुएशन में ज्यादा होते शुरुआत के स्टेज में और जब डोज को बढ़ाते हैं तब और कुछ दिनों में ये ऑटोमेटिक एडजस्ट हो जाता है दवा बंद करने की अक्सर जरूरत नहीं पड़ती है ऑफकोर्स कुछ केसेस में हो सकता है लेकिन तकरीबन ये वेल टॉलरेटेड दवाई है बहुत ज्यादा किफायती है बहुत ज्यादा इफेक्टिव है डायबिटीज पे तो अच्छी असर लाता ही है साथ-साथ में बॉडी के वजन पे भी इसका अच्छा इंपैक्ट पाया है तो दवा कतई मत कीजिएगा अपने डॉक्टर को पूछे बगैर ऑलराइट तो ये थे रिबेल्स और सिमा ग्लूटाट के कुछ इंपॉर्टेंट टिप्स ओके अगर आप यह दवा पहली बार ले रहे हैं तो प्लीज आपको यह दवा के बारे में विशेष और डिटेल में जानकारी होना जरूरी है उसके लिए मैंने एक अलग वीडियो बनाया है आप चाहे तो उस वीडियो को देखिएगा सेकंड मैंने ये टॉपिक पे और एक वीडियो बनाया है क्योंकि अक्सर बहुत से लोग जिनको डायबिटीज होता है उनको थायरॉयड की समस्या भी होती है और थायराइड की दवाइयां लेते हैं थायराइड की दवाइयां और सेमगलूटाइड का बहुत क्लोज इंटरेक्शन है और दवाइयों के टाइमिंग में कुछ अंतर रखना जरूरी है कैसे यह दवा दोनों अगर आप ले रहे हैं तो ध्यान रखना चाहिए उसके ऊपर भी मैंने अलग वीडियो बनाया है आप चाहे तो उस वीडियो को भी देखिएगा ऑलराइट तो यह सब वीडियोस आपको जागृत करेंगे आपकी सेहत को और अच्छा रखने के लिए अगर - Rybelsus (Semaglutide) for Type 2 Diabetes: Oral GLP-1 Benefits & Side Effects: Kind: captions Language: en Rebelsis semiglutide uses, benefits, and how it works. Rebelsis, semiglutide, is an FDA approved prescription medication designed to help adults with type 2 diabetes manage their blood sugar levels more effectively, belonging to a class of drugs known as GLP 1 receptor agonists. Rebelsis semiglutide mimics the action of a natural hormone in the body that helps regulate blood sugar. Unlike other GLP1 medications that require injections, rebelsis is the first oral form of semiglutide taken by mouth once daily. It should be taken on an empty stomach at least 30 minutes before eating, drinking, or taking any other oral medications. Rebels is used alongside a healthy diet and regular exercise to improve blood sugar control. In addition to managing blood glucose, rebelsis semiglatide is also FDA approved to lower the risk of major cardiovascular events including heart attack, stroke and heartrelated death in adults with type 2 diabetes who have existing heart disease or are at high risk. What is rebelsis semiglutide used for? To improve blood sugar control in adults with type 2 diabetes to reduce the risk of heart attack, stroke or heart-related death in people with type 2 diabetes and established heart disease or cardiovascular risk. How rebelsis semiglutide works? Rebelsis contains semiglutide a glucagon-l like peptide 1 GLP1 receptor agonist. This medication works by increasing insulin release from the pancreas when blood sugar levels are high, reducing glucose production by the liver, slowing down digestion, which helps prevent spikes in blood sugar after meals. These combined effects help stabilize blood sugar levels throughout the day. Additionally, rebelsis semiglutide can support weight loss, lower blood pressure, and reduce inflammation, contributing to improved heart and kidney health over time. Rebelsis semiglutide side effects. What you need to know like all prescription medications, rebelsis, semiglutide may cause side effects. Many of these are mild and tend to improve over time as your body adjusts to the medication. However, some side effects can be serious and require immediate medical attention. Always discuss any new or worsening symptoms with your health care provider. Common side effects of rebelsis, semiglutide. The most frequently reported side effects of rebelsis semiglutide include nausea 11 to 20%, stomach pain or discomfort 10 to 11%, diarrhea 9 to 10%, reduced appetite 6 to 9%, vomiting 6 to 8%. Constipation 5 to 6%. These side effects are generally mild and temporary. Taking rebels as prescribed and gradually adjusting your diet may help minimize stomach related symptoms. Other possible side effects. Some people taking rebelsis semiglutide may also experience bloating or fullness, indigestion or heartburn, burping or passing gas, acid reflux, dizziness, hair loss, faster than normal heartbeat. If these symptoms persist or become bothersome, consult your health care provider. Serious side effects. When to seek medical help. Although rare, serious side effects can occur while taking Rebelsis semiglutide. Contact your health care provider immediately if you experience any of the following. Possible thyroid tumors. Lump or swelling in the neck. Trouble swallowing. Horarseness or difficulty breathing. Vision changes. Diabetic retinopathy, blurred vision, vision loss, seeing dark spots or changes in color perception. Pancreatitis, inflammation of the pancreas, severe and persistent abdominal or back pain, with or without vomiting, low blood sugar, hypoglycemia, shaking, sweating, dizziness, confusion, or blurred vision, especially if used with other diabetes medications, kidney problems, changes in urination, swelling in the feet or ankles, fatigue or nausea, severe stomach issues, persistent nausea, vomiting, diarrhea or bloating, Gallbladder disease, pain in the upper right abdomen, fever, yellowing of the skin or eyes, jaundice or pale stools, serious allergic reactions, difficulty breathing, rash, hives, swelling of the face or throat or rapid heartbeat. Rebelsis semiglutide dosage forms and typical dosing. Rebelsis semiglatide is an oral medication used to help adults with type 2 diabetes improve blood sugar control and lower the risk of heart related complications. It's available in several tablet strengths to allow for gradual dose adjustments based on individual needs and tolerability. Typical dosing schedule for rebelsis semiglutide. Rebelsis semiglutide is available in two formulations R1 and R2. Although both contain semiglatide, they are not interchangeable and your health care provider will determine which version is appropriate for you. R1 formulation starting dose 3 mg by mouth once daily for the first 30 days. Maintenance dose increase to 7 mg once daily after 30 days. Maximum dose if needed 14 mg once daily. R2 formulation starting dose 1.5 milligrams by mouth once daily for the first 30 days maintenance dose increase to 4 milligrams once daily after 30 days maximum dose if needed 9 mg once daily how to take rebelsis semiglutide take rebels first thing in the morning on an empty stomach swallow the tablet hole with no more than half a cup of water about 4 ounces wait at least 30 minutes other oral medications. Following these instructions helps ensure your body absorbs the medication properly for maximum effectiveness. Rebelsis, semiglutide, contraindications. Certain health conditions make rebelsis semiglutide unsafe to use. Always tell your health care provider if any of the following apply to you. Personal or family history of metalary thyroid carcinoma MTC. Personal history of multiple endocrine neoplasia syndrome type 2. Men too. People with these conditions should not take rebelsis as the medication may increase the risk of thyroid tumors. Pharmacist tips for rebelsis. Semaglutide. When taking rebelsis semiglatide, following proper instructions is key to getting the most benefit and minimizing side effects. Here are essential pharmacist recommended tips to help you use rebelsis safely and effectively. How to take rebelsis semiglutide. Take rebelsis semiglutide first thing in the morning right after you wake up and before you eat or drink anything. Swallow the tablet whole with a small amount of plain water. No more than half a cup, around 4 ounces. Do not use other liquids such as coffee, juice, or milk as they can interfere with how your body absorbs the medication. Wait at least 30 minutes before eating, drinking, or taking any other oral medications. This allows rebelsis to be properly absorbed. Swallow the tablet hole. Do not crush, chew, or split it as this can affect the dose and reduce how well the medication works. Missed dose instructions. If you forget to take your dose, skip it and take your next dose at the usual time the following day. Never double your dose to make up for a missed tablet. Lifestyle support for best results. Rebelsis semiglutide works best when used as part of a comprehensive diabetes management plan that includes a balanced nutrient-rich diet, regular physical activity, routine blood sugar monitoring. Ask your health care team for personalized guidance on diet and exercise to support your treatment goals. Managing common side effects. It's normal to experience mild stomach related side effects such as nausea, vomiting, stomach pain, or diarrhea, especially when starting rebelsis or increasing the dose. These effects usually improve as your body adjusts. If they become severe or persistent, talk to your health care provider. Important safety warnings. Pancreatitis, inflammation of the pancreas. Stop taking Rebelsis and contact your health care provider immediately if you develop severe stomach pain. that spreads to your back with or without vomiting, low blood sugar, hypoglycemia. This can occur when rebelsis is taken with other diabetes medications such as insulin or sulfanyl uras. Watch for symptoms like shaking, sweating, dizziness, confusion, or blurred vision. Pregnancy and breastfeeding. If you plan to become pregnant, stop rebelsis at least 2 months before trying to conceive. Do not breastfeed while taking this medication as it can pass into breast milk and its effects on infants are not fully known. By taking Rebelsis semiglatide correctly and following your health care provers advice, you can achieve better blood sugar control, reduce cardiovascular risk, and support your long-term health goals. - Endocrinologist shares TOP TIPS to reduce Nausea on Ozempic, Wegovy, Mounjaro, Saxenda and Rybelsus: Kind: captions Language: en-CA There’s plenty of new medications now&nbsp; available for the management of diabetes&nbsp;&nbsp; and/or obesity. Some of these medications might&nbsp; cause nausea/vomiting or stomach upset. In today’s&nbsp;&nbsp; video I will teach you top hacks that I share&nbsp; with my patient to reduce these side effects. &nbsp; Medications that can typically cause nausea&nbsp; include semaglutide- trade name Ozempic or&nbsp;&nbsp; Rybelsus, dulaglutide- trade name Trulicity&nbsp; or liraglutide- trade name Victoza or Saxenda.&nbsp;&nbsp; These agents work by promoting your pancreas to&nbsp; produce insulin. They also slow down the transit&nbsp;&nbsp; time of food through the stomach and work on the&nbsp; hunger centre in the brain, making you feel full.&nbsp;&nbsp; If you’re experiencing nausea/ vomiting&nbsp; or stomach upset on these medications,&nbsp;&nbsp; here are 5 tips that you can try: 1. Start low and go slow: The side effects&nbsp;&nbsp; typically occur depending on the dose. Start the&nbsp; lowest possible dose of the medication and follow&nbsp;&nbsp; your doctor’s instructions on how to increase&nbsp; the dose. If you experience side effects when you&nbsp;&nbsp; move up to a higher dose, go back on the lower&nbsp; dose and stay on it for a little while longer&nbsp;&nbsp; before attempting to increase the dose again 2. Smaller and more frequent meals:&nbsp;&nbsp; Instead of 3 big meals a day, having smaller and&nbsp; more frequent meals while on these medications&nbsp;&nbsp; can significantly lower the risk of nausea.&nbsp; These medications reboot your hunger centre&nbsp;&nbsp; and you might find yourself feeling full&nbsp; even before you’ve consumed the portion sizes&nbsp;&nbsp; that you were previously accustomed to. 3. Listen to your body: These medications&nbsp;&nbsp; may make you feel full for many hours&nbsp; altogether. Listen to your body when on&nbsp;&nbsp; these medications and eat only when you’re hungry 4. Cut out the grease: Fried foods can increase&nbsp;&nbsp; nausea significantly. Limiting the amount of&nbsp; greasy, fatty foods can help overcome the nausea 5. This too shall pass: Perhaps the&nbsp; most reassuring aspect is that for an&nbsp;&nbsp; overwhelming majority of patients, even if&nbsp; they do get the side effects, these tend&nbsp;&nbsp; to decrease in intensity and frequency as&nbsp; time passes and can resolve completely. Try&nbsp;&nbsp; to see if you’re able to stick out with the&nbsp; side effects, which will improve with time. Bonus tip: Certain medications&nbsp;&nbsp; have injection pens that can be “micro titrated”&nbsp; For instance, if you are on Ozempic 0.25 mg/week&nbsp;&nbsp; without nausea but get nausea on the higher&nbsp; dose of 0.5 mg/week, you can try to increase&nbsp;&nbsp; the dose gradually. There are 20 microclicks&nbsp; that the pen turns between 0.25 and 0.50 mg.&nbsp;&nbsp; Try to increase by increments&nbsp; of 5 microclicks each week,&nbsp;&nbsp; which helps the body get gradually&nbsp; accustomed to the increasing dose. So these were my top tips for tolerating&nbsp; nausea/vomiting or stomach upset on diabetes or&nbsp;&nbsp; obesity medications. Don’t forget to like, share&nbsp; and subscribe to my channel for more such tips

- Pharmacist Explains - Rybelsus (the tablet version of Ozempic): Kind: captions Language: en is this the future of oamic not this exactly this is just a random tablet but at this stage we all know medications available and it is called pelsis that is the brand name for it and when I did some investigation I have found that there are many more medications in the pipeline currently undergoing the tablet version of OIC that is currently available for treating type 2 [Music] diabetes hello everyone it's carony from long game Health welcome back to another week on the channel if you're new here I put out a new video every Friday that can help educate you on Health in some ways so subscribe and check out my other videos while you're at it so Rellis came onto the scene in 2019 when it was approved for medical use in the United States and the thing to know here is that rib elsis is the brand name the medication that it contains is called semaglutide which is the exact same medication that is in OIC and also Al wovi so all three same medication OIC and wovi are injections and Rib elsis is a tablet so in theory much easier for someone to take so semaglutide it belongs to a class of medications called glp1 analoges which stands for glucagon like peptide 1 analoges and these medications are synthetic versions of a hormone that is naturally found in your body called glucagonlike peptide 1 now your body can make this hormone but in some situations like people with type 2 diabetes this hormone becomes less efficient so it's a hormone that plays a role in blood sugar control because when you eat food it signals for your body to make and release insulin into the bloodstream which leads to reduce blood sugar levels go watch my video on blood sugar control for more information on how exactly insulin works now increasing insulin levels is is great for people with type 2 diabetes because their blood sugar levels are high now another thing that these medications do is slow down the rate of stomach emptying when you eat food your stomach will periodically empty its contents into the intestines which is where all your nutrients get absorbed usually so when you slow down stomach emptying nutrients like glucose will enter the bloodstream at a slower rate and this is good because it can prevent blood sugar spikes after you eat now if you have type 2 diabetes you definitely want to avoid blood sugar spikes because these cause damage to blood vessels and nerves if they keep happening stomach emptying slowing down also means that food will stay in the stomach for longer and this is one of the reasons that weight loss is actually associated with these medications as well so that's how rib elsis will work by mimicking the effects of this glp1 hormone now one thing you may be wondering is why most of these medications are injections and not just tablets already well hormones they're a type of protein and unfortunately all proteins do not do well in acidic environments which is an issue if you need something to go through the stomach your stomach acid is extremely acidic and there are also enzymes in the stomach that break down protein to help digestion so both of these things essentially destroy semaglutide or any of the other glp1 analog medications which makes it very hard to have a tablet version of the medication because it has to go through the stomach so researchers have had to spend a lot of time figuring out how to get around this issue and they seem to have found a solution the ralis tablets contain semaglutide but they also have something called snack which stands for this lovely chemical name that I'm not going to bother saying but essentially when these tablets get to your stomach and meet your stomach acid the tablet will break AP heart and the snack that is inside the tablet will actually for a short period of time make your stomach acid less acidic which will protect the semaglutide from being destroyed and this allows the medication to get absorbed into your blood yay for science now this is an amazing development but it's not perfect yet and you will find that the rib elsis tablets need some specific conditions when you are taking them so for example they need to be taken on an empty stomach they need to be taken with at least 120 Ms of water because any less and the tablet actually doesn't break down enough to release that special snack component and finally you can't consume anything not water food or even other medications for at least half an hour after taking the medication because the researchers have found that these all reduce absorption of the medication by up to 34% so it's a bit of a picky medication but some people may prefer these to taking an injection so the next question to answer then would be is the tablet form just as good as the injection and well thankfully there have been some clinical trials for me to look through there was a series of Trials called the Pioneer studies and they compared how effective the tablet form of OIC was compared to Placebo which is essentially no medication treatment they also compared it to other tablet-based medications for type 2 diabetes and other injectable forms of glp1 analoges and I've also linked these studies in the description as well but to summarize what these trials found was that the tablet version of OIC seemed to be more effective than some of the existing tablet-based treatments for type 2 diabetes at reducing blood sugar levels and improving blood sugar control when compared to Placebo it had very good improvements to blood sugar control and also weight loss in patients with type 2 diabetes and when compared to other injectable glp1 analoges it was deemed not inferior which essentially means the tablet isn't exactly more effective or better than the injectable versions but they also aren't much worse so they seem to be giving similar effects at the moment which I think is a good thing now in terms of side effects though the biggest issue with these medications has been the nausea and unfortunately the tablets don't seem to give any Improvement on that the trials found similar side effects comparing the tablet to the injection based versions of other glp1 analoges and it was actually discovered that the nausea for the tablets hung around a bit longer when compared to injections and it is significant nausea it's enough for people to drop out of the trials now at this stage the clinical trials have been done only on patients with type 2 diabetes and while they did show weight loss Effectiveness it was only in this specific subset of patients which means at this stage there isn't enough evidence for use of the medication outside of type 2 diabetes to be approved but it does seem that there are lots of Trials currently going on and not just with the tablet form of OIC but other new medications too that would also be tablets so I do think it's just a matter of time before we see these start to enter the market and they'll probably flood the market eventually because obviously they would be very very profitable now at this stage it's a great potential therapy for people with type 2 diabetes who can't manage their condition with diet and lifestyle changes and where other medications have failed or people who would just really rather not have the injections now in terms of using this for weight loss it's not approved yet so you probably won't have access until more research and Trials come out even then I am a bit worried about a huge amount of people running to these medications as a Magic Bullet for weight loss when in reality this should be taken alongside proper nutrition and physical activity to make make sure that you don't lose too much muscle it's
