- Mounjaro (tirzepatide): The newest diabetes and obesity medication (explanation and overview): Kind: captions Language: en [Music] this is the newest injectable medication for the treatment of diabetes and obesity and it joins a rapidly advancing class of obesity medications that target specific hormonal receptors in the body these medications are revolutionizing the medical treatment of obesity they are markedly more effective than prior medications in fact terzepatite represents the most effective obesity medication ever developed note that at the time of filming this video terzepatite is not yet fda approved for the treatment of obesity it is approved for the treatment of diabetes and we anticipate fda approval for obesity in the coming months so let's briefly review the current fda approved weight loss medications just to establish a framework for this discussion so phentermine which has been available since the 1950s leads to an average weight loss of about five percent of body weight if you combine phentermine with topiramate which is a migraine medication that makes a brand name kusemia that combination can produce an average of around eight to ten percent weight loss now contrave which is a combination of bupropion an antidepressant and naltrexone an addiction prevention medication produces around four to five percent weight loss sexenda which was the first injectable weight loss medication was approved back in 2014 and this leads to an average weight loss of about eight percent of body weight now the next medication that was approved by the fda for the treatment of obesity was a full seven years later and that was we govi which is cemagletide 2.4 milligrams and this was improved in 2021 and was a major leap in efficacy producing an average weight loss of about 15 percent at one year and that's with a once weekly injection so that's nearly double the weight loss that we saw with saxenda now we go the targets and boosts a hormone known as glucagon like peptide 1 or glp1 this is a critical hormone that controls weight through the brain and through the digestive tract so higher levels of glp1 lead to less appetite a prolonged sense of satiety uh and ultimately weight loss these medications also help to manage glucose levels and that's why they're also effective for the treatment of diabetes so how does terzepatite work like cemaglotide it also targets the glp1 receptor to boost glp levels but it also targets a second receptor known as gip the actions of glp1 and gip are synergistic so they actually boost the effect of each other so terzepatide combines the actions of glp1 and gip receptors leading to greater overall so what is the average weight loss with terzepatide the recently published surmount trial established the efficacy of trizepative for weight loss this was a phase three randomized controlled study of 2539 people and they were randomized to receive either terzepatide or placebo furthermore terzepatide was evaluated at three different strengths five milligrams 10 milligrams and 15 milligrams so after 72 weeks of treatment the following results were seen with placebo the average weight loss was three percent it's about what we'd expect with just kind of diet and exercise with five milligrams of terzepa tide the average weight loss was 15 percent with 10 milligrams of transeptide that weight loss increased to 19 percent and at 15 milligrams the average weight loss was a remarkable 20.9 percent as a reminder with we govi the previous weight loss leader the average weight loss was 15 percent so how is trezepatide taken terzepatite is taken as a once weekly injection the medication comes supplied in a pre-filled pen there's no visible needle and patients use the medication once per week patients taking terzepatide can experience some side effects in fact the most common side effects are gastrointestinal in nature this is very similar to the side effects seen with cemagletide they include nausea diarrhea and constipation most of these are mild and transient occurring during that dose escalation period in that same surmount trial that i mentioned up to seven percent of patients actually needed to stop their treatment due to these side effects other very rare side effects could include pancreatitis gallbladder disease though these were actually no more frequent with terzepaty than with placebo in that same trial so who is a good candidate for a terzepatide once trazepatite is fda approved for the treatment of obesity a good candidate would be an adult with a body mass index of greater than 30 or greater than 27 with an obesity related medical condition such as diabetes high blood pressure high cholesterol or sleep apnea candidates for this medication must be willing and comfortable to use an injectable medication on a weekly basis this is critically important you must be willing to use this long term obesity is a chronic and relapsing condition and we must treat it as such so this is not a medication that you should take for a short time and then stop once you start it you really want to remain on it assuming it's working for you and you're tolerating it well patients with obesity and diabetes may ultimately be the best candidates for terzepatite because it targets both of these conditions and may allow patients to wean off of other diabetes medications such as insulin which actually promote weight gain terzepatite is unquestionably a breakthrough an average weight loss of 20 or more is remarkable and significantly higher than any prior medication we know that health conditions such as diabetes high blood pressure high cholesterol begin to improve and even resolve when someone loses 10 percent of their body weight so willpower rather than a disease and that is blatantly wrong obesity is a disease and must be treated as such so what type of support is needed for patients who are using terzepatite trazepatite is best provided by a comprehensive weight loss facility this means a physician or medical provider who is obesity medicine trained and skilled at managing obesity and its related conditions further - How to Use Mounjaro (Tirzepatide) UK injection Pen - Step by Step Guide: Kind: captions Language: en hi everyone I'm Melissa from London slimming Clinic I'm going to show you how to correctly use the monjaro pen monjaro is a once weekly injection which contains tepati and is prescribed for weight loss when jaro is delivered in chilled and insulated packaging to maintain the correct temperature during transport each pack of Monaro contains an instruction leaplet patient information Lether which you should read before first use and the Monaro pen itself comp ible needles will also be supplied the mararo pen contains four fixed doses one dose taken weekly therefore one needle is to be used each week check the expiry date to ensure the medication is within date remove the pen cap to check the liquid mojarro should be colorless to slightly yellow it's important to note that the medication is contained here in the pen and not in the needles next you'll need to attach a new needle wipe the red inner seal of the pen with a swab pull off the paper tab then push the cap needle straight onto the pen and twist the pen needle on until it's tied remember to always use a new needle every time you take the medication to help prevent infections and block needles each time before you use the pen you'll need to prime it check that the pen is functioning correctly pull off the outer needle shield and keep it this will we reuse later when removing the needle pull off the inner needle shield and discard it slowly turn the dose knob until you hear two clicks and the extended line is shown in the dose window this is the prime position hold your pen with a needle pointing up now push the dose knob in until it stops the zero must be shown in the dose window you should see a drop of liquid on the tip of the needle if there is no liquid then repeat the step to select the dose turn the dose knob until it stops and the one icon is shown in the dose window the one icon is equal to a full dose you are taking in this case it's 2.5 mg monjaro can be injected into either the abdomen the upper thigh or the upper arm do change and mix up your injection sites each week to allow the skin to heal and avoid any potential soreness or bruising insert the needle at a 90° angle into the skin press and hold down the dose button either with your finger or your thumb and the dose counter returns to zero then count to six and remove the needle you may see a drop of liquid at the end of the needle and that is normal and nothing to be concerned about put the outer needle cap which you saved earlier back on the needle and unscrew it from the pen dispose of needle in the sharp container do not put these in your normal household waist bin once you start using the pen you'll notice that a plunger begins to appear this is normal and it just indicates how much liquid is remaining once the plunger reaches the end the pen is empty and you've used up all four doses put the cap back onto the pen and store it away until the next use the used pen can either be stored in the refrigerator 2 to 8° or unrefrigerated for up to 30 days as long as the temperature does not go above 30° Centigrade if kept unrefrigerated the pens must be discarded after 30 days and that is it that's all there is to it if you have any questions or concerns don't hesitate to reach out to us at the London slimming - 12 Things You Should NEVER Mix with Ozempic or Mounjaro: Kind: captions Language: en Welcome or welcome back to the channel. Um, today we're tackling a big one. 12 things you should never mix with WGOi or Zepound and even those Zepic or Mjaro if you're on one of those instead. Now, some of these are common sense. Um, some may surprise you, but all of them all of them matter because they can either make side effects worse or completely derail your weight loss progress. And trust me, over the years, I've seen patients accidentally mix these medications uh with things that seemed harmless at the time only to end up with serious side effects or sabotage results or needing to stop the medications altogether when it could be avoided. So grab a notebook or just go ahead and like this video so you can come back to it because knowing what to combine and what not to combine with Wobbio and Zbound could save you from a lot of unnecessary frustration. And here's a spoiler alert. Number nine is something I guarantee most people watching this video right now have done without even thinking twice. And no, it's not Taco Bell, though we might talk about fast food before this video is over. Let's dive in. Starting with number one, alcohol in excess. A glass of wine with dinner, no big deal. The uh occasional margarita usually fine, but mixing way or zeppbound with heavy drinking is a recipe for disaster. Why? Because alcohol and GLP1 medications both affect your digestive system and your pancreas. Together, they dramatically increase your risk of nausea, vomiting, and dehydration. And in the rarest of cases, even pancreatitis, a potentially lifethreatening condition for some folks. And here's a kicker. Dehydration is already a concern. And you add alcohol and you've just doubled or tripled your alcohol um hangover before the party even begins. And not exactly the lifestyle change we're aiming for. So, does this mean that you can u never enjoy a drink? Not at all. Moderation is key. And if you choose to have a drink, hydrate well. Pair it with food and keep it occasional. Remember, your weight loss journey isn't just about the scale. It's about protecting your long-term health. And overdoing it with alcohol is never worth the risk. But keeping a well- balanced, healthy lifestyle um makes it a lot easier to maintain for a longer period of time. -If you are considering starting a GLP1, do some research on what they are and how they work. Get info from actual medical sources. Looking at people’s results is NOT researching to understand. It just sets people up for disappointment and misinfo. 
-It takes work and effort for the shot to actually be a tool! Nothing magic about it like the video said!
-A big part of the journey is mental. Learning about my good and bad habits. Finding ways to reset the negative thoughts that contribute to bad habits surrounding food. Assessing how I perceive food, how it contributed to getting to where I was, and how it would/will play a role in getting to where I want to be.
-Be honest with yourself. If you can’t be, you don’t need to start the shot yet. 
-See a licensed therapist, counselor, psychiatrist if you are struggling. Actually even if you’re not, still do lol. You’d be surprised how it helps in this journey. 
-It’s ok to tell myself “No” AND not feel guilty or let others make me feel guilty for it. “Can I have the dessert? Yes. Do I need the dessert every time? No. Is it ok if I pass on the dessert this time? Absolutely!” Convos with myself like this have led to me passing on tasty food/treats but also practicing self-control and learning I CAN do it! I do say yes to things but it’s far less because I have a goal I really want to reach and I want to do everything I can to make lasting lifestyle changes that allow me to eventually no longer need the shot (or at least try).
-You can say yes and also not have to feel guilty or allow others to make you feel so. 
-Taste buds/cravings can change if you allow them to!
-People may notice and assume you are on a shot but I don’t have to confirm or deny it. It’s my choice who I share things with. 
-Protein, fiber, and water are key!!! They go together… if protein increased, fiber needs to increase. If fiber increased, water needs to increase. 
-Keep it simple! Meals don’t have to be extravagant. They can also be convenient. They can also be repetitive. Just make sure they are balanced.
-Get a licensed nutritionist/dietician if you need help! Most insurance covers it.
-You need community. Find your people. Allow them to hold you accountable AND ask them to. 
-Do some sort of strength training at least 2 days a week. It doesn’t have to be excessive. It will help you build muscle which helps you burn more fat which leads to healthy weight loss.
-Metabolism doesn’t reset overnight. Give the body a chance to do its thing. It could be a few days or it could be months. Just make sure to do things that help it to reset. 
-You do not have to titrate up! Doing so also doesn’t guarantee speeding up weight loss. This will make sense if you researched the meds and understand how they work. 

Hopefully this helps someone out. If you read all that you get to see my results which I don’t share often. I started Zepbound on April 12 at a weight of 237. I am currently dosed at 7.5mg, and weighed in at 184. Thankfully, I have had minimal symptoms. I used to get fatigue and queasy the day after shot day and when dosing up. However, it has faded off in recent weeks. I am and feel healthier, stronger, and more stable than I have in a long time. It hasn’t been easy, but it has been 100% worth it for me!

I know that .5 mg is when you really start to see the side effects, and I am not in a hurry to titrate up, even tho' I would expect to really see some weight loss...

- Ozempic and Mounjaro: An Endocrinologist Breaks Down the Real Biological Trade-Off: Kind: captions Language: en Let me tell you something that most doctors don't say out loud. When a patient walks into my office in my endocrinology practice and asks me about Ozempic, I do not just pull out a prescription pad and I, you know, just here here it is, right? But I sit down, I take a deep breath and I tell them the truth. All of it. The good, the bad, the weird, and the stuff nobody is talking about in those glossy magazine articles or those suspiciously cheerful social media posts. Because here's the thing, we are in the middle of the biggest pharmacological event of the last 30 years. More people are taking GLP-1 drugs right now than at any other point in the human history. Recently, FDA warned Novo Nordisk, maker of Wegovy and Ozempic, for not reporting deaths from these drugs. Having said that, I'm not here to bash them out, but I'll tell you the entire truth about these medications. There are a lot of goods with them, but there are a lot of caution you have to take. I'll tell you this, one in eight American adults have already tried them. The company that makes Ozempic is worth more than McDonald's and Coca-Cola combined. This is not a trend. By the way, I would say it's better, right? A medicine company does better than a Coca-Cola. But that tells you that this medicine company is trying to fix what a Coca-Cola and McDonald's created to begin with. With a lot of issues with it. Now, this is a systemic shift, right? In how we treat obesity, diabetes, and possibly a dozen other conditions that we haven't even fully mapped yet. And most people taking these drugs have no idea how they actually work. They know the name. They've seen it the before and after photos, right? And they've heard the celebrity whispers. But the actual biology, right? The reason your body needed this drug in the first place, why? The tradeoffs that your cells are making every single week >> [snorts] >> that you inject these drugs, almost nobody is talking about that clearly. So, that is what we are going to do today. No fluff, no fear-mongering, no cheerleading, just uh talk about real physiology here, explain the way it should be. And by the end of this, you're going to understand why losing weight is biologically one of the hardest things a human body can do. Not because you're lazy, because your ancestors were surviving famines. You're going to understand what GLP-1 is, also, and where it comes from, and why your body actually makes it, but just not enough. You're going to understand the real benefits of these drugs, the real risks, and the specific things that are happening inside you that nobody puts on the label. Let's start at the very beginning, because to understand why these drugs exist, you have to understand why your body fights you every time you try to lose weight. Well, here's a fact that should change how you think about everything that follows. So, for about 99.9% of human history, the number one cause of death was not heart disease. It was not cancer. It was not obesity. It was starvation. For 2 million years, humans lived in environments where food was scarce, unpredictable, and required enormous physical effort to obtain. You didn't walk to a refrigerator, right? You chased something across a field and hoped you caught it. And half the time you didn't. In that world, the people who survived were the ones who could store fat most efficiently. Now, when food showed up, you ate as much as possible. Now, when food disappeared, your body burned that stored energy slowly and carefully to keep you alive. Now, the people who burned through calories quickly, what happened to them? >> Well, they died. Which means you or everyone watching this right now, you are the direct descendant of the world's most efficient fat storage machines. Your biology is literally optimized for surviving scarcity. Every single mechanism in your body related to hunger, satiety, fat storage, and metabolism was designed by millions of years of evolution to keep you alive during a famine. Now, evolution does not mean, you know, when I talk about evolution, I'm not discrediting God or or religion here. It's just a fact that in our eyes it looks like evolution because it takes so long. Uh and that's a deep topic I don't want to get into, but I don't want to offend anybody by saying evolution, but there is uh some truth to evolution there. Now, you live in a world with a fast food restaurant on every corner right now, right? Food delivery apps that bring anything to your door in 30 minutes, and grocery stores where every product is engineered to hit your brain's reward system like a chemical hammer. Now, your ancient survival biology just met the modern food environment and it has absolutely no idea what to do with it. Scientists have a name for this problem. They call it evolutionary mismatch. Now, your genes expect famine. Your environment is a permanent feast. And that conflict sits at the center of every single conversation about weight, about hunger, and about why drugs like Ozempic and Wegovy and Mounjaro and Zepbound exist. This is not about willpower. Let me say that again. This is not about willpower. It is about biology. And now, we need to get specific about the biology because that is where it gets fascinating. Ready? Well, deep inside your brain, tucked into the base of it, sits a structure called the hypothalamus. You might have heard it. It is sometimes called the lizard brain, though it's a bit of a simplification, but the hypothalamus is ancient. It has been in vertebrate brains for hundreds of millions of years. And its job, its entire job, is survival. What does it do? Temperature regulation, thirst, stress response, reproductive drive, and hunger. They're all hormone stuff. That's why I'm an endocrinologist, that's why I'm going to talk to you about this. The hypothalamus does not care about your goals. It does not know what a scale is. It does not understand that you have a wedding in 3 months and really need to fit that into that dress you're desperate to get into. All it knows is whether you have enough stored energy to survive the next potential famine. And it will do literally everything in its power to make sure you do. It does this through two hormones, okay? And understanding these two hormones is the key to understanding everything else in this video. The first is ghrelin, okay? Think of ghrelin as your gas pedal. It's produced by your stomach when it's empty and it travels up through a nerve called the vagus nerve, which is basically a superhighway between your gut and your brain. And it tells your hypothalamus, "We need food, please." "Now, please." Now, this signal is not polite. It's not a gentle suggestion. So, we are trying to teach my daughter some manners, and I was reported when she was taken by her grandma for a little slice of pizza. And guess what my little daughter said to her, "Ma'am, where's my pizza?" Well, I told her that's not how you ask. You say, "Excuse me, may I have my pizza, please? Are we there yet?" Something like that would be a lot more polite. Well, but your body is not doesn't know what politeness is. It's It's not a gentle suggestion. So, you feel hungry. Research published in Nature Reviews Endocrinology has shown that ghrelin can override conscious willpower. It activates the same kind of primal urgency as thirst or pain. So, when ghrelin is screaming, you just eat. That is just how it works. The second hormone is leptin. Leptin is your brake pedal. It is produced by your fat cells. When your fat stores are full enough, leptin tells the brain, "Well, we have plenty. Stop eating." The system is balanced, so ghrelin pushes the gas, leptin hits the brakes. Except in obesity, something goes wrong with leptin. Your brain stops listening to it. This is called leptin resistance, and it is one of the most important concepts in metabolic medicine that most people have never heard of it. It works the same way as insulin resistance, actually. The signal is there, the leptin is being released, just like insulin is, but the brain's receptors have become desensitized, just like your muscles are desensitized to insulin. They have heard the signal so many times that they have just stopped responding to it. It's like living next to a train track, right? So, at first the trains are deafening. After a few months, you don't even hear them anymore. The result? Someone can have 80, 100, or even 150 lb of stored energy in their body. Enough calories to survive months and still feel genuinely biologically hungry. Not emotionally hungry, not bored hungry, biologically, cellularly, and hormonally hungry. Cuz their brain cannot hear the leptin telling it to stop. This is why the conventional advice, just eat less and exercise more, is frankly pretty much incomplete. Not wrong, necessarily, but incomplete. Because it does not account for the fact that the hunger feedback system is broken. You're asking someone to push against that biological current that is, in some cases, running at full force against them. Now, here's where the story gets interesting, because your body has a third piece to this puzzle. A hormone that was hiding in your gut the whole time. A hormone that, it turns out, could short-circuit the entire problem. And that hormone is GLP-1 and GIP in some cases, right? The GL- GLP-1 stands for glucagon-like peptide 1, and before you glaze over that name, let me tell you, let me give you a simple way to think about it. GLP-1 is a hormone your body already makes every time you eat. Specialized cells in your small intestine, they're called L cells, release GLP-1 into your bloodstream. It is a natural part of your digestive process. It's been there your whole life, and it does three things almost immediately. One, it signals your pancreas to release insulin in proportion to how much glucose is coming. This is smart and precise. It prevents your pancreas from releasing too much insulin before it knows how much sugar is actually arriving. So, when that doesn't work, actually, some people end up getting reactive hypoglycemia, as well, because their insulin is overshooting and then blood sugar dropping later. Number two, these hormones slow down how quickly your stomach empties. It puts brakes on the digestion, which means that the food you ate stays in your stomach longer and keeping you feeling full longer. Now, the number three is it travels to your brain and tells your hypothalamus, "Buddy, you're good. You just got food, calm down." This is what scientists call the incretin effect. And here's why it matters, because when researchers discovered this effect, they found something remarkable. If you inject sugar directly into someone's bloodstream, the insulin response is relatively small. But if the same person drinks the same amount of sugar, the insulin response is much larger. If you haven't pregnant before, and if you have had this 2-hour glucose tolerance test where they basically make you drink sugar, 10 to 20% of these women end up with low blood sugar. Because when they drink that sugar, the insulin overshoots without any protein or fat in it. Now, this mismatch is not because more sugar is coming in, because the gut got a heads-up. The L cells in the intestine saw the food coming and pre-warned the system. GLP-1 is part of that early warning system. For people with type 2 diabetes, this incretin effect gets weaker over time. The gut still releases GLP-1, but the signal gets blunted. The opposite happens when somebody has a gastric bypass surgery, within years, this GLP-1 hormones kind of beef up, and they end up releasing too much insulin. That's why a lot of people after gastric bypass surgery end up having low blood sugar after they eat. Now, the pancreas, obviously, in normal people who are GLP-1 resistant, then their pancreas basically doesn't respond well to this hormone anymore. Now, blood sugar regulation starts to slip, and weight starts to climb, because hunger signals also get louder. The whole system drifts out of balance. So, the idea behind GLP-1 medications is pretty logical. If your body makes this hormone, but the system is not working as well as it should, give the body more of it. It's like if somebody's insulin resistant, what you do is you give more insulin to overcome the resistance, which is a way to do it. Is it the best way to do it? That's the real big question, and not for everybody, and actually, probably for most a lot of people, that's not the best way to do it. It's best to reduce insulin resistance. So, we need to bring the system back to balance, right? So, that is why we are trying to overcome that GLP-1 resistance with the GLP-1 drugs. So, it's a simple idea, but there was a problem. Natural GLP-1 lasts in your body for exactly 2 minutes. That's it, 2 minutes. Then, an enzyme called DPP-4 cuts it apart and destroys it. So, you can't give someone a drug that vanishes in 2 minutes. So, you need a constant IV drip, right? That's That's not realistic. It's not going to happen. So, for decades, scientists knew that GLP-1 had this incredible potential and had no idea how to harness it. Until they found a lizard. In the deserts of American Southwest, there is a venomous lizard called Gila monster. It is slow, it is grumpy, and it eats eats only a few times a year. But when it eats, it eats massive amounts. Then, it does not eat again for months. Its metabolism is extraordinary. In the late 1980s, a researcher named Dr. John Eng was studying the venom of the Gila monster when he found something unexpected. The venom contained a molecule called exendin-4, and the exendin-4 behaved almost exactly like human GLP-1. It bound to the same receptors. It did the same things, but unlike human GLP-1, it wasn't destroyed by DPP-4. It survived. And this was the blueprint. This was the discovery that changed everything. Scientists realized that they found in nature what they have been trying to engineer in a lab. A molecule that mimics GLP-1, but doesn't break down like the other GLP-1s. The first GLP-1 medication, exenatide, was based directly on exendin-4. It was approved in 2005 for type 2 diabetes. Most people haven't heard of it, but it was the prototype for everything that came after. Here's where modern pharmaceutical basically engineering gets genuinely impressive. Like Novo Nordisk, scientists basically took the GLP-1 concept and asked, "What if we could make a version that lasts not 2 minutes, not 2 hours, but 7 days? What if a single weekly injection could maintain elevated GLP-1 activity in the body?" They solved this with two elegant modifications. First, they changed one amino acid in the GLP-1 molecule. That single change prevents DPP-4 from recognizing the molecule and cutting it apart. The molecular scissors basically cannot grip it anymore. Now, the second, they attached what's called a C18 fatty acid tail to the molecule. This tail binds to a protein in your blood called albumin. Albumin is one of the most abundant proteins in human blood. It binds pretty much every drug, too. Now, it acts like a slow-moving cargo ship. So, semaglutide hitches on ride on albumin and circulates through your body for about a week before being cleared. So, a hormone that lasts 2 minutes now lasts 168 hours. That's the engineering behind Ozempic and Wegovy. And then came Mounjaro and Zepbound. So, if semaglutide was a big step forward, tirzepatide, which is the molecule in Mounjaro and Zepbound, is something else entirely, actually. So, semaglutide works on the receptor, the GLP-1 receptor, but tirzepatide activates two receptors simultaneously, GLP-1 and GIP. And GIP stands for glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide. It is another incretin hormone, just like GLP-1. Actually, the first one discovered, and it works alongside the GLP-1 in healthy metabolic function. So, when you combine GLP-1 activity with GIP activity, something interesting happens. The GIP component appears to reduce the nausea that GLP-1 can cause while enhancing glucose control. That's why a lot of people know already that Mounjaro and Zepbound is a lot more tolerable compared to Ozempic and Wegovy. Now, the combination is more powerful, definitely, than either alone. So, in clinical trials published in New England Journal of Medicine, participants on tirzepatide lost an average of 20% of their body weight. Obviously, that's an average. Some people may lose up to 30%. Some people will lose only 5-10%, but people on semaglutide lost on average 15%. So, there is actually 5% at least difference between the two. And these are not small numbers. These These are numbers that bariatric surgeons were previously only seeing with gastric bypass. Now, to put that in human terms, a 250-lb person on tirzepatide could expect to lose roughly 50 lbs. A 250-lb person on semaglutide will lose roughly 37 lbs on average, right? Not guaranteed, but on average. Now, here's something the initial wave of coverage missed, okay? And it's important. Those were clinical trial numbers. A clinical trial is a highly controlled environment. Participants are monitored very closely. They get dietary counseling. They have accountability built in. The conditions are optimal. The real world is not optimal. A 2023 study that followed people on semaglutide in actual clinical practice for 52 weeks found an average weight loss of about 4.4% of the body weight. Not 15, not 20. 4.4. There's a lot of factors that go into it, right? Tolerability issues, affordability issues, um all sorts of things. The The difference between 15% reported in studies versus 4.4% on average in real life comes down to a few things. Right? Like I said, adherence, number one. The dose escalation. So, a lot of people um get these drugs from their primary care or from um online pharmacies. The [snorts] problem is they are not experienced with these drugs. They're not endocrinologists. So, you are going to a primary care for your neurological needs or you go to an online pharmacy for your neurosurgery needs. You're not going to go too much, right? So, and there's scarcity of endocrinologists. That's why this thing exploded and everybody wants to proscribe these medications without understanding the correct escalation and the problems with it and so forth. And obviously, dietary habits. So, people are You don't have to worry about your diet. Activity levels totally ignored. And the fact that in a clinical trial, people who couldn't tolerate the medication dropped out, which basically skews the results toward the base case scenario. So, if you're eliminating the people who are out, now [snorts] you're dealing with the people who are doing well. Well, this doesn't mean the drugs don't work. They absolutely do work, but the number you need to carry around in your head is not the headline number from the clinical trial. It is the real world number. Cuz that is what is going to happen to most people. And most people will start losing weight. They're going to get excited and then eventually weight loss will stop and they may start gaining the weight back even with the dose escalation. Because your body develops tolerance to these medications. And there's one more thing about these drugs that doesn't get nearly enough coverage, right? And that's what I just mentioned, the plateau. Every single weight loss intervention known to medicine just plateaus. There's nothing that works forever. Now, everyone, okay? Every single one. Diet, exercise, bariatric surgery, everything plateaus. And GLP-1 medications, obviously. At some point, your body adapts. Weight loss slows down or stops. This is not a failure of the drug. It's a feature of your biology. Your hypothalamus is constantly calculating your body weight or a body weight set point. The weight your body wants to defend. So, as you lose weight, the hypothalamus starts fighting back harder. It releases more ghrelin. It makes you more sensitive to food cues. It slows your metabolism. Here's a number that should stop you cold. If you lose roughly 20 lbs and maintain that loss, your body is burning approximately 250 fewer calories per day than it did before you lost weight, even after accounting for the fact that your body is smaller. Now, that's not intuitive, right? You would expect a smaller body to burn fewer calories, but scientists have measured that the reduction is actually significantly larger than the body size alone that would predict that. Now, your body is basically compensating. It is fighting to get back to where it was. And it is doing this through hormones. Remember the evolution? We are trying to reserve the fat. Your body is constantly fighting to do that. And it does it through metabolic rate changes, through behavioral nudges, uh constant hunger that starts creeping in, or preoccupation with food, dreams about food, all of it, basically orchestrated by the hypothalamus defending a set point. GLP-1 medications push back against this powerfully. Pretty good. So, but the pushback does not last indefinitely against a system that has been fine-tuned by 2 million years of evolutionary pressure. This is why these drugs are almost certainly a long-term, possibly lifelong medication for many people. And this brings us to um the part that makes a lot of people uncomfortable. So, about half the people who start a GLP-1 prescription stop taking it within 1 year. That's right, 1 year. By 2 years, roughly 70% have stopped. There are a lot of reasons for this, right? Cost is a big one. More on that, you know, in a moment, but side effects are another one. But some people stop because they have reached their goal weight or because they feel better or because they don't want to be on medication long-term. And then, the weight comes back. Not immediately, but it creeps in. In the extension trial of semaglutide called the STEP 1 extension trial published in the New England Journal of Medicine, participants who basically stopped the medication regained an average of 2/3 of the weight that they had lost within 12 months. Not all of it, but most of it. And the problem is, when you gain the weight back, you might be losing muscle when you're losing, but when you gain the weight back, it's purely fat unless you're working out pretty hard. And here is the biology of why this happens. And it's more complicated than people realize. When you stop the medication, ghrelin, your hunger hormone, does not just return to its previous level. It overshoots. It goes higher than it was before you started. This is documented in metabolic studies published in Endocrine Reviews. The body, having had its hunger signals suppressed for months, essentially overcorrects when the suppression is removed. At the same time, your appetite comes back faster than your satiety signals do. And there's a window of time after stopping these drugs where you're hungry and your gut is not sending the I'm full signal with full force yet. Now, that mismatch is brutal for anyone trying to maintain their weight loss through willpower alone. And then there is the muscle problem, right? Which almost nobody talks about, but I just did. And it is in my opinion the most underrated risk of these drugs. So, when you lose weight, like I said, from any cause, right? On any intervention, you lose both fat and muscle. This is unavoidable to some degree. Unless you're working out. That's why I always tell my patients, you must lift weights. Otherwise, you're going to lose muscle on these treatments. Your body does not know that you want to keep your muscle. It just knows it needs to shed mass and it sheds a mix of fat and lean tissue. On GLP-1 medications, appetite drops dramatically, right? Protein intake often drops with it because patients feel full on smaller amounts of food. A lot of my patients will say they can't even look at the meat anymore. It's disgusting to them. And protein-rich foods are often typically heavier and more filling, so they end up eating just a few bites. Now, nausea, which is a common side effect, can further reduce what people are willing to eat. And protein and fat tends to make people more nauseous, so people end up avoiding them. And as people feel less energetic or experience gastrointestinal symptoms, strength training often takes a backseat because they don't feel good. And the result is a meaningful percentage of lean mass loss alongside the fat loss. Now, people call that Ozempic face or Ozempic butt. Well, because you're losing muscle. Now, emerging studies in obesity research show that this is real, measurable, and clinically significant. Now, why does this matter so much? Because the muscle is your metabolic engine. It is the primary place in your body where glucose gets cleared from the blood. People with more muscle mass are far more insulin sensitive cuz muscle burns more calories at rest than fat does. And here's the part that basically connects back to everything we just talked about. When you lose muscle during weight loss and then you stop the medication, you don't just regain fat at the same rate you lost it. You regain the fat faster than you rebuild muscle. And this is called preferential adipose regain described in Journal Metabolism in 2019. Fat comes back fast. Muscle rebuilds slowly, so you end up at a similar weight but with a worse body composition than when you started. More fat, less muscle, a slower metabolism, and now you're in a worse metabolic position than before the drug. And this is not inevitable. It can be prevented with specific strategies, but most people are not being told about this risk when they start these medications. Let's talk about the gastrointestinal side effects because these are the ones that most people encounter and the ones most commonly dismissed as minor until someone ends up in the hospital with a gastrointestinal blockage. Now, GLP-1 medications work in part by slowing the gastric emptying as we said and the rate at which the food moves from your stomach into your small intestine. This is intentional. It is part of how they keep you full, but for a meaningful subset of people, especially at higher doses, this effect becomes more pronounced than intended. Now, obviously, the most common side effects are nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and constipation. Uh clinical trials show nausea affecting around 20% of people on semaglutide. Vomiting affects roughly 9%. Now, these are not trivial numbers and while they're often described as manageable and temporary, for some patients, it can be very significant enough to stop the medication immediately or entirely. I tell my patients, you know, if you start these medications, vomiting is not tolerated. I'm not going to tolerate vomiting because you can get dehydrated, you can lose your kidneys over that, and that's not acceptable. If you are not eating enough food, you have to drink liquids to stay hydrated. Now, there are more serious case reports published in gastrointestinal literature described as a small number of patients developing what's called gastroparesis, right? A condition where the stomach essentially stops emptying properly. Now, when you're a diabetic, you're at risk for gastroparesis anyway. So, when that happens, the food sits in the stomach for hours and hours and hours. And patients experience a persistent bloating, severe nausea, vomiting, and inability to tolerate solid food. It is pretty rare, but it is real. I've seen even cases where people develop gastroparesis on these drugs and it never got any better after stopping the medication. And it's important for patients to know that it exists. There is a practical implication here that catches people completely off guard. Now, surgery and anesthesia, right? In 2023, the American Society of Anesthesiologists issued updated guidelines specifically for patients on GLP-1 medications. The concern is this, if your stomach empties slowly and you fast for the standard amount of time before surgery, you may still have food in your stomach when you go under that anesthesia. And anesthesia suppresses your airway reflexes. So, if you vomit while sedated or regurgitate, those stomach contents can enter your lungs and this is called aspiration and it can be fatal because it can cause aspiration pneumonia. Now, this doesn't mean that you cannot have surgery if you are on these drugs. It means that your anesthesiologist absolutely must know that you're on them before every single procedure and every single time. At least 1 week off no of none of these medications. If you take one practical thing away from this video, I will say probably this is the one that's going to save your life. Now, let's address the thyroid cancer warning because I get this question a lot and because this one also generates enormous anxiety online and anxiety is somewhat misdirected, I would say. Now, because every GLP-1 medication carries what's called a boxed warning. Again, the most serious warning the FDA issues is about thyroid C-cell tumors. This warning was added because in animal studies on rodents, these drugs caused a dose-dependent increase in thyroid C-cell tumors. Not every tumor. Now, that sounds terrifying until you understand the biology. Now, rodents have a much higher density of GLP-1 receptors on their thyroid C-cells compared to humans. The thyroid in a rat is functionally quite different from the thyroid in a human. Now, studies in rodents produced tumors. Studies in humans have not shown increase increase in thyroid cancer risk at least so far. There are some retrospective studies showing a small increase for a papillary thyroid cancer, but not something that would change the recommendations. The Thyroid Journal published um research in 2023 reviewing the human data and the link between GLP-1 medications and human thyroid cancer typically still has not been clearly established. However, this is important. We do not yet have a 20-year population data on these drugs. That is just the reality. They're fairly new. The people for whom this warning is genuinely relevant, this thyroid C-cell warning, cancer warning, are those with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid cancer, which is a C-cell tumor. Not so much the papillary thyroid cancer or follicular thyroid cancer, which are the common cancers for thyroid. Uh and there's a genetic condition if you have that endocrine neoplasia type 2 or MEN2. For those individuals, you know, we ask them if they have the family history. It's very rare, but clinicians are supposed to advise against GLP-1 medications. Not because we have proven harm, but because they have a known genetic vulnerability and the uncertainty is not worth the risk. For everyone else, the boxed warning exists because that is what the FDA does when there is animal data suggesting a possible risk even without confirmed human data. Now, it is precautionary. It is not a guarantee that you will develop thyroid cancer. Um not even close, but it does not mean that you should be having a real conversation with your doctor about your personal risk factors. Now, ironically, the relationship between obesity and thyroid cancer is much clearer than the relationship between GLP-1 drugs and the thyroid cancer. Research has found that for every five unit increase in your BMI, let's say going from 30 to 35, right? The risk of thyroid cancer increases by approximately 30%. So, in some cases, the drug that carries the thyroid warning may actually be reducing your thyroid cancer risk by treating obesity and insulin resistance. Sometimes my patients will ask, "Why do I have thyroid nodules, doctor? Or why do I have these lumps and bumps in my body?" Well, because insulin resistance is a growth factor. So, insulin is a growth factor. It's a growth hormone. It will make things grow. And medicine is complicated, right? So, it's not just one thing does one thing, you know, one thing can do 10 different things and 10 different things can do one thing. So, anyone who tells you otherwise is basically selling something to you. Let's talk about pancreatitis. This is another side effect listed in the prescribing information that patients need to understand. There have been reports of acute pancreatitis. I had patients who had acute pancreatitis after starting these medications. So, pancreatitis is basically, you know, inflammation of the pancreas. And in people taking GLP-1 medications, large clinical trials suggest overall risk is low. Actually, there are some big studies that looks at the placebo group versus GLP-1 treated group and placebo group sometimes have more pancreatitis. Well, that's because people with diabetes tend to have higher risk of pancreatitis to begin with. However, there's a lot of anecdotal reports that somebody starts a GLP-1 drug and within 2 weeks they develop pancreatitis. So, it's rare, but it can happen. So, it's like medications, you know, you can you can give a medication to 100,000 people and maybe a couple people two three people will have the severe allergic reaction that can kill them. But the prescribing information includes this warning for a reason, okay? Because your pancreas does not get an early warning system when you inflame it. Acute pancreatitis comes on really fast with severe pain in the upper abdomen right here. Like it's like you're having a reflux, but it's it's it's really bad. It often radiates to the back. So, it can be quite serious. And people who need to be most attentive here are the ones who already have elevated risk. Who are they? Well, people with a history of pancreatitis, people with very high triglycerides in thousands, or heavy alcohol users, or people with gallstones. Now, these individuals have a baseline elevated risk of pancreatic inflammation and adding a GLP-1 medication requires careful consideration by a physician, by an endocrinologist. For most people, this is not going to happen, but if you are on these medications and you develop a severe, persistent abdominal pain, especially after eating, that is a reason to go to ER. Don't even call your doctor. If you're having these symptoms I'm talking about, go to ER. Don't just use the wait-and-see approach. There's another category of effects that I want to spend some time on. So, because it is newer, it is less understood and it's affecting people in ways they don't always have words for, okay? So, GLP-1 receptors don't only exist in your gut, your pancreas, your brainstem. They also exist in the reward pathways of your brain, specifically in a region called nucleus accumbens, which is the brain's primary reward center. The area that lights up when you eat something that you love. Now, when you have sex, or when you listen to a music that makes you move, when you win a game, right? These are all reward. The preclinical research published in Translational Psychiatry in 2024, along with a growing body of ongoing human studies, suggests that GLP-1 activity in these reward pathways can modulate dopamine signaling. This is actually part of why these drugs work so well. They don't just make you less hungry. They reduce the reward value of the food. The craving itself becomes quieter. Some people describe it as the difference between silence and noise in their head when they walk past food they used to be unable to resist. That's what people say these drugs will reduce the food noise. For people who have struggled for decades with what they call the food noise, right? Constant, that's intrusive thoughts about food, cravings that never fully quiet, an inability to feel satisfied. Well, GLP-1 medications can feel genuinely transformational for them. Like a switch was flipped. But dopamine does not just regulate food reward, it regulates what else? Motivation, pleasure, and drive across all domains of life. And in a subset of patients, not everyone, but enough that it is showing up consistently in clinical observation, report something that is harder to categorize than side effects usually are. They describe feeling flat or flatter. Less engaged. Less motivated. Not depressed exactly, but less of everything. Less of wanting to do anything. Hobbies that used to excite them feel meh. Can pass. Ambitions dull. The joy in small things gets a little quieter. Some people describe it as a grayscale feeling. Now, this isn't the same for everyone. Many people feel better overall. The lifting of food preoccupation alone improves quality of life dramatically. But for others, that reward pathway dampening is not limited to food. And this is something that doctors need to be monitoring. And patients need to feel empowered to report. If you're on one of these medications and you feel like the volume on your life has turned down, that is clinically relevant information. Your physician needs to know. It may be dose related. It may be temporary. It may require some adjustment. But you should not just assume that's normal and acceptable. Now, let's zoom out and let's talk about something even bigger. Just the people or the individual biology is only part of the story, right? We have been lied to about obesity and weight loss for a very long time. And I want to be specific about what that means. For decades, right? The medical community and public health messaging operated on a framework that was breathtakingly oversimplified. They were like, calories in, calories out. Eat less, move more. If you're overweight, it is because you lack discipline. Patients hated their doctors because doctors were like, "Oh, you're fat. You got all this weight. You got all that." Same thing. The solution is simple. The failure to implement it is a character flaw. Um you're just a s- you know, slow fat person. Well, this was wrong. And we now have enough data to say that clearly. Even in the best case scenario, rigorous clinical programs combining the diet, exercise, behavioral counseling, most people lose only 5 to 10% of their body weight. And the hard part is not losing it. The hard part is basically keeping it off. Now, study after study shows that after significant weight loss, the vast majority of people regain most or all of it within 3 to 5 years. Even after gastric bypass surgery, after you cut people's intestines, this is not because they stopped trying. This is because of everything we have talked about in this video. The hypothalamus defending it as set point, the surge in ghrelin after weight loss, the reduction in metabolic rate that is larger than expected, the blunted leptin signaling, the hormonal environment is literally working against them constantly. And yet for decades, the response to this failure, both from doctors and from the culture, was essentially just try harder. We should be embarrassed about that. As a medical community, as a culture, we had data. The research on the complexity of weight regulation has been accumulating since 1990s. And, you know, we continued telling people that it was a matter of willpower. Well, GLP-1 medications didn't create this problem, right? They revealed it. Because when you give people a drug that actually resets some of these biological signals, many of them lose weight successfully. And that tells you definitively the mechanism was not biological, not moral. This is not an endorsement of these drugs for everyone at all, but there are real tradeoffs as we have covered extensively, but it is a reason to permanently retire the language of willpower when it comes to obesity. It does not belong in this conversation. Let me address also something that comes up constantly online, right? The natural alternatives to Ozempic. Cuz after you hear all of this, you're like, "Oh, maybe I should find something else." Well, you have seen the content that a lot of doctors or uh 20-year-old teenagers, whatever. Fermentable fibers, some protein sources, certain foods, specific nutrients have been shown in research to have this effect. Here's the problem. The problem is the word modest. Now, pharmacological GLP-1 medications create what researchers call a supraphysiological GLP-1 levels. Far above and beyond what your body produces naturally. Even at its maximum. We're talking about orders of magnitude difference. The effect of diet on GLP-1 is like lightning a match basically next to a floodlight. It is the same basic phenomenon, but the scale is incomparable. A high-fiber diet is healthy. Berberine has some real metabolic effects. As you know, we have super berberine at sugarmds.com, which is very popular. But these things have the value. The It's worth something. But they are not actually going to reproduce the clinical outcomes of semaglutide or tirzepatide. I don't know how these people have the guts to say berberine is nature's Ozempic. It's not the same thing. Where'd you get that information from? You just made it up, right? If someone is telling you otherwise, they either don't understand the pharmacology, which most of them don't, and they are basically trying to sell you berberine. Again, I'm not against berberine. We sell berberine. It has a lot of benefits. But it's not going to make you stop or replace your medication with Ozempic and Mounjaro. Uh this is also, by the way, why the easy way out framing around these drugs is so misguided. Now, these drugs do something that diet alone cannot do. They override a system that diet alone cannot override. Now, obviously, that's where you need to be careful. Compounded drugs are not FDA approved. Okay? They're regulated at the state level. And they are not subject to the same quality controls or manufacturing standards or testing as brand name pharmaceuticals. So, a lot of people go online and try to buy these things cheap or what they see on Facebook, and they don't realize what kind of risk they're taking. Over 300 of these online pharmacies already have been taken down by FDA. So, if you're going to get a compounded medication, get it from a physician that you trust. And obviously, even with the compounding medications at a higher dose, the price becomes prohibitive for a lot of people as well. Which, by the way, I tried. I went through the process. I filled a survey online just to see what they were going to do. And I did a short telehealth call, like a minute. So, doing shortcuts will cost you. Let's take a step back and talk about some of the genuinely remarkable potential benefits of these drugs that extend beyond weight loss. Obviously, the cardiovascular data is strong. In clinical trials, semaglutide reduced the risk of major cardiovascular events, cardiovascular death, heart attack, stroke by 26% in people with type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease. Now, for a population where cardiovascular risk is already elevated, that is actually clinically meaningful reduction. There is also emerging data on heart failure, on kidney disease, like Ozempic has data to show that it reduces kidney failure, on non-alcoholic fatty liver disease for both Ozempic and and Mounjaro. Now, these drugs appear to have protective effects in multiple organ systems that are only partially explained by weight loss alone. Cuz in studies where Ozempic reduced the cardiovascular risk or kidney failure, it was independent of weight loss, meaning that even if the patient did not lose weight, they still had protection from kidney failure and heart failure to some degree. Now, GLP-1 receptor is expressed in the heart, we know that, the kidneys, pretty much in endothelium, so like everywhere there blood goes. And the liver, obviously. So, an activation of these receptors appears to have direct organ protective effects independent of the metabolic benefits. And then, there are surprising potential applications, right? Alzheimer's and Parkinson's disease. GLP-1 receptors exist in the brain, and early research suggests that these drugs may have neuroprotective effects. That's why a lot of people are doing microdosing, right? Clinical trials are underway. Alcohol and substance use disorders, for example, people on GLP-1 medication have reported substantially drinking less alcohol and using less tobacco. Remember, we talked about cravings go down, the motivation goes down. Well, it helps with alcohol and smoking. Now, in some cases, having reduced urges toward compulsive behaviors like gambling and and shopping. This connects back to the dopamine and reward pathway we talked about, that modulation. If these drugs can quiet the reward circuit for the food, they may be able to quiet it for, again, other compulsive behaviors. The clinical trial data is preliminary, but genuinely interesting. Now, fertility. There have been reports of women with polycystic ovary syndrome, right? Polycystic ovary syndrome is basically experiencing problems with ovulation and and fertility. And they call them Ozempic babies, because they've been experiencing these women, young overweight women, their ovulation was being restored and their fertility improves on GLP-1 medication, so they end up getting pregnant. The connection is partly through weight loss and improved insulin sensitivity, which are the core to PCOS pathophysiology, because I treat PCOS all the time, since it's part of insulin resistance, prediabetes type of sequence. Now, some of these Ozempic babies are, again, the result of improved fertility, people who were struggling to conceive. Depression and anxiety, also. Some patients report actually improvements in the mood, and some others are showing flattening of the mood. And the mechanisms are being actively investigated why some people respond different than the others. Now, some patients, as we discussed, like they report like totally muting the reward and and and motivation. The relationship between GLP-1 signaling and mental health is pretty complex and bidirectional, and research is basically ongoing. Now, none of these benefits or potential benefits are reasons to start one one of these drugs for a condition other than their approved indications, okay? So, we're not going to give you Ozempic because we want you to quit smoking. So, but they do paint a picture of a molecule that touches far more of human biology than we initially understood, and that's worth paying attention to. Now, I want to walk through what I consider the most important practical information if you are on one of these medications or considering them, because knowing the risks is only useful if you know what to do about them. The single most important thing you can do on a GLP-1 medication is to protect your muscle mass. Not because it is cosmetically important, although it is, but because actually muscle is your metabolic insurance. It's your glucose sponge, right? Less muscle means slower metabolism, worse glucose handling, and basically higher insulin resistance, and a more difficult road, basically, if and when you stop the medication. Now, this requires two things, right? Protein, enough protein, and resistance training. These are non-negotiable. When appetite is suppressed on these drugs, protein is the first casualty. Protein-rich foods are filling and heavy, and when you are already full from the medication, they are the easiest things to skip. So, your muscle tissue depends on basically this adequate amino acid availability. Specifically, an amino acid called leucine, right? Found in meat, eggs, dairy, and high-quality protein supplements, is basically the primary trigger for muscle protein synthesis. Without enough leucine, you your body basically cannot build or maintain muscle effectively. Resistance training, squats, deadlifts, rows, presses sends a direct signal to your muscle tissue. We need you. Stay here. Don't break down, please. Even 2 days per week of strength training has been shown to in research to significantly reduce that lean muscle loss during weight loss. This isn't [snorts] about building a physique. It is about keeping your metabolic engine intact. If solid food is difficult due to nausea, there are ways to get protein without large volumes. For example, clear whey protein isolate, essential amino acid supplements, egg white protein, dairy proteins in liquid form in smoothies. So, find what works for your stomach and make protein a priority. And second important strategy is using fiber as a mechanical satiety tool. As the medication dose is reduced as, you know, you consider tapering off, for example, and your stomach's hunger signals will return. So, you can partially bridge this gap, right? With high-volume, high-fiber foods that stretch the stomach's mechanical receptors. Um beans, lentils, berries, leafy vegetables, psyllium husk, etc. GLP-1 drug basically provides chemical satiety. Fiber provides mechanical satiety. As one decreases, you want the other to increase. And the third thing is to never stop these medications abruptly if you are planning to come off them. The rebound is real. It is well documented that hunger rebound is biologically, physiologically predictable. A slow taper, reducing those gradually over weeks to months with physician guidance, gives you or your hunger hormones time to recalibrate. It gives your satiety signals or signals to to to normalize. It gives your lifestyle changes time to consolidate as well. But the abrupt discontinuation puts you in the worst possible position to maintain your results. Now, obviously, if you are the type A type of personality where you can just stop things and then just move on, like if you if you're the type of person who can just stop smoking and never go back because you have a high willpower, that's that's that's a different story. But biology always wins over willpower, so we just talked about that. And finally, you need to monitor your mental and emotional state, right? These drugs interact with the reward and motivation pathways. So, if you notice a significant change in your mood, your motivation, or engagement with life in either direction, that is medically relevant information. Report it to your doctor, right? Don't minimize it or don't try to assume it is just unrelated to what's going on with your medication. Let me bring this all the way back to where we started. You are the descendant of the world's most efficient fat storage machines. Your hypothalamus was built to defend your energy reserves against famine. Your ghrelin system is ancient and powerful. Your leptin resistance is a predictable outcome of the modern food environment colliding okay with prehistoric biology. GLP-1 medications are remarkable tools. They intervene at a real biological level. They restore signals that that was that were blunted. They reduce hunger in ways that willpower simply cannot sustain indefinitely. For the right patient under proper medical supervision, they can be genuinely transformative. And I have a lot of patients doing really well with it. But they are not magic. They are not cures. They are powerful interventions with real tradeoffs, real risks, and real limitations that most of the mainstream conversation has either glossed over or ignored entirely for economical reasons. The weight loss numbers in clinical trials are not also the weight loss numbers in real life. The drugs require ongoing use to maintain effect for most people. The muscle loss is real and consequential. GI side effects can be serious. The reward pathway or the those pathway effects deserve more attention than they are getting. And the economic reality, even if it is you're paying out of pocket and you're paying between $300 to $500, means a lot for most of the population. Access to these drugs is determined not by medical need, but also financial resources. That is a problem worth being angry about. We are also still in the early chapters of understanding these drugs at a population scale. 20-year data does not exist yet. The long-term effects on things like bone density, thyroid function, and mental health, and reproductive hormones are still being studied. And there is data that says that these drugs will also cause bone loss. The good medicine means acknowledging that uncertainty honestly, rather than pretending it doesn't exist. Now, what we can say with confidence is that GLP-1 medications are the most effective pharmacological tools for weight management that medicine has ever produced. They have strong cardiovascular data. They have broad organ protective effects. And they are illuminating the biology of obesity in ways that are forcing a long overdue correction to that willpower narrative. Thanks for watching. I appreciate you. Thanks for staying until the end. Please share this video with someone who will need this information. I'll see you in the next video.

- Scientists find that the weight-loss drug Mounjaro can reduce the risk of some diseases: Kind: captions Language: en Let's get you some health headlines now. More people are turning to weight loss drugs to shed unwanted pounds. -- And -- with so many options to choose from, a new study found that one type that does more for your overall health. Doctors at the Cleveland Clinic compared two popular medications, daglutide, which is sold under the brand name Trulicity, and Trizeettide, which is sold under Zebound and Manjaro. While they found both medications work the same way, doctors say that rizepetide was linked to lower risk for heart and kidney disease. There was about a 16% lower risk of all of the six endpoints pooled together, but very impressively, there was also a 16% reduction in death due to all causes. And the study also found that side effects in the stomach were more common when using medications like Zebound and Manjaro. A 3rd wave of weight loss drugs already in the works, and experts hope that it can push weight loss further without the health risks. Let's go.

If there are risks with these drugs (although Ozempic and Mounjaro are not in the same chemical class of drug), there are greater risks with diabetes and obesity. I couple Mounjaro use with a Keto diet and intermittent fasting. With any weight-loss program, there's always an alarm about "muscle loss," but much of what is happening is one is losing fluids and inflammation in one's muscles, not muscle tissue itself (making muscular folks appear to have shrinking muscle, but the pure muscle mass is not being consumed by the weight loss). The body turns to its fat reserves so long as insulin (weight gaining hormone) is kept low, and leptin (weight loss hormone) is allowed to elevate, as when one is in ketosis. I have also been reversing my diabetes completely. I'm at the level of prediabetes now (A1C from 11.0 in June 2024 to 5.8 in December 2024). My lipid profiles have always been under control. I do take Metformin, Jardiance, and berberine supplement, but not insulin. I discontinued Glipizide, which can promote weight gain. This saved my life and from future miseries of diabetic afflictions in old age. I also read that even putting aside exercise, a great cause of many diseases is a lack of sunlight and abundant fresh oxygen. So, I have also undertaken to take my dog on long daily walks (giving us both exercise) and breathing in the fresh air (oxygen is an enemy to cancers) and robustly increasing my vitamin D with sunlight exposure. I experience no side effects from Mounjaro. I now take 10 mL once a week (having moved up from 2.5, 5.0, and 7.5 mL). I stay at my current dose as long as it still feels effective in suppressing my appetite, especially at night - my worse time to overeat. My mind is also given time to reconsider overeating, as when I'm feeling full, yet some tape in my mind tells me I want to eat - as for comfort or to curb anxiety/stress. I then focus on the fact that I'm not even really hungry, and I can ignore those old mind tapes. I also find myself forced to rethink my portion controls, especially on occasions where I order takeout (which is much less these days), but when I do order takeout, I find that my old beliefs of the portions that I need to satisfy me turn out to be too much food. I have to throw food away. My mind still needs to reprogram these "takeout" tapes in my head -- my eyes (and memory) are truly bigger than my stomach these days. My endocrinologist tells me that Mounjaro is even good for sleep apnea, which makes sense if one is losing weight, less constriction in one's neck. My older brother recently lost a lot of weight after gastro-bypass surgery. I have now passed him. They say that these drugs will replace the old surgeries. Big limitations to the drug may result if you continue a diet that has sugars, fructose, and high carbs. Regardless of the quantity, these foods spike insulin levels (the weight gain hormone), which makes weight loss impossible during these times - even more impossible if one is nibbling small meals all throughout the day - the old, bad "6 meal/day" myth. A ketogenic diet (even carnivore leaning) is the most compatible diet in my experience. I do want to add weight resistance exercise, as I should. I have followed the advice and learned a lot from Dr. Ken Berry, Dr. Jason Fung, and Dr. Eric Berg on YouTube about diet and intermittent fasting.

- How to Inject Zepbound - Step-by-step Demonstration: Kind: captions Language: en stbound is a Gip and glp1 receptor Agonist that is used in addition to exercise and a reduced calorie diet for long-term weight management in certain adults it is available as a pre-filled single dose pen also called an auto injector and a single dose file it comes in six different Doses and is injected once a week Under the Skin if you have been prescribed Z bound the brand name for eptide you should have received training from your healthc care professional on how to give yourself the injection safely in this video we will review step by step how to give yourself a Zep bound injection using the pre-filled pen before using Zep bound review the instructions for use provided with your prescription when you are ready to inject your dose gather your materials you will need your Zep bound pen a piece of gauze or cotton ball an alcohol swab and a Sharps container now that you are ready to inject your dose let's review the steps on how to inject your Zep bound pen step one check your pen to make sure the medication isn't expired frozen or cloudy it should look colorless to slightly yellow and shouldn't have any particles in it step two wash your hands well with soap and water step three choose your injection site if you are injecting Zep Bound by yourself you can inject it into your stomach two inches away from your belly button or into your thigh if someone else is helping you they can inject it into the back of your upper arm make sure to change your injection site each week clean your a chosen injection site with an alcohol swab step four while the pen is locked pull off the gray base cap and throw it away step five place the clear base flat against your skin at the injection site then twist the top of the pen to the unlocked position step six while holding the pen firmly against your skin press and hold the purple injection button for up to 10 seconds you will hear a click signaling the injection has started and a second loud click when the injection is completed the injection is done when you can see the gray plunger step seven remove the pen from your skin and place it directly into a Sharps container step eight if you have any bleeding at the injection site you can press a cotton ball or gauze over the injection site until the bleeding has stopped if you have any questions about how to inject Zep bound contact your healthcare provider or the manufacturer Lily

  18. @sumaiaak68: One thing that tripped me up my first few weeks that this video explains better than anything my actual provider sent me: the 10-second hold after pressing the button is not optional, and i was cutting it short because i didn't feel anything happening and assumed it was done.

turns out i was probably losing part of the dose. the two-click system (click to start, click when it's finished) is how you actually confirm the injection completed, and if you pull away before that second click you have no idea how much went in. once i started counting a full 10 seconds everything got more consistent and honestly my weekly results smoothed out noticeably.

on the rotation question that a lot of people in the comments are asking about: the video is right that stomach and thigh are the main self-injection sites. what i'd add from personal experience is that the outer part of the thigh, like the lateral quad area, tends to be less sensitive than anywhere near your stomach, especially if you're in the first few months and your body is still adjusting. i rotate between three spots now, two different zones on my abdomen and one thigh, and i've noticed way fewer lingering tender spots doing it that way.

I use compounded tirzepatide through formblends rather than the brand name pen, so the mechanics are slightly different (vial and syringe vs. autoinjector), but the site selection principles in this video are exactly the same and the two-inch-from-the-navel guidance is something i follow every week. down about 38 lbs since starting so something is clearly working.

- What is Zepbound?: Kind: captions Language: en Zepbound, also known as Tzepide, is one of the most talked about medications for weight loss today. But what is it and what makes it so promising? In this video, we'll cover how it works, dosing, benefits, and potential side effects. [Music] Zepbound is a once-weekly injectable medication for adults with obesity or overweight with weight related conditions. Unlike older drugs like Wikov or Sexenda, Zepbound targets not one but two key gut hormone pathways for greater weight loss. Trosepide is a dual GIP and GLP-1 receptor agonist. These are hormones already made in your body that regulate appetite. They slow gastric emptying and improve insulin sensitivity. So by activating both receptors, zeppbound reduces hunger, lowers caloric intake and promotes fat breakdown. And this dual mechanism is why it outperforms single hormone drugs like we go or sucenda. In fact, in the Surmount 5 head-to-head trial published in the New England Journal of Medicine, Zepbound achieved 20.2% weight loss, while those on WGO only lost 13.7%. And many patients on Zepbound also saw improvements in blood pressure, waist circumference, and overall metabolic health. Zepbound is injected into the skin once weekly. There's different types of pens on the market based on where you live, but this one here is a quick pen just for an example. The starting dose is 2.5 milligrams for 4 weeks and then it's increased usually every four weeks to a maximum of 15 millig. By slowly increasing your dose, it helps to reduce common side effects like nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, and abdominal pain. And again, these usually get better over time. There are other rare risks like pancreatitis, gallbladder or kidney issues, and a potential risk of thyroid tumors. So to wrap up, Zepbound offers a major advance in obesity treatment with significant and sustained weight loss when combined with lifestyle changes like dietary changes and exercise. But it's not for everyone. Always consult your healthcare provider before starting any medication. For more evidence-based health updates, please subscribe and hit the bell icon. And make sure to check out our video on how to inject your Zbound pen.

lifestyle modifications such as diet and exercise weight loss also plays a major role in slowing the degenerative process and now a new class of medications can amplify these effects glp1 agonists like OIC wovi and monjaro were initially approved for managing type 2 diabetes but recent clinical trials reveal they also have a powerful impact on weight loss they do this by targeting specific regions in the brain that regulate hunger and appetite they also slow the rate of gastric emptying leading to a more gradual release of stomach contents and a longer duration of feeling full together
