check as we also need to discuss the potential downsides of gop1 Agonist first The observed benefits to arthritis need to be validated by larger more diverse clinical trials and let's not forget gop1 Agonist come with its share of potential drawbacks such as cost and side effects and there are two big concerns that I want to highlight number one keep in mind that gp1 agonists function by creating a calorie deficit essentially putting your body in a state resembling starvation in this state your body primarily turns to fat as an alternative energy source however fat alone cannot provide all the essential nutrients which is why the body also needs to break down muscle and while typical weight loss from a calorie deficit leads to approximately 20 to 25% muscle loss some studies such as this one suggest that glp1 Agonist result in up to 50% muscle loss so the concern here is that okay you've lost 30 lbs of weight but it turns out your body composition is actually worse than what it was before because you've lost more muscle mass relative to Fat this becomes even more important for people who are dealing with conditions such as arthritis tontis and tonopa or problems related to muscles and Bones our muscles play a key key role in keeping our joints stable and supported muscle loss and muscle weakness often lead to worse pain and the reality is as we age one of the greatest Health threats is losing muscle mass this is a key contributor to conditions like sarcopenia and loss of functional Independence in older adults muscle loss increases the likelihood of Falls in serious injuries such as hip fractures and to emphasize how important this issue is Research indicates that oneyear mortality rates following a hip fracture in older adults range from 15 to 30% but simply if 10 older adults suffer a hip fracture two or three of them are likely to pass away within 1 year this death rate is significantly higher compared to people of the same age who haven't experienced a hip fracture so you can see how if glp1 agonists are accelerating muscle loss this can potentially be a big problem now the way to counteract this is to make sure everyone on glp1 Agonist are also on a resistance and strengthening program to try to prevent these effects in addition newer types of gop1 Agonist such as tepati or Monaro may not cause the same reductions to lean muscle mass as OIC and wobi okay so that was the first big problem with gop1 Agonist the second big concern is the tendency for rapid weight regain once the medication is discontinued this usually occurs because most people don't make lasting changes to their eating and exercise habits when you take away a medicine that controls your appetite it's easy to start eating more again adding to the complexity eating fewer calories than you're used to can actually slow down your metabolism for example instead of burning say 2,000 calories a day your body might go down to burning only 1,500 or 12200 calories per day if you then go back to your old eating habits with this slower metabolism you're setting yourself up for rapid weight gain and unfortunately this is what we see in many of the clinical trials that are now being published studies such as this one show that glp1 Agonist do indeed help tremendously with weight loss but one year after withdrawal of the medication participants regained over 2third of their prior weight loss with similar changes to cardiometabolic variables the key takeaway is that while glp1 agonists can effectively enhance weight loss and improve health outcomes they shouldn't be considered a standalone solution or a quick fix in addition we still don't know if there are any potential long-term side effects and there's also the possibility of becoming reliant on the medication to maintain weight loss the people who have been most successful with glp1 agonists don't just count on the medication for weight loss they incorporate it into a comprehensive plan that includes changes in diet controlling portion sizes and integrating a balanced exercise routine with both cardio and strength training exercises the ultimate aim is to establish long-term habits so you can wean off of the medication and still keep a stable weight whether we ultimately use glp1 Agonist for the treatment of arthritis is going to depend a lot on how the medication affects muscle mass but the preliminary data seems very promising and if we can combine the effects of weight loss with a strength training regimen a lot of people with arthritis are going chance of side effects after the initial 4 weeks Zep bound dosage is typically increased to 5 mg once a week this step-by-step approach allows for careful monitoring of your response to the medication - Revolutionary Approach to Weight Loss -  Zepbound (tirzepatide)  Explained: Kind: captions Language: en Zep bound is a new weight management medication used during obesity to promote weight loss it mimics hormones naturally produced in our intestines which decrease appetite additionally it slows gastric emptying making a person feel Fuller for longer consequently this reduces the urge to eat and Cravings Zep bound is significant because it has demonstrated High Effectiveness in meta analyzes people taking Zep bound experience and average body weight reduction of 22% which is considered a very high rate of weight loss it is a newly FDA approved drug and is the first dual incretin Agonist meaning it resembles or mimics the body's natural hormones and enhances their action this drug is administered as a weekly injection now let's discuss how it works in the body and its main limitations Zep bound tepati mimics two incretin hormones which are released in our gut in response to food intake specifically Zep bound resembles glucagon like peptid 1 glp1 and glucose dependent insulinotropic polypeptide Gip this means Z bound binds to the natural hormone receptors and activates them its first action is to stimulate insulin secretion both hormones induce insulin secretion from the pancreas thus Zep bound prompts the pancreas to release insulin in response to glucose presence in the blood regarding appetite and food intake glp the one slows down gastric emptying leading to prolonged satiety and a reduction in overall calorie intake in terms of lipolysis both Gip and glp the one stimulate the breakdown of fats in the liver and muscles meaning the body uses them for energy thus accelerating weight loss this process also increases energy expenditure or metabolic rate causing more calories to be burned furthermore these hormones inhibit glucagen secretion meaning the liver releases less glucose into the bloodstream resulting in fewer blood sugar spikes this is because glucagen stimulates glucose production from glycogen by the liver additionally glp one increases the production of glucose transporter proteins facilitating glucose uptake by cells the primary indication for Zep bound is obesity specifically when the body mass index BMI is more than 40 it is also indicated for those with a BMI of 35 or higher accompanied by comorbidities such as diabetes hypertension and cardiovascular diseases as well as for individuals with type 2 diabetes the most significant limitation of the drug is its cost as it is expensive there is also limited data on its it long-term safety being a new drug there isn't sufficient information regarding its long-term effects in various cases the main side effects of Zep bound include abdominal discomfort diarrhea or constipation and sometimes noria although these side effects are temporary and usually Mild they are noteworthy Zep bound is contraindicated in cases of a history of pancreatis or multiple endocrine neoplasia syndrome at a can stimulate pancreatic enzymes and potentially exacerbate inflammation it may also promote the growth of tumors which is why it is contraindicated in these situations it is important to note however that there is no evidence suggesting that Zep bound increases the risk of any cancer but as previously mentioned the long-term results are not yet known due to the drugs novelty

- Lilly will offer higher doses of Zepbound on its website | REUTERS: Kind: captions Language: en Eli Lily said Monday it will ship the two highest doses of its popular weight loss drug Zepbound to cash paying customers on its website starting early August. That will make doses up to 15 milligrams available in single dose vials online for $499 per month or less regardless of insurance status. Lily and rival Novo Nordisks started offering vials at discounted prices on their respective websites to expand access and stave off competition from compounded versions or cheaper copies of their weight loss drugs. The Lily Direct website was launched last year to help patients directly order some drugs. In February, it reduced the price of Zepbound vials and expanded the range of doses for patients who choose to pay cash through the website. In May, CBS Health said it had decided to drop Zepbound from some lists of medicines it covers for reimbursement from July 1st, but would keep reimbursing for Novo Nordisk Buovi after negotiating a more favorable price from the Danish drug maker. Both Lily and Novo have been competing in the potential 150 billion dollar obesity drug market, but recent prescription data shared by analysts indicates Lily has taken the lead, overtaking no vote despite its initial first mover advantage.

AI overview: Obesity is not solely a disease of the poor or rich, but it is more prevalent among those with lower incomes and who experience food insecurity. While anyone can be obese, obesity is strongly linked to poverty, low income, and a lack of food security in the United States. 
Here's a more detailed look: Poverty and Obesity: Studies show a correlation between poverty and obesity rates, particularly in the United States. 
Income and Obesity: Lower income levels are often associated with higher rates of obesity, especially among women. 
Food Insecurity: Food insecurity, often linked to poverty, can contribute to obesity by leading to consumption of cheaper, less nutritious foods. 
Global Trends: While obesity was once considered a problem of wealthy nations, it's now a global issue with increasing rates in developing countries. 
Socioeconomic Factors: Obesity is also influenced by factors beyond income, such as education, race, and ethnicity, with disparities existing between different groups. 
Reverse Causality: Some research also suggests that obesity may lead to reduced income, creating a cycle of health and economic hardship. 
In summary, while anyone can be obese, the condition is more prevalent among those with lower socioeconomic status and those experiencing food insecurity. 
Note: This information is based on the search results provided. It's always recommended to consult with a healthcare professional for personalized advice and information.

Now, a recent study published by Martin and Friends actually took a look to see what is happening inside of the brains of people that are taking Zepbound and how does that differ from people that aren't taking Zepbound. And what they did is they rounded up about 114 individuals that had obesity or overweight. And they split them into one of three groups. One group got Zepbound at 10 milligrams once a week. Another group got Saxenda, the once daily injection. And the final group was given a placebo. And the authors didn't just track weight loss, they tracked hunger, cravings, impulsivity, and they did fMRI scans of their brains. Now, what the authors found after 3 weeks is that individuals that were taking Zepbound were consuming calories less than the placebo group. That's like skipping a Big Mac meal every single day without even knowing it. And by six weeks, the individuals on Zepbound lost 7 kilos or about 15 pounds. The people on Saxenda lost about 3.7 kilos and those in the placebo group lost about 0.6 kilos. Now, to get a really good idea of this food noise aspect of things and what role it was playing, the authors used a bunch of validated scales in order to determine what was happening, what were people's cravings, impulsive behaviors, restrictive behaviors, and that sort of thing. what was going on for each individual that parttook in the study. And here's a gist of the results for those that were on Zepbound. The people that were taking Zepbound weren't trying extra hard to lose more weight. They just weren't as hungry. They weren't obsessing over food like everybody else. Their cravings for sweets and fast food decreased dramatically. And they had less impulsivity with food and less of an emotional pull to eat. And so it was clear the zapbound wasn't working just through willpower and helping them in that respect. It was actually making their brain quieter and helping them to change their behaviors around food. Now things got really cool with the fMRI scans. And here you can see how the brain scans show that between baseline and week three, the differences in the areas that light up for those that are in the placebo, the saxenda group, and the zepbound group. And these fMRI scans didn't just show vague brain changes or anything like that. They highlighted significant changes in the areas where food obsession lives. And Zepbound did a really good job of dimming those areas down. And I know most of you aren't going to be brain surgeons. So here's a quick and dirty as to what exactly the authors found. There was a decrease in signal in the orbital frontal cortex. This is kind of like your brain's pleasure calculator. That's going to light up when you see things like fries and cake. Zepbound dial down the noise there so food no longer becomes the headliner. Zepbound also cause things to calm down in the singulate gyrus. This is the I know I shouldn't but I did zone which helps to calm down impulses and helps with less white knuckling past the snack drawer. We also saw signals decrease in the medial prefrontal cortex which is kind of like decision central and zepbound stops making every snack choice become a life or death situation. And the final area that we saw dim down was the hippocampus. Now your hippocampus is kind of like the thermostat of your body. It wants to keep everything in balance and so it doesn't want anything to get too out of hand especially when it comes to us getting too hungry or not having enough nutrients or us losing fat. But our emotional memory with food is also stored here. And zepbound was found to soften or to decrease the signaling that was coming out of there which is going to help us to decrease our hunger and cravings and such like that especially for those nostalgia type foods that we have a lot of good memories around. So, we don't know if those brain changes stuck around longterm or after the medication had cleared the system. But what we do know is that when people stop these medications, a vast majority of them get food noise coming back and they ultimately regain a lot of the weight that they lost, if not then some. And so, it's very likely that once the drug is cleared out of the system, your brain simply reverts back to the old patterns that were there before. And why? Well, this isn't permanent neuroplasticity. It's a pharmacological influence. And the rewiring and changes that we see in the brain, well, they only work when the medication is present. You pull the plug and your brain goes right back to its cupcake obsessed ways. - Zepbound: A Complete Guide To Achieving Your Weight Loss Goals: Kind: captions Language: en-US Today, we're delving deep into&nbsp; Zepbound, the latest FDA-approved&nbsp;&nbsp; marvel in weight-loss treatment options.&nbsp; If you're seeking a breakthrough in your&nbsp;&nbsp; weight management journey, you'll want&nbsp; to stick around for this. Don't forget,&nbsp;&nbsp; if you're using Zepbound or other weight-loss&nbsp; injections like Wegovy or Saxenda,&nbsp;&nbsp; keeping them at the right temperature is key.&nbsp; The 4AllFamily medical-grade travel coolers&nbsp;&nbsp; are perfect for this. To get your cooler,&nbsp; you can visit the link below this video.&nbsp; Ready to dive into the&nbsp; world of Zepbound? Let’s go!&nbsp; WHAT IS ZEPBOUND? "Zepbound is more than just a&nbsp;&nbsp; new drug; it's a landmark in obesity treatment.&nbsp; Approved just recently, on November 8, 2023,&nbsp;&nbsp; Zepbound is a brand name for Tirzepatide.&nbsp; But why is it making such a buzz only a&nbsp;&nbsp; month after its approval? What makes it stand&nbsp; out in the crowd of weight loss treatments?&nbsp; Well, basically, it's the first dual-action drug&nbsp; targeting both GIP and GLP-1 receptors, making it&nbsp;&nbsp; distinct from other weight-loss injections like&nbsp; Wegovy or Saxenda. This dual-action approach&nbsp;&nbsp; means Zepbound not only curbs your appetite but&nbsp; also enhances your body'’s metabolic function.&nbsp; HOW DOES IT WORK? Now, let’s try to put it in simpler words.&nbsp; Tirzepatide, the active ingredient in Zepbound&nbsp; works by mimicking the actions of both GIP and&nbsp;&nbsp; GLP-1, two hormones naturally involved in our&nbsp; metabolism. Yes, I know, that doesn’t sound&nbsp;&nbsp; English at all... but don’t worry, I’ll explain: · GIP stands for glucose-dependent insulinotropic&nbsp;&nbsp; polypeptide. This hormone enhances insulin&nbsp; secretion and reduces glucagon release,&nbsp;&nbsp; keeping our blood sugar levels in check, and&nbsp; improving insulin sensitivity, leading to reduced&nbsp;&nbsp; fat storage and a more efficient metabolism. · GLP-1 stands for glucagon-like peptide-1.&nbsp;&nbsp; This hormone decreases appetite&nbsp; and, consequently, food intake.&nbsp; So, think of Zepbound as a master of disguise,&nbsp; tricking your body into thinking it has more&nbsp;&nbsp; of these hormones than it actually&nbsp; does. This means you feel less hungry,&nbsp;&nbsp; and your body gets better at handling the food&nbsp; you’re ingesting – a double win for weight loss!&nbsp; This dual-action mechanism is a key&nbsp; distinguishing feature of Zepbound,&nbsp;&nbsp; setting it apart from other weight-loss drugs that&nbsp; may target only one of these two hormones and only&nbsp;&nbsp; a single aspect of metabolic regulation.” UNPACKING ZEPBOUND VS. MOUNJARO&nbsp; "There's been a lot of buzz about Mounjaro as&nbsp; well over the last year. And actually, Mounjaro&nbsp;&nbsp; contains the same drug as Zepbound, Tirzepatide,&nbsp; and is made by the same manufacturer, Eli Lilly.&nbsp;&nbsp; So, what’s the difference? Why do we have two&nbsp; identical drugs with two different names then?&nbsp; Well, it’s all about clinical trials and&nbsp; FDA-approvals. Mounjaro has been tested and&nbsp;&nbsp; approved for Type 2 diabetes, while Zepbound&nbsp; is for weight loss. But this dual usage of&nbsp;&nbsp; Tirzepatide showcases its potential in treating&nbsp; different conditions, emphasizing the need for&nbsp;&nbsp; professional medical advice in their use." ELIGIBILITY FOR ZEPBOUND&nbsp; "It’s important to understand that Zepbound&nbsp; is not a one-stop solution for everyone. It's&nbsp;&nbsp; a powerful medication for adults struggling with&nbsp; obesity or overweight and related health issues,&nbsp;&nbsp; like diabetes or hypertension for example. Using Zepbound requires careful medical&nbsp;&nbsp; supervision, making it essential that it's&nbsp; prescribed and monitored by healthcare&nbsp;&nbsp; professionals. It's specifically designed for&nbsp; those who need medical intervention to manage&nbsp;&nbsp; their weight and should not be used for aesthetic&nbsp; weight loss. Additionally, Zepbound hasn’t been&nbsp;&nbsp; approved for children. HOW TO USE ZEPBOUND?&nbsp; "Using Zepbound involves a weekly subcutaneous&nbsp; injection. Available in various dosages,&nbsp;&nbsp; from 2.5 mg to 15 mg, your doctor&nbsp; will determine the best dose for you.&nbsp; Injections can be a bit overwhelming at first,&nbsp; but don’t worry. Zepbound comes in a prefilled,&nbsp;&nbsp; single-dose, very easy to use injector pen.&nbsp; We’ve got a whole video on that, showing you&nbsp;&nbsp; how to use your injector pen safely! ZEPBOUND'S CLINICAL TRIUMPHS&nbsp; "In clinical trials, Zepbound has shown remarkable&nbsp; efficacy, hence the big buzz it’s making today.&nbsp;&nbsp; It's not just about short-term weight loss;&nbsp; it’s about maintaining that loss sustainably.&nbsp; In different studies, participants on higher&nbsp; doses of Zepbound experienced significant and&nbsp;&nbsp; sustained weight reduction, up to 18% of&nbsp; body weight over 72 weeks of treatment,&nbsp;&nbsp; proving its effectiveness as a long-term solution.&nbsp; But, remember, Zepbound works best alongside a&nbsp;&nbsp; balanced diet and regular exercise regime! SIDE EFFECTS: WHAT TO EXPECT&nbsp; "Unfortunately, like all medications,&nbsp; Zepbound comes with its own potential&nbsp;&nbsp; side effects too. Most commonly, you might&nbsp; encounter gastrointestinal discomfort,&nbsp;&nbsp; injection site reactions, fatigue, or headaches.&nbsp; Besides, serious side effects, like hypoglycemia,&nbsp;&nbsp; kidney failure, gallbladder problems, or&nbsp; pancreatitis, are rare but can happen. Also,&nbsp;&nbsp; Zepbound may be contraindicated for some&nbsp; patients, including those with a history of&nbsp;&nbsp; Medullary Thyroid Cancer and pregnant women. For a list of all possible side effects and&nbsp;&nbsp; contraindications of Zepbound, you should refer&nbsp; to the manufacturer’s website or ask your doctor.&nbsp;&nbsp; And like with any treatment, the benefits&nbsp; vs. risks ratio should always be evaluated&nbsp;&nbsp; with the help of your doctor.” HOW MUCH DOES ZEPBOUND COST?&nbsp; "Now, let's address the elephant in the room –&nbsp; ‘’The Cost’’. So, why do food choices matter on GLP-1? So, GLP-1 slows digestion and makes you eat less. This means that you have a lot less room to meet all of your body's nutrition needs. you can't eat quite as much. These are all because you are not eating correctly and not fueling your body properly. Okay? Okay. So, it is not the medication that does any of those things. - 10 Things No One Tells You About Ozempic and Zepbound: Kind: captions Language: en Semaglutide is one of the most talked about medications in the world right now. You hear about it on social media and you see headlines calling it either a miracle drug or something dangerous that no one should touch. But what almost no one talks about is how semlutide actually feels in real life and why so many people either struggle on it or quit too early. Today I want to walk you through the things people don't find out until they're already on it. Sometimes months in when confusion and frustration have already set in. I'm Dr. Kevin Joseph. I'm a board-certified internal medicine physician and I've lost over 150 pounds using GLP-1 medications and peptide therapy. I also work with patients every day who are navigating these medications. And my goal here is not to scare you, but to give you clarity because once you understand what semiglutide is actually doing, everything about the experience starts to make sense. The first thing that most people misunderstand is where semiglutide actually works. People assume this is a stomach medication that it just slows digestion and makes you feel full. But the truth is, semiglutide is also a brain medication. It acts on GLP-1 receptors in areas of the brain responsible for appetite regulation, reward signaling, and impulse control. That constant background noise around food, the mental chatter that tells you to snack even when you're not physically hungry, starts to quiet down. Many people describe this as the first time in their life that they've actually finally able to hear their own hunger cues instead of being driven by them. This is why semiglutide can feel life-changing for people who have struggled with food obsession for decades. This was the biggest game changer for me. I was a huge emotional eater and my hunger and blood sugar dysregulation controlled every aspect of my life. I would get angry if I was hungry and didn't know what I was eating next. I would literally think about my next meal constantly to the point where it interfered with everyday life. Zi once quieted all of this and finally let me be in control of not just my stomach but my brain. What surprises people next is that hunger doesn't disappear the way they expected. You don't stop getting hungry. What changes is the quality of hunger. Before semlutide, hunger often feels emotional, urgent, and overwhelming. Like I just talked about on semaglutide, hunger becomes calmer and more physical. You notice your body asking for food rather than your brain demanding it. This is why people often say they forget to eat or suddenly realize they're full halfway through a meal. That doesn't mean the medication is suppressing your body in an unhealthy way. It means your satiety signals are finally being heard. The brain stomach connection is being connected. Another thing people aren't prepared for is how nonlinear weight loss becomes. Initially, when you first start GLP-1 medications, the weight melts off and we quickly become conditioned to expect predictable weekly drops on the scale. However, Ompic and Zepbound don't work like that. There are weeks where nothing changes, weeks where the scale goes up sometimes, and weeks where it suddenly drops several pounds at once. This isn't failure or resistance. And I get this question at least once a day from either people online or my own patients because maybe the scale hasn't budged in, you know, 2 to 3 weeks. So, everyone immediately thinks the medication stopped working or they are doing something wrong. But that's not it at all. This isn't failure. It's normal human physiology. As insulin sensitivity improves and inflammation decreases, your body shifts fluids, glycogen, and fat in ways that don't always show up immediately on the scale. People who don't understand this panic and increase their dose too quickly, which actually makes things worse, leading to fatigue and other unwanted side effects. I promise you that even if the scale isn't moving every week, your body composition is improving or your metabolic health is improving just as much. So just stay the course. Side effects are another area where there is a lot of misinformation. Many people are told that nausea, fatigue, and digestive issues are inevitable and something you just have to push through. In reality, most side effects are dose dependent. They tend to happen when the dose is increased faster than the body can adapt. Some glutide and tepatide have long half- lives, which means it accumulates over time. A dose that felt fine for two weeks can suddenly feel overwhelming in weeks three and four. This doesn't mean that the medication is wrong for you. It usually means that the dose is too high for your current physiology, which is why I always recommend my patients start with micro doing and work their way up slowly if needed. One of the most important things that no one explains well is the risk of muscle loss. Semaglutide does not selectively burn fat. It reduces overall intake. And without intentional protein intake and resistance training, your body will break down muscle along with fat. However, this is not exclusive to GOP ones. The same findings were seen in people post beriatric surgery who also severely restricted calories without an emphasis on resistance training. But remember, muscle is not just cosmetic. It is metabolically active tissue that protects insulin sensitivity, supports longevity, and keeps your metabolism roaring even when you aren't doing anything. When people lose weight quickly without protecting muscle, they often feel weak, tired, and discouraged. So please remember to make protein intake and resistance training a core part of your weight loss plan. Constipation is another issue people are embarrassed to talk about but it's extremely common. Semaglutide and transepite slow down gastric emptying and intestinal mobility. That's part of how it helps with appetite control. But slower digestion means you have to be proactive about hydration, electrolytes, and fiber timing to minimize constipation. Constipation isn't a sign that something is wrong, but a signal that your digestive system needs support while adapts to a new pace. Another thing that often catches people off guard is how their taste preferences change. Food that often felt irresistible may suddenly feel heavy or unappealing. Sugary or greasy foods may lose their reward value. This can feel strange, even emotional, because food is tied to comfort and identity for so many people, myself included. But this shift is actually a sign that the medication is altering dopamine signaling around food, not just appetite. Many people eventually realize they're eating for nourishment instead of emotional regulation for the first time in their lives. This was the biggest game changanger for me and one of the main reasons I will never come off of this medication. I will never let food control my life again. Another thing people don't expect is how much inflammation improves on semlutide independent of weight loss. I mentioned before they work on the brain, but GLP-1 receptors are also abundant on many different types of cells, especially those that play a huge role in inflammation. My patients often report less joint pain, improved blood pressures, better blood sugar control, and more stable energy throughout the day. This is because GOP1 signaling affects inflammation, insulin resistance, and metabolic stress at a cellular level. Weight loss is just the most visible effect, but it's not the only one. What no one prepares you for is how different stopping semoglutad feels compared to starting it. When the medication wears off, hunger signaling returns. But now the difference is that you're more aware of it because you've experienced life without constant food noise. This can feel intense if you don't have a plan. It doesn't mean you've become dependent or that your body is broken. It means your physiology is returning to baseline. And this is where habits, protein intake, and structured routines become essential. So, make sure while you are on your GLP-1 journey that if you plan on coming off of this medication in the future, you create healthy eating habits and sustainable workout routines to help keep your health on track. - Wegovy vs. Zepbound: The Slimming Drug Showdown: Kind: captions Language: en Wegovy and Zepbound are both FDA approved weight&nbsp; loss medications that belong to a drug class&nbsp;&nbsp; called gp1 Agonist which were originally designed&nbsp; to treat type 2 diabetes even though these meds&nbsp;&nbsp; come from the same drug family they have their&nbsp; differences that are unique and they're worth&nbsp;&nbsp; knowing especially if you're considering starting&nbsp; one of these agents my name is Dominique and I'm&nbsp;&nbsp; a clinical pharmacist and I'm here to teach you&nbsp; how these agents work and break down what makes&nbsp;&nbsp; these weight loss options different so you can&nbsp; make better informed decisions regarding your&nbsp;&nbsp; healthcare as well as your pockets. Wegovy contains the same ingredient as Ozempic&nbsp; which is semaglutide. That's right same ingredient&nbsp;&nbsp; and naturally your next question would be what&nbsp; makes Wegovy different than Ozempic and the short&nbsp;&nbsp; answer is branding, marketing, big pharma making&nbsp; that coin. Ozempic carries the FDA indication for&nbsp;&nbsp; type 2 diabetes and after they saw substantial&nbsp; weight loss as a side effect of the medication&nbsp;&nbsp; Ozempic started becoming over and inappropriate&nbsp; prescribed for weight loss now this presented an&nbsp;&nbsp; opportunity for manufacturers to make some&nbsp; money alas ozempic was rebranded to Wegovy&nbsp;&nbsp; which shortly was approved by the FDA to be used&nbsp; for weight loss alone with or without diabetes&nbsp;&nbsp; they have the same ingredients and their doses&nbsp; differ less than 0.5 mg from each other but yet&nbsp;&nbsp; they have different brand names same thing goes&nbsp; for Zepbound, Z bound contains the same active&nbsp;&nbsp; ingredient in Mounjaro which is tirzepatide&nbsp; they have the same ingredient in and their&nbsp;&nbsp; doses are actually the same but yet they have&nbsp; different brand names essentially what I'm saying&nbsp;&nbsp; is Wegovy is the weight loss brand diversion of&nbsp; Ozempic and Zepbound is the weight loss branded&nbsp;&nbsp; version of mounjaro now let's get into how&nbsp; Wegovy and Zepbound are similar and different. Both Zepbound and Wegovy are once weekly&nbsp; injections and have substantial weight&nbsp;&nbsp; loss effects but then there are some areas that&nbsp; they differ well actually a lot of ways like how&nbsp;&nbsp; work how much they cost available formulations&nbsp; heart benefit and one is a bit more effective or&nbsp;&nbsp; a lot more effective at Weight Loss than the&nbsp; other don't worry I'm not going to leave you&nbsp;&nbsp; hanging I'm actually going to walk you through&nbsp; each of these both of these drugs belong to glp&nbsp;&nbsp; Agonist drug class glp1 glucagon like peptide 1&nbsp; is a hormone released from the intestines when&nbsp;&nbsp; food is ingested and once glp1 is released it&nbsp; is able to bind to specific receptors found in&nbsp;&nbsp; various tissues throughout the body including our&nbsp; gastric cells cells in the pancreas which is the&nbsp;&nbsp; organ that releases insulin and the hypothalamus&nbsp; which is the area of the brain that regulates&nbsp;&nbsp; hunger and satiety so that feeling of fullness&nbsp; when glp1 binds to these receptors it number one&nbsp;&nbsp; helps the pancreas produce more insulin on its&nbsp; own number two Works to prevent the liver from&nbsp;&nbsp; releasing additional blood sugar that's already&nbsp; stored in the body and number three it's slowing&nbsp;&nbsp; down the rate of food leaving your stomach which&nbsp; we call delayed gastric emptying which means more&nbsp;&nbsp; time to break down carbohydrates so we're getting&nbsp; less spikes of blood sugar following our meals so&nbsp;&nbsp; not only does this reduce the sugar spikes but it&nbsp; also is making you feel Fuller faster so you're&nbsp;&nbsp; not eating as much per meal and here's where&nbsp; weight loss also comes in which is the fourth&nbsp;&nbsp; benefit glp1 also has activity in the brain that&nbsp; suppresses the appetite meaning you're eating&nbsp;&nbsp; less and you're feeling Fuller faster in fact&nbsp; this appetite suppression can be so significant&nbsp;&nbsp; I've seen patients tell me that they can barely&nbsp; get one meal in a day both Wegovy and Zepbound&nbsp;&nbsp; have glp1 Agonist effect but Zepbound has an&nbsp; additional hormone called Gip Gip stands for&nbsp;&nbsp; gastric inhibitory peptide in other translations&nbsp; but it's another hormone that has similar effects&nbsp;&nbsp; of gp1 and how it facilitates insulin production&nbsp; but one of the things that makes it different&nbsp;&nbsp; is that it actually stimulates the release of&nbsp; stored blood sugar in the event that someone's&nbsp;&nbsp; blood sugar drops too low essentially&nbsp; the addition of GIP to glp1 in Zepbound&nbsp;&nbsp; enhances the weight loss effect but provides a&nbsp; buffer to prevent blood sugar from falling too Now if you're a data nerd like me I included the&nbsp; data so let's talk about it in the clinical trials&nbsp;&nbsp; both Zepbound and Wegovy were tested in adult&nbsp; patients without diabetes who were classified&nbsp;&nbsp; as obese and they had an average participant&nbsp; weighing around 226 to 235lb When comparing the&nbsp;&nbsp; group that didn't take Wegovy to the group that&nbsp; did the Wegovy group showed an average 35 weight&nbsp;&nbsp; loss at the end of a 2-year study compared to a&nbsp; six pound weight loss in the group also known the&nbsp;&nbsp; placebo group of the adults that were just on&nbsp; a reduced caloric diet and an exercise regimen&nbsp;&nbsp; alone and that sounds substantial right well&nbsp; Zepbound studies showed that up to 48 pounds&nbsp;&nbsp; of weight loss was achieved on the max dosing&nbsp; compared to 7lbs for Placebo over the course of&nbsp;&nbsp; 17 months and we don't have a head head comparison&nbsp; study yet of Wegovy versus Zepbound but from these&nbsp;&nbsp; trials we can see that zepbound is leading the way&nbsp; in the weight loss race by approximately 13 lbs. Wegovy, also known as semaglutide, also&nbsp; known as Ozempic has been shown to have&nbsp;&nbsp; cardiovascular protection in folks&nbsp; with the history of heart attack or&nbsp;&nbsp; stroke however Zepbound which is ter&nbsp; eptide does not have this benefit. Wegovy comes in a pre-filled auto injector&nbsp; that is approximately $45 per dose and since&nbsp;&nbsp; these since these medications are dosed once a&nbsp; week a month supply would be approximately to&nbsp;&nbsp; $1,600 Z bound on the other hand has options it&nbsp; originally hit the market as a pre-filled auto&nbsp;&nbsp; injector as well for roughly $320 a dose which is&nbsp; approximately 1,300 a month however in an attempt&nbsp;&nbsp; to make more people buy the medication I mean to&nbsp; make the medication more affordable they recently&nbsp;&nbsp; announced that patients can get get zepbound for&nbsp; 40% of the original price if they want to drop&nbsp;&nbsp; their own dosing from a vile like the good old&nbsp; days when insulin first came on board bringing&nbsp;&nbsp; that monthly cost down to $480 still expensive&nbsp; but not as expensive while I'm here I feel&nbsp;&nbsp; the need to share a warning not many people are&nbsp; talking about if you or if you know of a sexually&nbsp;&nbsp; active woman of childbearing age who's on oral&nbsp; contraception and they're considering Zepbound&nbsp;&nbsp; or Mounjaro I need you to listen closely come on&nbsp; if you are not planning on reproducing the Earth&nbsp;&nbsp; anytime soon you may want to double up on your&nbsp; contraception in the initial weeks of starting&nbsp;&nbsp; Zepbound or Mounjaro the reason being is the&nbsp; active ingredient to tirzepatide has a special&nbsp;&nbsp; warning recommending patients who are using oral&nbsp; hormonal contraceptives that they should switch to&nbsp;&nbsp; either a non-oral contraceptive method or they&nbsp; should add a barrier method of contraception&nbsp;&nbsp; for 4 weeks after tirzepatide is started and&nbsp; for 4 weeks whenever the dose is increased.   20. @metteh.j.4836: The REAL reason for the different names is mostly linked to insurance, national drug subsidizing and the need to distinguish between treatment of diabetes and obesity. Also FDA etc. local instances approval of the drug used for weight loss alone.
¤ Ozempic/Zepbound can/should then be subscribed for diabetes only (or certain BMI etc. according to local rules), probably with some kind of insurance or subsidizing scheme fitting the diagnosis.
¤ Wegovy/Mounjaro can be subscribed for weigthloss alone, with either a different or (more common) no reimbursement.


The distinguishment also helps ensure enough products for the diabetics, for whom a medication scarcity can have big health impacts. When the weight loss side effect was discovered, demand exploded more than could be met by production supply - opening the market TEMPORARILY for compounders in the US. Some of these compounders now uses manipulated dosages as a loophole to continue selling their copied products, which in turn undermine the business model and hence research performed in this area.

Not all diabetics can be subscribe Ozempic/Zepbound, if their older and cheaper treatment works for them. I'm ofc. speaking generally and with a global perspective here, well knowing that the rules differ and change.

FYI, I'm from Denmark, heart- and homeland of Novo Nordisk, who produces Victoza, Saxenda, Ozempic, Wegovy and the future pill CagriSema. In US the consumers price is around 6x-7x of ex. Denmark, NO subsidizing. It is due to a faulty healthcare system that drives prices up so several middlemen and insurance companies can profit, on the expense of the consumer. Don't blame Novo, blame your government!
